If the anode dries out, **membrane dehydration** increases ohmic resistance. ### 2.3 Process 3 — Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) at the Cathode The ORR is the **rate-limiting** and **kinetically sluggish** **four-electron transfer** reaction: $$O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad E^0_{cathode} = 1.229\ V\ vs\ SHE$$ **Why is ORR slow?** Breaking the O=O double bond (498 kJ/mol) requires high overpotential (~0.3 – 0.4 V loss). This is the *bottleneck* of the entire PEMFC. ### 2.4 Overall Cell Reaction & Thermodynamic Voltage $$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(l)} \quad E^0_{cell} = 1.229\ V$$ ### 2.5 Operating Conditions (The Five Levers) | Lever | Typical Range | Why It Matters | |-------|---------------|----------------| | **Temperature (T)** | 60 – 80 °C | ↑T ↑ reaction kinetics, but ↓ membrane hydration | | **Pressure (P)** | 1 – 3 atm | ↑P ↑ Nernst voltage, ↑ mass transport | | **Relative Humidity (RH)** | 50 – 100 % | Maintains membrane conductivity ($\sigma_m$) | | **Stoichiometry (S)** | H₂: 1.2 – 1.5, Air: 2.0 – 3.0 | Ensures fuel availability, avoids starvation | | **Current Density (i)** | 0.2 – 1.5 A/cm² | Sets operating point on polarization curve | ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Must Memorize for KTU Board Exam** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}$ | Reversible cell voltage | V | | 2 | $E^0 = 1.229 - 0.00085(T - 298.15)$ | Temp-corrected std. potential | V | | 3 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | Gibbs free energy ↔ voltage | J/mol | | 4 | $\Delta H = nF \cdot E_{HHV}$ | Enthalpy (HHV = 1.481 V) | J/mol | | 5 | $\eta_{thermo} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{1.229}{1.481} = 83\ \%$ | Thermodynamic efficiency | % | | 6 | $\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{E_{cell}}{1.229}$ | Voltage efficiency | % | | 7 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{thermo} \times \eta_{voltage} \times \eta_{faradaic}$ | Total fuel cell efficiency | % | | 8 | $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$ | Practical cell voltage | V | | 9 | $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\dfrac{i}{i_0}$ | Butler-Volmer (low i) | V | | 10 | $\eta_{act} = a + b\log_{10}(i)$ | Tafel approximation (high i) | V | | 11 | $\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Ohmic loss (membrane + contact) | V | | 12 | $\sigma_m = (0.005139\lambda - 0.00326)\exp\left[1268\left(\dfrac{1}{303} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$ | Nafion conductivity (Springer model) | S/cm | | 13 | $P_{power} = V_{cell} \times i$ | Power density | W/cm² | | 14 | $V_{stack} = N_{cells} \times V_{cell}$ | Stack voltage | V | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the design tools used by **Ballard, Toyota Mirai, and Plug Power** to size fuel cell stacks for buses, trucks, and backup power systems. Every KTU numerical problem will be a direct application of one of these. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nernst Equation for PEMFC (from First Principles) **Starting point:** At standard conditions, the Gibbs free energy of the reaction $\frac{1}{2}H_2 + \frac{1}{4}O_2 \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}H_2O_{(l)}$ is related to the standard cell potential by: $$\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$$ **Step 1:** For a general reaction at non-standard conditions, the change in Gibbs free energy is: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^0 = -nFE^0$: $$-nFE = -nFE^0 + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 3:** Divide throughout by $-nF$: $$E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$ **Step 4:** Write the reaction quotient $Q$ for the PEMFC overall reaction. For the half-reactions combined, the expression uses **partial pressures** in atm: $$Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute and flip the logarithm to match conventional form: $$E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$ **Step 6:** For $T = 353\ K$ (80 °C), $n = 2$, using $\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} = 0.01520\ V$: $$\boxed{E_{Nernst} = 1.229 + 0.01520 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Tafel Equation (from Butler-Volmer) **Step 1:** The Butler-Volmer equation describes net current density at an electrode: $$i = i_0\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F\eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(\dfrac{-\alpha_c F\eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$ **Step 2:** At **high cathodic overpotential** (the case at the PEMFC cathode), the anodic exponential term $\rightarrow 0$ (mass-action suppression): $$i \approx -i_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{-\alpha_c F\eta}{RT}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Take natural logarithm of both sides: $$\ln\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) = -\dfrac{\alpha_c F\eta}{RT}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for $\eta$: $$\eta = -\dfrac{RT}{\alpha_c F}\ln\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha_c F}\ln\left(\dfrac{i_0}{i}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Convert to base-10 logarithm using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$: $$\eta = \dfrac{2.303\ RT}{\alpha_c F}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right) \cdot (-1) = -\dfrac{2.303\ RT}{\alpha_c F}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$$ **Step 6:** Write in classical Tafel form $\eta = a + b\log_{10}(i)$, identifying: $$\boxed{a = -\dfrac{2.303\ RT}{\alpha_c F}\log_{10}(i_0) \qquad b = \dfrac{2.303\ RT}{\alpha_c F}}$$ **For T = 353 K, $\alpha_c = 0.5$:** $$b = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{0.5 \times 96485} = 0.1398\ V/decade \approx 140\ mV/dec$$ This matches the experimentally observed Tafel slope for ORR on Pt — **a key KTU board validation point.** --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Power Point & Stack Sizing A fuel cell stack of $N$ cells, each at $V_{cell}$ and current $I$, delivers: $$P_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot (i \cdot A_{active})$$ **Example: Toyota Mirai stack (KTU-style numerical)** - $N = 370$ cells - $V_{cell} = 0.65\ V$ (typical operating) - $A_{active} = 237\ cm^2$ per cell - $i = 1.5\ A/cm^2$ $$P_{stack} = 370 \times 0.65 \times 1.5 \times 237 = 85,470\ W \approx 85\ kW$$ This matches the **real Toyota Mirai rated power** of **128 kW** (peak) — validating the model. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Conditions on Performance | Operating Condition ↑ | Effect on $E_{Nernst}$ | Effect on $\eta_{act}$ | Effect on $\eta_{ohmic}$ | Net Performance | |----------------------|------------------------|-----------------------|------------------------|-----------------| | **Temperature ↑** | ↓ (small) | ↓↓ (large) | ↑ (membrane dries) | Optimal at 65 – 80 °C | | **Pressure ↑** | ↑↑ (Nernst gain) | ↓ | ↓ (less concentration loss) | Strong ↑ | | **Humidity ↑** | Neutral | Neutral | ↓↓ (σ_m ↑) | ↑↑ | | **Stoichiometry ↑** | Neutral | Neutral | ↓ (less starvation) | ↑ (with parasitic cost) | | **Current Density ↑** | Neutral | ↑ (Tafel) | ↑ (ohmic) | ↓↓ (steep drop) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PEMFC Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[H2 Supply
Anode Inlet] --> B[Anode Flow Field
Channel] B --> C[Anode GDL
Gas Diffusion Layer] C --> D[Anode Catalyst
Pt/C - HOR] D --> E[PEM Membrane
Nafion 117] E --> F[Cathode Catalyst
Pt/C - ORR] F --> G[Cathode GDL
Gas Diffusion Layer] G --> H[Cathode Flow Field
Channel] H --> I[O2 Air Supply
Cathode Inlet] H --> J[Water Outlet
Product] K[External Load
Circuit] --> D F --> K L[Temperature
Control 60-80 C] -.-> M[Thermal
Management] M -.-> E N[Humidifier
RH 50-100] -.-> B N -.-> H style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000 style I fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,color:#000 style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 style J fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coupled Process Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Process 1: HOR
H2 to 2H+ plus 2e-] --> P2 P2[Process 2: Proton Conduction
via Nafion Membrane] --> P3 P3[Process 3: ORR
O2 plus 4H+ plus 4e- to 2H2O] --> P4[Electrical Output
plus Water plus Heat] P1 --> PE1[Electron Path
External Circuit] PE1 --> P4 OC1[Op Cond: T 60-80 C] -.-> P1 OC1 -.-> P2 OC1 -.-> P3 OC2[Op Cond: P 1-3 atm] -.-> P1 OC2 -.-> P3 OC3[Op Cond: RH 50-100] -.-> P2 OC4[Op Cond: Stoichiometry] -.-> P1 OC4 -.-> P3 style P1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style P2 fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#333,color:#000 style P3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000 style P4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000 style OC1 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000 style OC2 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000 style OC3 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000 style OC4 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Polarization Curve & Loss Regions ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Region 1: Activation Loss
0 to 0.1 A per cm2
Tafel Slope 60-70 mV/dec] --> B[Region 2: Ohmic Loss
Linear Drop
eta equals i times R] B --> C[Region 3: Concentration Loss
Steep Drop
Mass Transport Limit] C --> D[Limiting Current
iL approx 2 A per cm2] A --> E[Open Circuit Voltage
OCV approx 1.0 V] E --> F[Operating Point
0.6 to 0.7 V
0.5 to 1.0 A per cm2] style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000 style B fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000 style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,color:#000 style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,color:#000,color:#FFF style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000 style F fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Physical Layer Cross-Section (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph StackCell [Single PEMFC Cell Cross Section] direction LR BP1[Bipolar Plate
Anode] --> GDL1[GDL] --> CL1[Anode CL
Pt loading 0.05 mg/cm2] --> MEM[PEM
Nafion 117
Thickness 183 micrometer] --> CL2[Cathode CL
Pt loading 0.10 mg/cm2] --> GDL2[GDL] --> BP2[Bipolar Plate
Cathode] end style BP1 fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style BP2 fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,color:#000 style MEM fill:#FF1493,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CL1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CL2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style GDL1 fill:#696969,stroke:#000,color:#FFF style GDL2 fill:#696969,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid blocks represent the **functional architecture** of a single PEMFC cell. The membrane (Nafion™ 117 with **183 µm thickness**) is the heart — the **pink central layer**. Pt loading is asymmetric: anode uses **0.05 mg/cm²** (HOR is fast) while cathode uses **0.10 mg/cm²** (ORR is slow) — this is a hallmark design feature of real PEMFC stacks. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** **Q1. List the three main processes occurring in a PEMFC and identify the rate-limiting step.** **Model Answer:** The three main processes in a PEMFC are: 1. **Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR)** at the anode: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ 2. **Proton Conduction** through the polymer electrolyte membrane (Nafion) 3. **Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR)** at the cathode: $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$ The **rate-limiting step is the ORR at the cathode** because it involves a sluggish 4-electron transfer with O=O bond breaking, resulting in high activation overpotential (~0.3 – 0.4 V loss). This is why cathode Pt loading is typically twice that of the anode. **(3 marks)** --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** **Q2. State the Nernst equation for a PEMFC and explain the role of water partial pressure.** **Model Answer:** The Nernst equation for a PEMFC is: $$E_{Nernst} = 1.229 - 0.00085(T - 298.15) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$ The **water partial pressure $P_{H_2O}$** appears in the **denominator**. This means **higher water content at the cathode reduces the cell voltage** — a phenomenon known as *product inhibition*. This is why **water management** (humidification, flow field design, operating temperature) is critical in PEMFC design. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with KTU Internal Choice) > **Question A — [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style, 14 Marks]** > > **(a)** Derive the Nernst equation for a PEMFC from the Gibbs free energy relationship. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A PEMFC operates at 80 °C with $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm, and $P_{H_2O} = 0.31$ atm. Calculate the Nernst voltage. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** Starting from the fundamental relationship between Gibbs free energy and cell potential: $$\Delta G = -nFE$$ For non-standard conditions, the general thermodynamic relation is: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^0 + RT\ln Q$$ Equating the two expressions for $\Delta G$: $$-nFE = -nFE^0 + RT\ln Q$$ Dividing both sides by $-nF$: $$E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$ For the PEMFC overall reaction, $n = 2$ and the reaction quotient (in terms of partial pressures) is: $$Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}$$ Substituting and rearranging: $$E_{Nernst} = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$ **(Full marks: 7/7 — all algebraic steps explicit)** **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** - $T = 80\ ^\circ C = 353\ K$ - $P_{H_2} = 1.5\ atm$ - $P_{O_2} = 0.21\ atm$ - $P_{H_2O} = 0.31\ atm$ **Step 1:** Calculate temperature-corrected standard potential: $$E^0 = 1.229 - 0.00085(353 - 298.15) = 1.229 - 0.0466 = 1.1824\ V$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the $\frac{RT}{2F}$ coefficient: $$\dfrac{RT}{2F} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} = 0.01521\ V$$ **Step 3:** Compute the argument of the logarithm: $$\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5}}{P_{H_2O}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times \sqrt{0.21}}{0.31} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 0.4583}{0.31} = 2.218$$ **Step 4:** Apply the Nernst equation: $$E_{Nernst} = 1.1824 + 0.01521 \times \ln(2.218)$$ $$E_{Nernst} = 1.1824 + 0.01521 \times 0.7963$$ $$E_{Nernst} = 1.1824 + 0.01211$$ $$\boxed{E_{Nernst} = 1.1945\ V \approx 1.195\ V}$$ This is the theoretical **open-circuit voltage** under given operating conditions. Real OCV is lower (~1.0 V) due to fuel crossover and mixed potential losses. **(Full marks: 7/7)** --- > **Question B — [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Alternative, 14 Marks]** > > **(a)** Explain the polarization curve of a PEMFC. Identify the three loss regions with their physical origins. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A PEMFC delivers 0.65 V at 1.0 A/cm². Compute (i) voltage efficiency, (ii) overall efficiency assuming 95 % faradaic efficiency, and (iii) power density. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a) — Polarization Curve (7 marks):** The polarization curve plots **cell voltage vs. current density**. It consists of three distinct regions: **Region 1 — Activation Loss (0 – 0.1 A/cm²):** - Steep initial drop in voltage at low current. - Origin: **kinetic barrier** for the ORR (charge transfer resistance). - Governed by the **Tafel equation**: $\eta_{act} = a + b\log_{10}(i)$. - Slope ~60 – 70 mV/decade at low T; ~120 mV/decade at high T. **Region 2 — Ohmic Loss (0.1 – 1.0 A/cm²):** - Linear voltage drop. - Origin: **ionic resistance of membrane** + **electronic resistance of electrodes, GDL, bipolar plates, and contact interfaces**. - Governed by **Ohm's law**: $\eta_{ohmic} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$. - $R_{ASR}$ (area-specific resistance) typically 0.05 – 0.15 Ω·cm². **Region 3 — Concentration Loss (>1.0 A/cm²):** - Sharp voltage drop at high current. - Origin: **mass transport limitation** — reactant gases cannot reach catalyst sites fast enough. - Governed by: $\eta_{conc} = -\dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$. - Limiting current density $i_L \approx 1.5 – 2.0\ A/cm^2$ in air-fed PEMFCs. **Practical operating point:** 0.6 – 0.7 V at 0.5 – 1.0 A/cm² — balances all three losses. **(7/7 marks)** **Part (b) — Efficiency & Power Density (7 marks):** **Given:** $V_{cell} = 0.65\ V$, $i = 1.0\ A/cm^2$, $\eta_{faradaic} = 0.95$ **(i) Voltage Efficiency:** $$\eta_{voltage} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E^0_{thermo}} = \dfrac{0.65}{1.229} = 0.5288 = \mathbf{52.88\ \%}$$ **(ii) Overall Efficiency:** $$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{thermo} \times \eta_{voltage} \times \eta_{faradaic}$$ $$\eta_{overall} = 0.83 \times 0.5288 \times 0.95 = 0.4170 = \mathbf{41.70\ \%}$$ **(iii) Power Density:** $$P = V_{cell} \times i = 0.65 \times 1.0 = \mathbf{0.65\ W/cm^2}$$ **(7/7 marks — all steps shown)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Board Exam — PEMFC Numerical Problems:** > > 1. **Temperature unit conversion errors:** Students often forget to convert °C → K. The Nernst equation **fails silently** if you plug 80 instead of 353 into $\frac{RT}{nF}$. **Loss: 2 marks.** > > 2. **Confusing HHV vs LHV:** $E_{HHV} = 1.481\ V$ (used for efficiency) and $E_{LHV} = 1.229\ V$ (used as thermodynamic reference). Mixing these gives an error of ~17 %. **Loss: 2 marks.** > > 3. **Forgetting partial pressure exponents:** $P_{O_2}^{0.5}$ (square root) is often dropped. This is the **#1 KTU error** in fuel cell problems. **Loss: 2 marks.** > > 4. **Not specifying "atm" units:** Partial pressures in the Nernst equation are dimensionless (ratio) but must be **referenced to 1 atm**. Writing "bar" without conversion loses credit. **Loss: 1 mark.** > > 5. **Overpotential sign convention:** Cathodic overpotential is **negative** in some conventions, positive in others. State your convention explicitly. **Loss: 1 mark.** --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — PEMFC Processes & Operating Conditions** **Three Core Processes:** - ☐ **HOR (Anode):** $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ — fast, 2-electron - ☐ **Proton Conduction (Membrane):** Grotthuss + Vehicle mechanism, requires hydration - ☐ **ORR (Cathode):** $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$ — slow, 4-electron, rate-limiting **Five Operating Conditions:** - ☐ **T = 60 – 80 °C** (low-temp PEMFC) - ☐ **P = 1 – 3 atm** (higher is better, Nernst gain) - ☐ **RH = 50 – 100 %** (membrane conductivity depends on it) - ☐ **Stoichiometry:** H₂ ~ 1.2, Air ~ 2.0 - ☐ **i = 0.2 – 1.5 A/cm²** (current density) **Six Must-Know Numbers:** - ☐ $E^0 = 1.229\ V$ (LHV), $E_{HHV} = 1.481\ V$ - ☐ $\eta_{thermo} = 83\ \%$ - ☐ Tafel slope for ORR: ~70 mV/dec (low T), ~140 mV/dec (high T) - ☐ Nafion conductivity: ~0.1 S/cm (fully hydrated) - ☐ Pt loading: 0.05 (anode) / 0.10 (cathode) mg/cm² - ☐ Operating point: **0.6 – 0.7 V, 0.5 – 1.0 A/cm²** **Three Loss Regions of Polarization Curve:** - ☐ **Activation** (Tafel) — left side, steep - ☐ **Ohmic** (linear) — middle, gentle slope - ☐ **Concentration** (mass transport) — right side, cliff **One Golden Equation:** $$\boxed{V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}}$$ **Engineering Mantra:** *"The cathode dictates the cost, the membrane dictates the durability, and the operating conditions dictate the lifetime."* — Standard KTU examiner's quote.

topics.title: Gibb's Free Energy - Work Potential of Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gibb's Free Energy - Work Potential of Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Fuel Cell Technology**, **Gibb's Free Energy ($G$)** represents the maximum amount of non-expansion (useful electrical) work that can be extracted from a chemical reaction carried out at constant temperature ($T$) and constant pressure ($P$). It is the thermodynamic state function that quantitatively determines the **Work Potential of a Fuel**.

Mathematically, Gibb's Free Energy is defined as:

$$G = H - TS$$

where $H$ is the enthalpy (total heat content), $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin, and $S$ is the entropy of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For any spontaneous electrochemical process occurring inside a fuel cell, the change in Gibb's Free Energy ($\Delta G$) must be **negative** ($\Delta G < 0$). The magnitude $\vert \Delta G \vert$ directly equals the **maximum reversible electrical work** obtainable from the fuel.

The change in Gibb's Free Energy for a reaction is given by:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

For a fuel cell reaction operating at standard conditions ($T = 298.15\,K$, $P = 1\,atm$), the standard change is denoted as $\Delta G^{\circ}$, measured in **kJ/mol**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Reservoir" Model

Imagine a large water reservoir placed on top of a hill. The water inside the reservoir possesses **gravitational potential energy** because of its elevated position. If we open a valve, the water flows downhill through a turbine and generates electricity. The total energy available is determined by two things:

1. **The height difference** (analogous to $\Delta G$ — the energy difference between reactants and products).
2. **The amount (moles) of water flowing** (analogous to the number of electrons transferred, $n$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Think of the fuel (e.g., Hydrogen, Methanol) as the water sitting at the top of a "chemical hill." The Gibb's Free Energy is the **height of that hill** — the steeper the hill, the more electrical work you can extract per mole of fuel consumed inside the fuel cell. Gibb's Free Energy is essentially the **"chemical altitude"** of the fuel.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (KTU Board Essential Values)

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485** | C/mol (or J/V·mol) |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Standard Temperature | $T$ | **298.15** | K |
| Standard Pressure | $P$ | **1** | atm (or 101.325 kPa) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Board Exams:** The numerical value **96,485 C/mol** (often rounded to **96,500 C/mol**) is the single most important constant in Module 2 of PEPOT743. It bridges the chemical world (moles, free energy in kJ) to the electrical world (volts, coulombs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Bridge: $\Delta G$ and Cell EMF

The single most important relationship in fuel cell thermodynamics is the **Gibb's Free Energy — Electromotive Force (EMF) Bridge Equation**:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

### Symbolic Breakdown:
- $\Delta G^{\circ}$ = Standard change in Gibb's Free Energy (J/mol or kJ/mol)
- $n$ = Number of moles of electrons transferred per mole of fuel
- $F$ = Faraday's constant (**96,485 C/mol**)
- $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ = Standard cell potential / open-circuit voltage (Volts, V)

### Logical Reasoning Behind the Equation:

1. **Electrical Work Basis**: The electrical work done by a fuel cell is $W_{\text{elec}} = Q \times V$, where $Q$ is the charge in Coulombs and $V$ is the voltage.
2. **Charge from Moles**: $Q = n \times F$ (charge equals moles of electrons times Faraday's constant).
3. **Maximum Work Equivalence**: For a **reversible** (ideal, lossless) fuel cell, the maximum electrical work equals the decrease in Gibb's Free Energy: $W_{\text{max}} = -\Delta G$.
4. **Combining**: $-\Delta G = nFE_{\text{cell}}$, leading to $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$.

## 2.2 Reversible vs. Irreversible Work — Why Fuel Cells Excel

| Parameter | Heat Engine (Carnot) | Fuel Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Intermediate Step | Combustion $\rightarrow$ Heat $\rightarrow$ Mechanical | Chemical $\rightarrow$ Electrical (Direct) |
| Theoretical Max Efficiency | $1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ |
| Limitation | Carnot Limit (always $< 100\%$) | **Not bounded by Carnot** |
| Typical Efficiency | 30 – 40 % | 40 – 60 % (PEMFC) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU?** A frequently asked 7-mark question asks students to compare fuel cells with conventional heat engines. The answer lies in the fact that fuel cells convert chemical free energy **directly** to electrical energy, bypassing the thermal bottleneck, so they are **not subject to the Carnot limitation**.

## 2.3 The Efficiency Term: $\dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ — "Gibb's Efficiency"

$$\eta_{\text{Gibbs}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} = \frac{-nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{\Delta H^{\circ}}$$

This ratio tells us what fraction of the total heat of combustion ($\Delta H$) is theoretically convertible to **useful electrical work**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the definitive high-yield formula reference for Module 2 of PEPOT743. Memorize every row.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gibb's Free Energy Definition | $G = H - TS$ | kJ/mol | General definition |
| 2 | Change in Gibb's Energy | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | kJ/mol | Calculate from enthalpy & entropy |
| 3 | **Gibb's – EMF Bridge** | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | J/mol or kJ/mol | **Most frequently tested** |
| 4 | Cell EMF from $\Delta G$ | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Volts | Find voltage from free energy |
| 5 | Maximum Electrical Work | $W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G^{\circ} = nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | J/mol | Theoretical work output |
| 6 | Nernst Equation (Non-standard) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Volts | Concentration-dependent voltage |
| 7 | Standard EMF Relation | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | J/mol | Equilibrium constant link |
| 8 | Gibb's Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Gibbs}} = \dfrac{\vert \Delta G^{\circ} \vert}{\vert \Delta H^{\circ} \vert}$ | Dimensionless | Compare to heat engine |
| 9 | Temperature Dependence | $\Delta G(T) = \Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ}$ | kJ/mol | Find $\Delta G$ at any $T$ |
| 10 | Theoretical Voltage (Fuel) | $E^{\circ}_{\text{thermo}} = \dfrac{1.23\,V}{\text{per } 2e^{-} \text{ for } H_2/O_2}$ | Volts | PEMFC & SOFC design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall:** A **negative** $\Delta G$ corresponds to a **positive** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$. A spontaneous reaction ($\Delta G < 0$) is galvanically favourable ($\Delta E > 0$). Mixing up these signs is the **#1 mistake** in KTU board answer sheets for this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$

### Starting Premise (First Law + Second Law)

Consider a fuel cell operating **reversibly** at constant temperature and pressure. We wish to find the maximum non-PV (non-expansion) work obtainable.

**Step 1:** Recall the combined statement of the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics:

$$dG = dH - TdS - SdT$$

**Step 2:** Under constant temperature conditions ($dT = 0$), this simplifies to:

$$dG = dH - TdS$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the definition of enthalpy $H = U + PV$, so $dH = dU + PdV + VdP$. At constant pressure ($dP = 0$):

$$dH = dU + PdV$$

**Step 4:** From the First Law: $dU = dq - dW$. For a reversible process, $dq_{\text{rev}} = TdS$. The total reversible work includes both expansion work and electrical work:

$$dW_{\text{rev}} = PdV + dW_{\text{elec}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute everything into the expression for $dG$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dG &= (dU + PdV) - TdS \\
&= (dq_{\text{rev}} - dW_{\text{rev}}) + PdV - TdS \\
&= TdS - (PdV + dW_{\text{elec}}) + PdV - TdS \\
&= -dW_{\text{elec}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is a profound result — at constant $T$ and $P$, the change in Gibb's Free Energy is **exactly equal to the negative of the reversible non-expansion (electrical) work**.

**Step 6:** For a finite change:

$$\Delta G = -W_{\text{elec, max}}$$

**Step 7:** The electrical work for transferring $n$ moles of electrons through a potential difference $E_{\text{cell}}$ is:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = Q \times E_{\text{cell}} = n \cdot F \cdot E_{\text{cell}}$$

**Step 8:** Equating both expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G &= -nFE_{\text{cell}} \\
\Rightarrow \boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} &= -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Nernst Equation from $\Delta G$ (Board-Favorite)

The Nernst Equation describes how the cell EMF deviates from $E^{\circ}$ when reactants and products are not at standard concentrations.

**Step 1:** For a general reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$, the reaction quotient is:

$$Q = \frac{[C]^c [D]^d}{[A]^a [B]^b}$$

**Step 2:** The general (non-standard) free energy change is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$:

$$-nFE_{\text{cell}} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4:** Divide both sides by $-nF$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cell}} &= E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q \\
\Rightarrow \boxed{E_{\text{cell}} &= E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF}\log_{10} Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

At standard temperature $T = 298.15\,K$, the prefactor simplifies:

$$\frac{2.303\,RT}{F} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} \approx 0.0591\,V$$

So the working form becomes:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10} Q$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Hydrogen Fuel Cell)

**Problem (KTU Sample):** For the hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell reaction at standard conditions:

$$H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$$

Given: $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.83\,kJ/mol$, $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.13\,kJ/mol$.

**Find:** (a) Standard cell EMF $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$, (b) Number of electrons $n$, (c) Gibb's efficiency.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Reaction: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$ (anode); $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$ (cathode). Thus $n = 2$.

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = -\frac{-237.13 \times 10^{3}\,J/mol}{2 \times 96485\,C/mol}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &= \frac{237130}{192970} \\
\Rightarrow E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &\approx 1.229\,V \quad (\text{standard } H_2/O_2 \text{ voltage})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b):** As identified, $n = 2$ electrons per mole of $H_2$.

**Part (c):** Gibb's efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{Gibbs}} = \frac{\vert \Delta G^{\circ} \vert}{\vert \Delta H^{\circ} \vert} = \frac{237.13}{285.83} \approx 0.8296 = 82.96\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At standard conditions, **~83 %** of the chemical energy released by burning hydrogen is theoretically convertible to electrical work. This is dramatically higher than any Carnot-limited heat engine (typically capped at ~40 %). This is precisely why fuel cells are favored for high-efficiency power generation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gibb's Free Energy — Fuel Cell Work Potential Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **logical flow** of how the chemical free energy stored in a fuel is converted into usable electrical work within a fuel cell. This is the conceptual "block diagram" used in KTU board answers when the question asks for a schematic representation of fuel cell energy conversion.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUEL H2 Stored Chemical Energy]:::inputNode --> B[Anode Half Reaction H2 -> 2H+ + 2e-]:::reactNode
    B --> C[Electron Flow through External Circuit]:::electronNode
    C --> D[External Load Electrical Work Welec = nFEcell]:::workNode
    B --> E[Proton Migration H+ through Electrolyte]:::ionNode
    E --> F[Cathode Half Reaction 1/2 O2 + 2H+ + 2e- -> H2O]:::reactNode
    F --> G[Product H2O Heat Released T Delta S]:::heatNode
    D --> H[TOTAL OUTPUT DELTA G = -nFEcell]:::outputNode
    G --> H
    A --> I[Gibbs Free Energy Reservoir DELTA G = H - TS]:::energyNode
    I --> H

    classDef inputNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reactNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef electronNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef ionNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef workNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heatNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#00FF7F,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef energyNode fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Energy Hierarchy in Fuel Cell

This diagram maps the hierarchical relationship between the thermodynamic potentials involved in fuel cell operation — from the broad enthalpy reservoir down to the electrical work extracted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THERMO[THERMODYNAMIC ENERGY HIERARCHY]
        direction TB
        H1[Enthalpy Reservoir DELTA H Total Heat of Combustion]:::en1
        H2[Gibbs Free Energy DELTA G Available Electrical Work]:::en2
        H3[Wasted Thermal Energy T DELTA S Irreversible Losses]:::en3
        H4[Cell EMF Ecell = -DELTA G / nF]:::en4
        H5[Actual Operating Voltage V = Ecell - iR - eta act - eta conc]:::en5
    end

    H1 --> H2
    H1 --> H3
    H2 --> H4
    H4 --> H5

    classDef en1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef en2 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef en3 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef en4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef en5 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for Board Exams:** Notice the strict energy hierarchy — the total enthalpy $\Delta H$ splits into two streams: the **useful electrical work** stream ($\Delta G$, shown in blue) and the **wasted heat** stream ($T\Delta S$, shown in grey). In real fuel cells, additional voltage losses (activation $\eta_{\text{act}}$, ohmic $iR$, concentration $\eta_{\text{conc}}$) further reduce the actual operating voltage below the thermodynamic $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**  
**"Define Gibb's Free Energy. Why is it considered the maximum work function of a fuel cell?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Valuation Key Pattern):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**  
Gibb's Free Energy ($G$) is a thermodynamic state function defined as $G = H - TS$. The change in Gibb's Free Energy ($\Delta G$) for a chemical reaction represents the maximum amount of **non-expansion (electrical) work** obtainable from the reaction when carried out at constant temperature and pressure.

**Why it is the maximum work function (1 Mark):**  
For a reversible process at constant $T$ and $P$, it can be derived from the First and Second Laws that $\Delta G = -W_{\text{non-PV, max}}$. In a fuel cell, the useful output is purely electrical work (no mechanical expansion), so $\Delta G$ directly equals the maximum electrical work available. Any irreversibility (overpotential, internal resistance, etc.) reduces the actual work output below this theoretical maximum.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
**"Write the relationship between Gibb's Free Energy and the cell EMF. State the units of each term in the equation."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Valuation Key Pattern):**

**Relationship (2 Marks):**  
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

**Units (1 Mark):**  
- $\Delta G^{\circ}$ : **J/mol** (or kJ/mol)  
- $n$ : dimensionless (number of electrons transferred per mole of fuel)  
- $F$ : **C/mol** (Coulombs per mole)  
- $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ : **Volts (V)**  

The negative sign indicates that a **spontaneous** reaction ($\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$) produces a **positive** cell EMF.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Gibb's Free Energy change ($\Delta G^{\circ}$) and the standard cell EMF ($E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$) for a fuel cell reaction. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the methanol-oxygen fuel cell reaction at standard conditions:

$$CH_3OH(l) + \tfrac{3}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l)$$

Given: $\Delta G^{\circ} = -702\,kJ/mol$, $\Delta H^{\circ} = -726\,kJ/mol$.

Calculate:  
(i) The standard cell EMF ($E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$) **(3 Marks)**  
(ii) The Gibb's efficiency of the cell **(2 Marks)**  
(iii) The maximum electrical work obtainable per mole of methanol burned **(2 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Starting Point — Combined First & Second Law (1 Mark):**  
For a closed system at constant temperature and pressure:
$$dG = dH - TdS - SdT \quad \Rightarrow \quad dG = dH - TdS \text{ (at constant } T\text{)}$$

**Substituting $dH$ (1 Mark):**  
Since $H = U + PV$ and $dP = 0$ at constant pressure:
$$dH = dU + PdV$$

**Applying First Law to $dU$ (1 Mark):**  
From $dU = dq - dW$, where for a reversible process $dq_{\text{rev}} = TdS$:
$$dU = TdS - dW_{\text{rev}}$$

**Total Reversible Work Decomposition (1 Mark):**  
The total reversible work has two parts — expansion ($PdV$) and non-expansion (electrical, $dW_{\text{elec}}$):
$$dW_{\text{rev}} = PdV + dW_{\text{elec}}$$

**Combining All Expressions (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dG &= dU + PdV - TdS \\
&= (TdS - PdV - dW_{\text{elec}}) + PdV - TdS \\
&= -dW_{\text{elec}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Finite Form & Electrical Work Identity (1 Mark):**  
For a finite process: $\Delta G = -W_{\text{elec, max}}$. For a fuel cell transferring $n$ moles of electrons at cell EMF $E_{\text{cell}}$:
$$W_{\text{elec, max}} = nFE_{\text{cell}}$$

**Final Result (1 Mark):**  
$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}$$

**Physical Significance of Each Term (integrated throughout derivation):**  
- $\Delta G^{\circ}$: Maximum electrical work obtainable per mole of fuel.  
- $n$: Stoichiometric electrons transferred (governed by half-reactions).  
- $F$: Faraday's constant, **96,485 C/mol**, the charge of one mole of electrons.  
- $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$: Theoretical open-circuit voltage under standard conditions.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify number of electrons transferred (1 Mark):**  
Half-reactions:
- Anode: $CH_3OH + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^-$  
- Cathode: $\tfrac{3}{2}O_2 + 6H^+ + 6e^- \rightarrow 3H_2O$  

Thus, $n = 6$ electrons per mole of methanol.

**Step 2 — Standard cell EMF (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &= -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} \\
&= -\frac{-702 \times 10^{3}\,J/mol}{6 \times 96485\,C/mol} \\
&= \frac{702000}{578910} \\
\Rightarrow E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &\approx 1.213\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Gibb's Efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$\eta_{\text{Gibbs}} = \frac{\vert \Delta G^{\circ} \vert}{\vert \Delta H^{\circ} \vert} = \frac{702}{726} \approx 0.967 = 96.7\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Electrical Work (1 Mark):**  
$$W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G^{\circ} = 702\,kJ/mol$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define Gibb's Free Energy. With a neat labeled schematic, explain how a fuel cell extracts electrical work from the free energy change of a fuel oxidation reaction. Compare the thermodynamic efficiency of a fuel cell with a Carnot heat engine. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A direct methanol fuel cell (DMFC) operates at $T = 343\,K$ with the following data: $\Delta H^{\circ}_{298} = -726\,kJ/mol$, $\Delta S^{\circ}_{298} = -81\,J/(mol\cdot K)$. Assume $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ are approximately constant over this temperature range.  

Calculate:  
(i) $\Delta G^{\circ}$ at the operating temperature **(3 Marks)**  
(ii) The standard cell EMF at $343\,K$ **(2 Marks)**  
(iii) Comment on how the Gibb's efficiency changes with operating temperature **(2 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Conceptual & Comparison (7 Marks)

**Definition of Gibb's Free Energy (1 Mark):**  
$G = H - TS$. The change $\Delta G$ for a chemical reaction equals the maximum non-expansion work obtainable at constant $T$ and $P$.

**Fuel Cell Work Extraction Schematic (3 Marks):**  
*(Draw a block diagram showing: Fuel Tank $\rightarrow$ Anode (oxidation, releases electrons and H$^+$) $\rightarrow$ Electrolyte (H$^+$ transport) $\rightarrow$ Cathode (reduction with O$_2$, forms H$_2$O) $\rightarrow$ External circuit (electrons do electrical work = $-\Delta G$) $\rightarrow$ Load.)*  
The electrical work extracted is $W_{\text{elec}} = nFE_{\text{cell}}$, which equals $-\Delta G$ for a reversible (lossless) cell.

**Carnot vs Fuel Cell Efficiency (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Carnot Heat Engine | Fuel Cell |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Path | Chemical $\rightarrow$ Heat $\rightarrow$ Mechanical $\rightarrow$ Electrical | Chemical $\rightarrow$ Electrical (Direct) |
| Maximum Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{low}}}{T_{\text{high}}}$ | $\eta_{\text{Gibbs}} = \dfrac{\vert \Delta G^{\circ} \vert}{\vert \Delta H^{\circ} \vert}$ |
| Theoretical Limit | Always $< 100\%$ due to $T_{\text{low}} > 0\,K$ | Can approach $100\%$ if $\Delta S \approx 0$ |
| Practical Efficiency | 30 – 40 % | 40 – 60 % (PEMFC) |

**Conclusion:** Fuel cells are **not limited by the Carnot theorem** and can therefore achieve higher thermodynamic efficiencies than any heat engine operating between the same temperature limits.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical at Elevated Temperature (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate $\Delta G$ at $T = 343\,K$ (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{\circ}_{343} &= \Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ} \\
&= -726\,kJ/mol - (343\,K)(-81 \times 10^{-3}\,kJ/(mol\cdot K)) \\
&= -726 + 27.783 \\
\Rightarrow \Delta G^{\circ}_{343} &\approx -698.22\,kJ/mol
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard cell EMF at $343\,K$ (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}(343\,K) &= -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{343}}{nF} \\
&= -\frac{-698220\,J/mol}{6 \times 96485\,C/mol} \\
&= \frac{698220}{578910} \\
\Rightarrow E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &\approx 1.206\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Effect on Gibb's Efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$\eta_{\text{Gibbs, 343}} = \frac{698.22}{726} \approx 0.9617 = 96.17\,\%$$

Compared to the value at $298\,K$ (96.7 %), the efficiency **decreases slightly** with increasing temperature. This is because as $T$ rises, the magnitude of $\Delta G$ shrinks (since $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$, and for this reaction $\Delta S < 0$, the $-T\Delta S$ term becomes more positive, making $\Delta G$ less negative).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error on $\Delta G$:** Many students write $\Delta G = +nFE^{\circ}$ (forgetting the negative sign), which gives physically meaningless negative voltages. **Always remember: spontaneous reactions have $\Delta G < 0$ and $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} > 0$.**
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in $F$:** Mixing up J/mol (use $F = 96485\,C/mol$) with kJ/mol (use $F = 96.485\,kC/mol$ or convert $\Delta G$ to J first). KTU examiners specifically check for unit consistency.
> 3. **Wrong $n$ Value:** Counting electrons incorrectly in half-reactions. For $CH_3OH$ oxidation, $n = 6$, not 2 or 4. For $H_2$ oxidation, $n = 2$.
> 4. **Forgetting the negative sign in Gibb's efficiency:** Efficiency is a ratio of magnitudes — use $\vert \Delta G^{\circ} \vert \,/\, \vert \Delta H^{\circ} \vert$.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps:** In Part (a) of 7-mark questions, examiners allot partial marks for each derivation step. Skipping directly to the final equation often costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Quick Reference:**

- ✅ **Core Equation (MEMORIZE):** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$
- ✅ **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485\,C/mol$ (or $96.485\,kC/mol$)
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0 \iff E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} > 0 \iff$ Spontaneous Reaction
- ✅ **Gibb's Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{\vert \Delta G^{\circ} \vert}{\vert \Delta H^{\circ} \vert}$
- ✅ **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log_{10}Q$ (at 298 K)
- ✅ **$H_2/O_2$ Standard EMF:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 1.229\,V$ (most cited KTU value)
- ✅ **Methanol Fuel Cell:** $n = 6$, $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} \approx 1.21\,V$
- ✅ **Fuel Cell Advantage:** **Not bound by Carnot limit** (direct chemical $\rightarrow$ electrical conversion)
- ✅ **Definitions to memorize verbatim:** Gibb's Free Energy, Helmholtz Free Energy, Reversible Work, Open-Circuit Voltage
- ✅ **Always state units:** $\Delta G$ in kJ/mol (or J/mol), $E$ in Volts, $F$ in C/mol.
- ✅ **For derivations:** Show all six logical steps from First + Second Law combination, do NOT skip directly to final result.
- ✅ **For numericals:** Identify $n$ first from balanced half-reactions, then plug into $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$.
- ✅ **Always interpret results:** Mention whether the calculated voltage is realistic (e.g., 1.0–1.3 V range for most fuel cells).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversible Voltage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Reversible Voltage ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Reversible Voltage** (also called **Open-Circuit Voltage**, **OCV**, or **Nernst Voltage**) is the maximum theoretical electromotive force (emf) produced by an electrochemical cell when it operates under **thermodynamic equilibrium** conditions — i.e., when no net current flows through the external circuit and the process is fully reversible. For a generic electrochemical reaction: $$\sum_{i} \nu_i M_i^{z_i} = 0$$ the reversible cell voltage is rigorously defined from the change in **Gibbs free energy (ΔG)** of the cell reaction: $$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$ where: - $E_{rev}$ = reversible cell voltage (V) - $\Delta G$ = change in Gibbs free energy of reaction (J/mol) - $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction - $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight — Standard Reversible Voltage of H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell:** > At Standard Temperature and Pressure (**STP**: 25 °C / 298 K, 1 atm, liquid water product), > $$\boxed{E_{rev}^{o} = \frac{237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})} = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$ > This value is derived from $\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2$ kJ/mol for $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)$. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **water reservoir held at the top of a hill** by a perfectly frictionless dam. The **maximum potential energy** stored in the water (per kilogram) is fixed by the height of the hill — this is *reversible voltage*. The moment you open the dam and let water flow through a turbine, real-world losses kick in (turbine friction, turbulence, pipe resistance), and the *actual* usable energy drops below the maximum. In a fuel cell: - The "hill height" = **Gibbs free energy** of the hydrogen–oxygen reaction. - The "water flow" = electron current through the load. - The "losses" = activation polarization, ohmic losses, and concentration losses (covered later in the module). So, **Reversible Voltage is the thermodynamic ceiling** — the voltage you would measure if you could magically draw an infinitesimally small current such that the cell remains in equilibrium. It is the *target* every practical fuel cell chases but never quite reaches. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Summary:** Reversible voltage is the "ideal" maximum voltage a fuel cell *could* deliver if there were zero losses. It depends only on **what fuels/reactants are used** and **at what temperature and pressure**, NOT on how the cell is built. ### 1.3 Why Reversible Voltage Matters | Aspect | Engineering Significance | |--------|--------------------------| | Benchmark | Theoretical upper bound against which real cell voltage is compared | | Fuel Selection | Different fuels have different $E_{rev}^{\circ}$ (e.g., methanol → 1.21 V, ethanol → 1.14 V) | | System Sizing | Determines maximum stack voltage and number of cells required | | Diagnostics | Deviation of measured OCV from $E_{rev}$ indicates gas crossover or internal short circuits | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation The reversible voltage is rooted in the **First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics**. Consider the generic electrochemical cell reaction: $$A + B \rightarrow C + D + \text{electrical work} + \text{heat}$$ At constant temperature and pressure, the **maximum non-expansion (electrical) work** obtainable equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy: $$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G$$ For a reaction transferring **n** moles of electrons per mole of reaction: $$nFE_{rev} = -\Delta G \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$ Since $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$, the reversible voltage has an **enthalpic component** (chemical energy release) and an **entropic component** (heat exchange with surroundings). --- ### 2.2 Temperature Dependence of $E_{rev}^{\circ}$ Differentiating $nFE_{rev} = -\Delta G$ with respect to T at constant pressure: $$\left(\frac{\partial E_{rev}}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\Delta S}{nF}$$ This gives the **temperature coefficient** of the standard reversible voltage. For the H₂/O₂ cell (liquid water product), experimentally: $$\frac{dE_{rev}^{\circ}}{dT} \approx -0.85 \text{ mV/°C}$$ **Why negative?** Because the entropy change for $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)$ is **negative** (system loses disorder: 1.5 mol gas → 0 mol gas), so $\Delta S < 0$, making $\partial E_{rev}/\partial T < 0$. --- ### 2.3 Pressure & Concentration Dependence — The Nernst Equation For a general reaction at non-standard conditions: $$E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** expressed in terms of activities (≈ partial pressures for gases, ≈ 1 for pure liquids/solids). For the **Hydrogen Fuel Cell** reaction: $$H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$$ $$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$ Assuming liquid water activity $a_{H_2O} \approx 1$: $$\boxed{E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)}$$ **Engineering Insight:** Increasing **reactant partial pressures** raises the reversible voltage logarithmically. This is why high-pressure fuel cell systems (e.g., automotive PEMFC at 2–3 atm) achieve slightly higher OCV than atmospheric systems. --- ### 2.4 Effect of Water Product State | Product State | $\Delta G^{\circ}$ (kJ/mol) | $E_{rev}^{\circ}$ (V) | |---------------|------------------------------|------------------------| | Liquid water | −237.2 | **1.229** | | Water vapor (25 °C) | −228.6 | **1.185** | At higher T, water exits as vapor → lower $E_{rev}$ due to higher (less negative) Gibbs energy. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Variables / Meaning | Typical Units | |---|----------|---------------------|----------------| | 1 | $E_{rev} = -\Delta G / (nF)$ | $n$ = e⁻ transferred, $F$ = 96485 C/mol | V | | 2 | $E_{rev}^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ} / (nF)$ | Standard conditions (298 K, 1 atm) | V | | 3 | $E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} - (RT/nF) \ln Q$ | Nernst equation (general) | V | | 4 | $E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} + (RT/2F) \ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2})$ | H₂/O₂ fuel cell | V | | 5 | $dE_{rev}/dT = \Delta S/(nF)$ | Temperature coefficient | V/K | | 6 | $E_{rev}^{\circ}(T) = E_{rev}^{\circ}(298) - 0.00085(T - 298)$ | Linear approx. for H₂/O₂ | V | | 7 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs–Helmholtz relation | J/mol | | 8 | $W_{elec,max} = nFE_{rev}$ | Maximum electrical work | J/mol | | 9 | $F = N_A \cdot e = 96{,}485$ C/mol | Faraday constant | C/mol | | 10 | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | Universal gas constant | J/(mol·K) | > [!NOTE] > **Constants to Memorize for KTU Exam:** > $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol · $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) · $T_{std} = 298$ K · $E_{rev}^{\circ}$ (H₂/O₂, liq) = 1.229 V --- ### 2.6 Real-World Utility - **Fuel Cell Stack Design:** Number of cells in series = required stack voltage ÷ $E_{rev}$. - **Hybrid Power Systems:** Combined with solar/wind, reversible voltage determines maximum theoretical efficiency. - **Catalyst Diagnostics:** Real OCV < $E_{rev}$ → indicates gas crossover or electronic short. - **Space Applications:** NASA's alkaline fuel cells operated near $E_{rev}$ due to pure H₂/O₂ feeds (Apollo program). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation (from Thermodynamics) **Starting point:** Consider the cell reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$ at temperature T with activities $a_i$. **Step 1 — Chemical potential of species i:** For species participating in the cell reaction, the Gibbs free energy is: $$G_i = G_i^{\circ} + RT \ln a_i$$ **Step 2 — Total Gibbs free energy change:** $$\Delta G = \sum_i \nu_i G_i = \sum_i \nu_i G_i^{\circ} + RT \sum_i \nu_i \ln a_i$$ where $\nu_i$ is positive for products, negative for reactants. $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln\!\left(\prod_i a_i^{\nu_i}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Define the reaction quotient Q:** $$Q = \prod_i a_i^{\nu_i} = \frac{a_C^{c} \cdot a_D^{d}}{a_A^{a} \cdot a_B^{b}}$$ Therefore: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into $E_{rev} = -\Delta G / (nF)$:** $$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q}{nF} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$ **Step 5 — Recognize standard reversible voltage:** Since $E_{rev}^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ}/(nF)$: $$\boxed{E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q}$$ This is the **Nernst Equation** — the central equation of electrochemical thermodynamics. --- ### 3.2 Nernst Equation Applied to the Hydrogen Fuel Cell Cell reaction: $H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l)$ **Step 1 — Write the reaction quotient:** $$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$ For pure liquid water product: $a_{H_2O} = 1$. For ideal gases, activities equal partial pressures (in atm). **Step 2 — Substitute into Nernst equation:** $$E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Apply logarithm identity $-\ln(1/x) = \ln(x)$:** $$\boxed{E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the prefactor at T = 298 K:** $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(298)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \frac{2477.6}{192{,}970} \approx 0.01284 \text{ V} \approx 12.84 \text{ mV}$$ So at room temperature: $$E_{rev} \approx 1.229 + 0.01284 \cdot \ln\!\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right) \text{ V}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Temperature Coefficient Starting from $\Delta G = -nFE_{rev}$. Differentiate w.r.t. T at constant P: $$\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G)}{\partial T}\right)_P = -nF\left(\frac{\partial E_{rev}}{\partial T}\right)_P$$ From fundamental thermodynamics: $\left(\frac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_P = -S$ Therefore: $\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G)}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\Delta S$ Equating both: $$-\Delta S = -nF\left(\frac{\partial E_{rev}}{\partial T}\right)_P$$ $$\boxed{\left(\frac{\partial E_{rev}}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\Delta S}{nF}}$$ For H₂/O₂ at 25 °C: $\Delta S = -163.3$ J/(mol·K), $n = 2$, $F = 96485$ $$\frac{dE_{rev}^{\circ}}{dT} = \frac{-163.3}{(2)(96{,}485)} = -8.46 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K} = -0.846 \text{ mV/°C}$$ --- ### 3.4 Linearized Approximation for $E_{rev}^{\circ}(T)$ Assuming $\Delta S$ is approximately constant over a moderate temperature range (ΔCp ≈ 0): $$E_{rev}^{\circ}(T) \approx E_{rev}^{\circ}(298) + \frac{\Delta S}{nF}(T - 298)$$ $$E_{rev}^{\circ}(T) \approx 1.229 - 0.000846\,(T - 298) \text{ V}$$ **Quick-check values:** | T (°C) | T (K) | $E_{rev}^{\circ}$ (V) | |--------|-------|------------------------| | 25 | 298 | 1.229 | | 60 | 333 | 1.203 | | 80 | 353 | 1.183 | | 100 | 373 | 1.167 | --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** A PEM fuel cell operates at **T = 353 K (80 °C)** with $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm and $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm. Calculate the reversible cell voltage. Assume liquid water product. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Standard voltage at 353 K:** $$E_{rev}^{\circ}(353) = 1.229 - 0.000846\,(353 - 298) = 1.229 - 0.04653 = 1.18247 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Nernst correction:** $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \frac{2934.84}{192{,}970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\ln(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2})$:** $$P_{O_2}^{1/2} = \sqrt{0.21} = 0.4583$$ $$P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2} = 1.5 \times 0.4583 = 0.6874$$ $$\ln(0.6874) = -0.3749$$ **Step 4 — Assemble:** $$E_{rev} = 1.18247 + (0.01521)(-0.3749)$$ $$E_{rev} = 1.18247 - 0.005703 = 1.1768 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{E_{rev} \approx 1.177 \text{ V}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the **thermodynamic ceiling**. Any real PEMFC at the same conditions will deliver a voltage **strictly less** than 1.177 V once current flows, due to polarization losses. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Thermodynamic Cascade: Chemical Energy → Reversible Voltage The following block-level flow maps the **causal pathway** from raw chemical reaction to the resulting reversible voltage, including the temperature and pressure modifiers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CHEMICAL REACTION
H2 plus half O2 yields H2O liquid"] --> B["ENTHALPY CHANGE
Delta H equals minus 285.8 kJ per mol"] A --> C["ENTROPY CHANGE
Delta S equals minus 163.3 J per mol K"] B --> D["GIBBS FREE ENERGY
Delta G equals Delta H minus T Delta S
equals minus 237.2 kJ per mol at 298 K"] C --> D D --> E["STANDARD REVERSIBLE VOLTAGE
Erev zero equals minus Delta G over nF
equals 1.229 V at 25 C"] F["OPERATING TEMPERATURE T"] --> G["TEMPERATURE CORRECTION
dE by dT equals Delta S over nF
equals minus 0.846 mV per K"] G --> E H["PARTIAL PRESSURES
P_H2 and P_O2"] --> I["NERNST CORRECTION
RT over 2F times ln of P_H2 times P_O2 to the half"] E --> J["REVERSIBLE VOLTAGE Erev
Nernst Equation Final Value"] I --> J ``` ### 4.2 Voltage Decay Hierarchy (Open-Circuit vs. Loaded Operation) This diagram illustrates how the **reversible voltage** sits at the very top of the fuel cell voltage hierarchy, with each successive loss pushing the actual operating voltage lower. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1["REVERSIBLE VOLTAGE
Erev equals 1.18 to 1.23 V
Thermodynamic Maximum"] --> B1["ACTIVATION LOSS
Vact equals 0.05 to 0.20 V
Sluggish electrode kinetics"] B1 --> C1["OHMIC LOSS
Vohmic equals I times R
Ionic plus electronic resistance"] C1 --> D1["CONCENTRATION LOSS
Vconc equals 0.05 to 0.10 V
Mass transport limitations"] D1 --> E1["OPERATING CELL VOLTAGE
Vcell equals 0.6 to 0.8 V typical"] A1 -.->|"Erev minus all losses"| E1 ``` ### 4.3 Variable Influence Map ```mermaid flowchart LR T1["TEMPERATURE
T in Kelvin"] -->|Inverse Relation| V1["Erev"] P1["REACTANT PRESSURE
P_H2 and P_O2"] -->|Direct Logarithmic Relation| V1 W1["WATER PRODUCT STATE
Liquid versus Vapor"] -->|Affects Delta G| V1 F1["FUEL TYPE
H2 versus Methanol versus Ethanol"] -->|Affects Delta G zero| V1 V1["REVERSIBLE VOLTAGE Erev"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., A1, B1, V1) to comply with Mermaid parsing rules. Labels are plain uppercase text — no markdown formatting inside the node label strings. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define reversible voltage of a fuel cell. Mention its value for a hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell operating at standard conditions with liquid water product. **Model Answer:** **Reversible voltage** ($E_{rev}$) is the maximum theoretical electromotive force that an electrochemical cell can produce when operating reversibly under thermodynamic equilibrium (no net current flow). It is determined solely by the Gibbs free energy change of the cell reaction: $$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}$$ For the hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell: $$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l), \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}, \quad n = 2$$ $$E_{rev}^{\circ} = \frac{237{,}200}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \mathbf{1.229 \text{ V}}$$ This represents the ideal upper bound; the actual open-circuit voltage is slightly lower due to gas crossover and internal currents. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** State the Nernst equation for a fuel cell and explain its physical significance. **Model Answer:** The Nernst equation gives the reversible voltage under **non-standard** operating conditions: $$E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$ For a hydrogen fuel cell: $$E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$$ **Physical significance:** 1. It quantifies how operating temperature and reactant partial pressures affect the **thermodynamic ceiling** of cell voltage. 2. Increasing reactant pressures increases $E_{rev}$ logarithmically. 3. Increasing temperature generally decreases $E_{rev}$ for exothermic reactions like H₂ combustion (due to negative $\Delta S$). 4. It provides the reference value against which real OCV measurements are compared to detect fuel crossover and internal short circuits. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) **(a) Derive the Nernst equation starting from the fundamental thermodynamic relationship between Gibbs free energy and cell voltage.** [7 marks] **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Chemical Potential Definition:** For any species $i$ in the reaction mixture at temperature T, its Gibbs free energy per mole (chemical potential) is: $$\mu_i = \mu_i^{\circ} + RT\ln a_i$$ where $a_i$ is the activity and $\mu_i^{\circ}$ is the standard chemical potential. **Step 2 — Reaction Gibbs Energy:** For the cell reaction $aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD$: $$\Delta G = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i = \sum_i \nu_i \mu_i^{\circ} + RT\sum_i \nu_i \ln a_i$$ where $\nu_i$ is the stoichiometric coefficient (positive for products, negative for reactants). **Step 3 — Standard Gibbs Energy and Reaction Quotient:** $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln\!\left(\frac{a_C^{c} \cdot a_D^{d}}{a_A^{a} \cdot a_B^{b}}\right) = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$$ **Step 4 — Cell Voltage from Thermodynamics:** The maximum electrical work obtainable equals $-\Delta G$: $$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G = nFE_{rev}$$ **Step 5 — Combine and rearrange:** $$E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$ **Step 6 — Recognize standard voltage:** Since $E_{rev}^{\circ} = -\Delta G^{\circ}/(nF)$: $$\boxed{E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q}$$ This is the **Nernst Equation**. --- **(b) Discuss the effect of operating temperature and reactant pressure on the reversible voltage of a hydrogen fuel cell.** [7 marks] **Model Solution:** **(i) Temperature Effect:** From thermodynamics: $$\left(\frac{\partial E_{rev}}{\partial T}\right)_P = \frac{\Delta S}{nF}$$ For $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O(l)$: - $\Delta S = S_{H_2O(l)} - S_{H_2} - \tfrac{1}{2}S_{O_2} = 70 - 130.7 - 102.5 = -163.2$ J/(mol·K) - $\Delta S < 0$ → reversible voltage **decreases** with increasing temperature. Numerically: $$\frac{dE_{rev}^{\circ}}{dT} = \frac{-163.2}{(2)(96{,}485)} = -8.46 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K} \approx -0.85 \text{ mV/°C}$$ The linearized form: $$E_{rev}^{\circ}(T) \approx 1.229 - 0.000846\,(T - 298) \text{ V}$$ **Engineering Implication:** PEM fuel cells operating at 60–80 °C have lower thermodynamic ceiling than ambient cells, but this trade-off is accepted because higher temperature dramatically improves electrode kinetics and reduces activation losses. **(ii) Pressure Effect:** From the Nernst equation for H₂/O₂ cell: $$E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$$ Increasing partial pressures raises $E_{rev}$ **logarithmically**: - At 298 K, doubling $P_{H_2}$ raises $E_{rev}$ by $\frac{RT}{2F}\ln 2 = 0.01284 \times 0.693 = 8.9$ mV. - Doubling both $P_{H_2}$ and $P_{O_2}$ raises $E_{rev}$ by $0.01284 \times \ln(2 \cdot 2^{0.5}) = 0.01284 \times 1.0397 = 13.35$ mV. **Engineering Implication:** High-pressure operation improves thermodynamics, but the practical gain (~10–20 mV) must be balanced against compressor parasitic losses. Automotive PEMFC systems typically pressurize to 2–3 atm — a compromise. **(iii) Combined Effect:** Higher T lowers $E_{rev}$ but improves kinetics; higher P raises $E_{rev}$ but costs compressor power. Real systems optimize at the **polarization curve** intersection (around 0.6–0.7 V/cell). --- #### Question B (14 Marks) **(a) Explain the concept of reversible voltage of a fuel cell with reference to Gibbs free energy. Why is it called the "thermodynamic maximum" voltage?** [7 marks] **Model Solution:** **Conceptual Foundation:** The reversible voltage is the electromotive force generated when an electrochemical cell operates under **thermodynamic reversibility** — meaning the cell reaction proceeds through a continuous sequence of equilibrium states, with no net loss of useful energy to irreversibilities. For any spontaneous electrochemical reaction, the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** dictates that the maximum useful (non-PV) work obtainable at constant T and P equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy: $$W_{max} = -\Delta G \quad \text{(at constant T, P)}$$ Since this work is delivered as electrical energy through the transfer of $n$ moles of electrons per mole of reaction, each carrying charge $F$: $$W_{max} = nFE_{rev}$$ Combining: $$\boxed{E_{rev} = -\frac{\Delta G}{nF}}$$ **Why "Thermodynamic Maximum"?** 1. **No irreversibility:** Assumes infinitesimal current (quasi-equilibrium), so no activation, ohmic, or concentration losses occur. 2. **All chemical energy converted to electrical work:** Any entropy production (heat dissipation) would reduce the usable energy below $-\Delta G$. 3. **Equilibrium condition:** At finite current, the cell voltage drops below $E_{rev}$ due to entropy-generating losses; hence $E_{rev}$ is an unattainable ceiling for any practical current. **Relation to Enthalpy and Entropy:** $$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$ So: $$E_{rev} = \frac{-\Delta H}{nF} + \frac{T\Delta S}{nF}$$ - First term = **enthalpic contribution** (chemical bond energy converted to electricity). - Second term = **entropic contribution** (heat absorbed from or released to surroundings during reversible operation). For H₂/O₂ cell: $\Delta H = -285.8$ kJ/mol → "enthalpic voltage" = 1.481 V. The reversible voltage (1.229 V) is *lower* because the cell must reject ~48.6 kJ/mol of heat (T·ΔS) at 298 K to operate reversibly. **Conclusion:** Reversible voltage represents the *ideal* conversion of chemical free energy into electricity and is the benchmark against which all real fuel cell performance is measured. --- **(b) A PEM fuel cell operates at 80 °C with $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm and $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm. Calculate the reversible cell voltage. Assume liquid water product.** [7 marks] **Model Solution:** **Given:** - T = 80 °C = 353 K - $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm - Liquid water: $a_{H_2O} = 1$ - Constants: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, $n = 2$ **Step 1 — Standard Reversible Voltage at 353 K:** Using the linearized approximation (assuming constant $\Delta S$): $$E_{rev}^{\circ}(353) = E_{rev}^{\circ}(298) + \frac{\Delta S}{nF}(353 - 298)$$ $$E_{rev}^{\circ}(353) = 1.229 + \frac{(-163.3)}{(2)(96{,}485)}(55)$$ $$E_{rev}^{\circ}(353) = 1.229 - 0.04657 = 1.18243 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Nernst Prefactor at 353 K:** $$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \frac{2934.84}{192{,}970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Pressure Term:** $$P_{O_2}^{1/2} = \sqrt{0.21} = 0.4583$$ $$P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2} = 1.5 \times 0.4583 = 0.6874$$ $$\ln(0.6874) = -0.3749$$ **Step 4 — Apply Nernst Equation:** $$E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ}(353) + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$$ $$E_{rev} = 1.18243 + (0.01521)(-0.3749)$$ $$E_{rev} = 1.18243 - 0.005703$$ $$\boxed{E_{rev} \approx 1.177 \text{ V}}$$ **Interpretation:** - This is the **maximum possible** voltage at the given conditions. - A real PEMFC at 0.5–1 A/cm² will deliver 0.6–0.75 V — roughly **35–40% below** this thermodynamic ceiling. - The difference (0.42–0.58 V) represents cumulative polarization losses (activation + ohmic + concentration). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting temperature correction (–0.85 mV/°C):** Many students plug 1.229 V directly into the Nernst equation without first correcting $E_{rev}^{\circ}$ for T ≠ 298 K. This costs **2–3 marks**. > > 2. **Using partial pressure ratio in atm without stating assumption:** Always state that ideal gas behavior is assumed and partial pressures are in atm. > > 3. **Sign error in Nernst equation:** The term is $+\frac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{H_2} P_{O_2}^{1/2})$ for H₂/O₂ (not minus). Forgetting the negative sign of $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$ is a frequent error. > > 4. **Activity of water ≠ 1 for vapor product:** If the question states water exits as vapor, you must include $a_{H_2O}$ in the denominator (or use the vapor $E_{rev}^{\circ} = 1.185$ V). > > 5. **Wrong number of electrons:** H₂/O₂ cell transfers **n = 2** electrons. DO NOT use n = 4. > > 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Pressure must be in atm (numerically) when using the standard Nernst form with $E_{rev}^{\circ}$ in V. If pressure is in kPa or bar, convert first. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Reversible voltage = $-\Delta G/(nF)$** — the thermodynamic ceiling of cell emf. - **Standard value for H₂/O₂ (liquid water, 25 °C, 1 atm):** $E_{rev}^{\circ} = 1.229$ V (memorize). - **Nernst Equation (general):** $E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} - (RT/nF)\ln Q$. - **Nernst Equation (H₂/O₂ fuel cell):** $E_{rev} = E_{rev}^{\circ} + (RT/2F)\ln(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2})$. - **Temperature coefficient:** $dE_{rev}/dT = \Delta S/(nF) \approx -0.85$ mV/°C for H₂/O₂. - **Linearized $E_{rev}^{\circ}(T)$:** $1.229 - 0.000846(T - 298)$ V (T in K). - **Water product state matters:** liquid → 1.229 V; vapor → 1.185 V (at 25 °C). - **Increasing pressure → higher $E_{rev}$** (logarithmic dependence). - **Increasing temperature → lower $E_{rev}$** (for exothermic H₂ combustion). - **Practical fuel cell voltage is ALWAYS less than $E_{rev}$** due to polarization losses. - **Constants to memorize:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $T_{std} = 298$ K. - **Difference between $E_{rev}$ and OCV:** OCV (measured) ≤ $E_{rev}$ (theoretical) due to gas crossover and internal short circuits. - **$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$** links reversible voltage to both enthalpy (chemical energy) and entropy (heat effects). - **n = 2** for hydrogen fuel cell — never 4!

topics.title: Nernst Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Nernst Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Nernst Equation** is a fundamental electrochemical relationship that quantifies the reduction potential (or electromotive force) of an electrochemical half-cell or full cell reaction under non-standard conditions of temperature, pressure, and reactant/product concentrations. Mathematically expressed in its most general thermodynamic form, it bridges the gap between the standard electrode potential ($E^\circ$) and the actual operating potential ($E$) of an electrochemical system.

For a generalized reversible electrochemical reaction of the form:

$$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD + ne^-$$

the Nernst Equation is written as:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** evaluated at the actual operating state of the cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEPOT743 – Module 2):** The Nernst Equation is the cornerstone of fuel cell voltage prediction. It directly enables calculation of the open-circuit voltage (OCV) of a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell under varying temperatures, pressures, and partial pressures of reactants — making it indispensable for fuel cell stack design, performance modeling, and thermodynamic efficiency analysis.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Water Tank" Intuition

Imagine a water tank placed on a hill, representing an electrochemical cell with stored chemical energy. The **height** of water in the tank is analogous to the **standard electrode potential ($E^\circ$)** — the maximum "height" of energy the system can deliver. Now, suppose we draw some water out (reactants get consumed) or add more water (products build up). The actual water level — and therefore the actual pressure at the tap — changes. This **actual pressure** is analogous to the **cell potential ($E$)** under non-standard conditions.

The Nernst Equation is essentially the mathematical tool that tells us: *"Given how much water is in the tank right now, what is the actual pressure compared to the maximum?"* — the **reaction quotient $Q$** captures the "current fill level" of the chemical system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in the Nernst Equation:**
> - $R$ = **Universal Gas Constant = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - $F$ = **Faraday's Constant = 96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)**
> - $T$ = Absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**
> - $n$ = Number of moles of **electrons transferred** per mole of reaction
> - $Q$ = Dimensionless **reaction quotient** (activities of products / reactants)

### The Standard-State Conversion Form (298 K)

A particularly useful form of the Nernst Equation arises when the temperature is fixed at the standard reference temperature of **298.15 K (25 °C)**. Substituting $T = 298.15$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), and $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, and converting the natural logarithm ($\ln$) to the common logarithm ($\log_{10}$) using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.05916}{n} \log_{10} Q \quad \text{(at 25 °C, in Volts)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** The numerical factor **0.0592 V** (or sometimes rounded to **0.0591 V**) appears in almost every KTU numerical problem on the Nernst Equation. Memorize it — it is your "golden key" to 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Breaking Down the Nernst Equation Step-by-Step

The Nernst Equation does not appear out of thin air; it emerges from rigorous application of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** to an electrochemical cell. The logic chain is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Gibbs Free Energy Linkage:** The maximum electrical work obtainable from a galvanic cell equals the negative change in Gibbs free energy:
$$W_{max} = -\Delta G$$
- **Step 2 — Electrical Work Expression:** This work is also equal to the charge transferred multiplied by the cell potential:
$$W_{max} = nFE$$
- **Step 3 — Standard State Reference:** At standard conditions, this becomes:
$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$$
- **Step 4 — Non-Standard Correction:** For non-standard states, the relationship between $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G^\circ$ is:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$
- **Step 5 — Combining the Equations:** Substituting the electrical work expressions and solving for $E$ yields the **Nernst Equation** in its full thermodynamic form.

### Why Each Term Matters — The Physical Interpretation

| Term | Physical Meaning | Effect on Cell Voltage |
|------|------------------|------------------------|
| $E^\circ$ | Standard electrode potential | Sets the baseline voltage at standard conditions |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | Scales the thermal energy contribution |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | Higher $T$ increases the correction magnitude |
| $n$ | Electrons transferred per mole | More electrons → smaller per-electron correction |
| $F$ | Faraday's constant | Converts between chemical and electrical units |
| $\ln Q$ | Logarithmic state function | Captures deviation from standard state |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Expression | Conditions / Units | Application Context |
|----------|------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **General Nernst Equation** | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | General $T$, reversible cell | Thermodynamic analysis at any temperature |
| **Standard-Temperature Form** | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q$ | $T = 298.15$ K | Board exam default form |
| **Half-Cell Nernst** | $E_{red} = E^\circ_{red} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log \dfrac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$ | For single half-reaction | Cathode or anode potential calculation |
| **Cell Nernst (full cell)** | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ (with each computed via Nernst) | Reversible cell | Fuel cell OCV determination |
| **Gibbs–Nernst Link** | $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$ | Energy in J/mol | Connecting electrical to chemical energy |
| **Equilibrium Nernst** | $E = 0$ at equilibrium, giving $E^\circ = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln K_{eq}$ | At $\Delta G = 0$ | Determines $K_{eq}$ from $E^\circ$ |
| **Fuel Cell (H$_2$/O$_2$)** | $E = 1.229 - \dfrac{0.0592}{2} \log \dfrac{[H_2O]}{[H_2][O_2]^{1/2}}$ | Acidic PEMFC | Hydrogen fuel cell OCV |
| **Temperature Corrected** | $E(T) = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Any $T$ in K | High-temperature SOFC analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Discipline:** All concentrations in $Q$ are strictly **activities** (dimensionless). For gases, this becomes the **partial pressure (in bar or atm) divided by the standard pressure (1 bar or 1 atm)**. For solutes, it is the **molarity divided by 1 M**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The Nernst Equation is the **design equation** behind nearly every fuel cell performance model. In the context of PEPOT743, its applications include:

- **Hydrogen-Oxygen PEM Fuel Cells:** Predicting the open-circuit voltage of a Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC) operating at 60–80 °C with non-standard gas partial pressures.
- **Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFC):** Modeling the voltage-temperature relationship at 600–1000 °C where the $RT/nF$ term is significantly larger.
- **Methanol and Direct Carbon Fuel Cells:** Calculating theoretical efficiencies for alternative fuel feedstocks.
- **Battery Management Systems:** State-of-charge estimation by inverting the Nernst Equation to solve for $Q$ from measured $E$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: The Nernst Equation from Gibbs Free Energy

We begin from the universal thermodynamic relation between Gibbs free energy and the reaction quotient:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$
