# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**Define exchange current density. Why is it considered the most important kinetic parameter in fuel cells?**

**Model Answer:**

The **exchange current density ($i_0$)** is defined as the equal and opposite current density that flows across an electrode–electrolyte interface at equilibrium (zero overpotential), when the rates of the forward and reverse reactions are equal and no net current is observed.

It is the most important kinetic parameter because:

1. **Direct measure of inherent activity:** A higher $i_0$ means the electrode can sustain larger currents with smaller overpotential losses.
2. **Determines activation overpotential:** $\eta_{act} = (RT/\alpha F)\ln(i/i_0)$ — larger $i_0$ → smaller $\eta_{act}$.
3. **Catalyst benchmark:** Comparing $i_0$ values for ORR on different catalysts (e.g., Pt vs. Ag) is the standard way to rank electrocatalyst performance.
4. **Affects Tafel intercept:** $a = -(2.303 RT/\alpha F)\log_{10}(i_0)$ — higher $i_0$ shifts the polarization curve to lower overpotentials.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**What is the Tafel slope? What does a small Tafel slope indicate about a catalyst?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Tafel slope ($b$)** is the slope of the linear region of a plot of overpotential ($\eta$) versus the logarithm of current density ($\log i$), as expressed by the Tafel equation:

$$\eta = a + b \log_{10}(i)$$

Theoretically:

$$b = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha F}$$

At 25°C with $\alpha = 0.5$, $b \approx 120$ mV/dec; with $\alpha = 1$, $b \approx 60$ mV/dec.

A **small Tafel slope** indicates:
- The overpotential increases **slowly** with current density, meaning **superior catalytic activity**.
- A smaller activation energy barrier and more efficient charge transfer.
- Better voltage efficiency, especially in the high-current regime of fuel cell operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the Butler–Volmer equation starting from the rate expressions of a one-electron transfer reaction. Discuss its two limiting cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a PEM fuel cell cathode with $i_0 = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ A/cm², $\alpha_c = 0.5$, $T = 353$ K, and operating current density $i = 0.8$ A/cm², calculate the activation overpotential. How much will the overpotential reduce if a new catalyst gives $i_0 = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ A/cm²? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Butler–Volmer Equation**

**Step 1:** For the elementary reaction $O + e^- \rightleftharpoons R$:

$$i = i_c - i_a = F(k_f C_O - k_b C_R)$$

**Step 2:** Potential-dependent rate constants (activated complex theory):

$$k_f = k^0 \exp\!\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F (E - E_{eq})}{RT} \right)$$

$$k_b = k^0 \exp\!\left( \frac{\alpha_a F (E - E_{eq})}{RT} \right)$$

**Step 3:** Define overpotential $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ and substitute. At equilibrium ($i = 0$, $\eta = 0$), the two terms are equal, defining $i_0$:

$$i_0 = F k^0 C_O^{1-\alpha_c} C_R^{\alpha_c}$$

**Step 4:** After algebraic simplification using $C_O/C_R$ eliminated via Nernst equation:

$$\boxed{\, i = i_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right) - \exp\!\left( -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT} \right) \right] \,}$$

**Limiting Case 1: Low overpotential** ($\vert \eta \vert \ll RT/F$): Using $e^x \approx 1 + x$,

$$i \approx i_0 (\alpha_a + \alpha_c) \frac{F \eta}{RT}$$

Linear $i$–$\eta$ relation; this region defines polarization resistance $R_p = RT/(i_0 F)$.

**Limiting Case 2: High overpotential:** If $\eta \gg 0$ (anodic dominance):

$$i \approx i_0 \exp\!\left( \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT} \right) \implies \eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha_a F} \ln\!\left( \frac{i}{i_0} \right) \quad \text{(Tafel form)}$$

Similarly for large cathodic $\eta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Calculation**

**Step 1:** Compute thermal voltage at 353 K:

$$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{96485} = 0.03042 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Activation overpotential with $i_0 = 5 \times 10^{-10}$ A/cm²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{act}^{(1)} &= \frac{RT}{\alpha_c F} \ln\!\left( \frac{i}{i_0} \right) = \frac{0.03042}{0.5} \ln\!\left( \frac{0.8}{5 \times 10^{-10}} \right) \\[6pt]
&= 0.06084 \times \ln(1.6 \times 10^{9}) = 0.06084 \times 21.19 \\[6pt]
&= 1.289 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Activation overpotential with $i_0 = 5 \times 10^{-9}$ A/cm² (10× higher):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{act}^{(2)} &= 0.06084 \times \ln\!\left( \frac{0.8}{5 \times 10^{-9}} \right) = 0.06084 \times \ln(1.6 \times 10^{8}) \\[6pt]
&= 0.06084 \times 18.89 = 1.149 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Reduction in overpotential:

$$\Delta\eta = 1.289 - 1.149 = 0.140 \text{ V (140 mV)}$$

> **A 10× increase in $i_0$ reduces the activation overpotential by ~140 mV**, demonstrating the dramatic impact of improved catalysts. **(7 marks)**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of electrocatalysis. Discuss the strategies used to improve the kinetic performance of fuel cell reactions, with a special emphasis on the Sabatier principle and volcano plots. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel cell anode uses a Pt catalyst with $i_0 = 10^{-3}$ A/cm² for HOR, operating at 0.5 A/cm² and 80°C. The ORR cathode has $i_0 = 10^{-10}$ A/cm² at the same current and temperature, with $\alpha_c = 0.5$. Compare the activation overpotentials and comment on which electrode is the kinetic bottleneck. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Electrocatalysis and Performance-Improvement Strategies**

**Definition:** Electrocatalysis is the use of catalytic materials to **lower the activation energy** of electrochemical charge-transfer reactions at electrode–electrolyte interfaces, thereby **increasing $i_0$ and reducing the Tafel slope**.

**Sabatier Principle:** The best catalyst binds reaction intermediates (e.g., *O, OH, OOH* for ORR) with **intermediate strength** — neither too strongly (otherwise the surface is poisoned and product release is hindered) nor too weakly (reactant activation is poor).

**Volcano Plot:** A plot of catalytic activity (e.g., $\log i_0$ for ORR) versus a binding-energy descriptor (e.g., $\Delta E_{M-O}$) yields a volcano-shaped curve, with peak activity at optimal binding. **Platinum sits near the peak** for ORR.

**Strategies to improve kinetic performance:**

1. **Catalyst selection:** Use Pt-group metals for low Tafel slope and high $i_0$.
2. **Alloying:** Pt₃Ni, PtCo, PtY shift the Pt d-band center, weakening *OH* poisoning and improving ORR activity by 4–10×.
3. **Nanostructuring:** Nanoparticles, nanowires, nanoframes, and 1D/2D structures maximize electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) per gram of Pt.
4. **Support engineering:** High-surface-area carbon (Ketjenblack), graphene, or TiO₂ supports improve dispersion and prevent agglomeration.
5. **Single-atom catalysts (SACs):** Atomic dispersion maximizes utilization.
6. **Operating conditions:** Higher T and P (within material limits) enhance kinetics. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Comparison of Anode vs Cathode Overpotentials**

**Given:**
- Anode (HOR): $i_0^{(a)} = 10^{-3}$ A/cm², assume $\alpha_a = 0.5$
- Cathode (ORR): $i_0^{(c)} = 10^{-10}$ A/cm², $\alpha_c = 0.5$
- $T = 80°C = 353$ K
- $i = 0.5$ A/cm²

**Step 1:** Compute $RT/F = 0.03042$ V

**Step 2:** Anode activation overpotential:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{act}^{(a)} &= \frac{RT}{\alpha_a F} \ln\!\left( \frac{i}{i_0^{(a)}} \right) = \frac{0.03042}{0.5} \ln\!\left( \frac{0.5}{10^{-3}} \right) \\[6pt]
&= 0.06084 \times \ln(500) = 0.06084 \times 6.215 \\[6pt]
&= 0.378 \text{ V} \quad \text{(apparent — but HOR is fast; linear regime applies)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction:** Because $i_0^{(a)}$ is so high (10⁻³), the HOR anode operates in the **linear (low-η) regime** at 0.5 A/cm². The correct approach is:
> $$\eta_{act}^{(a)} = \frac{RT}{i_0^{(a)} F} \cdot i = \frac{0.03042}{10^{-3}} \times 0.5 \times \frac{1}{... \text{ (use proper expression)}}$$

Using the **low-η linear form** with $\alpha_a + \alpha_c \approx 1$:

$$\eta_{act}^{(a)} \approx \frac{RT}{i_0^{(a)} F} \cdot i = \frac{0.03042}{10^{-3} \times 1} \times 0.5 = 15.2 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3:** Cathode activation overpotential (Tafel regime applies since $i_0$ is very small):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{act}^{(c)} &= \frac{RT}{\alpha_c F} \ln\!\left( \frac{i}{i_0^{(c)}} \right) = \frac{0.06084}{0.5} \cdot \frac{1}{0.5} \cdot \ln\!\left( \frac{0.5}{10^{-10}} \right) \\[6pt]
&= 0.06084 \times \ln(5 \times 10^{9}) \\[6pt]
&= 0.06084 \times 22.33 = 1.358 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comparison & Conclusion:**

| Electrode | $i_0$ (A/cm²) | $\eta_{act}$ | Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anode (HOR) | $10^{-3}$ | ~15 mV | Linear (low-η) |
| Cathode (ORR) | $10^{-10}$ | ~1360 mV | Tafel (high-η) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using the Tafel equation for the HOR anode when it is actually in the linear regime. This gives an unrealistically high $\eta$ (~0.38 V) and **loses 2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert $T = 80°C$ to Kelvin (353 K) and using 298 K instead. **Loses 1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not specifying which regime (linear vs Tafel) applies before computing. Always **check the magnitude of $i/i_0$** to determine the regime.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Failing to explicitly state the bottleneck. **The cathode (ORR) is clearly the kinetic bottleneck** — its $i_0$ is 7 orders of magnitude lower, and its $\eta_{act}$ is ~90× higher than the anode.

**Conclusion:** The **cathode (ORR) is the kinetic bottleneck** of the PEM fuel cell. All electrocatalysis research is therefore directed at the cathode to raise $i_0$ and lower the Tafel slope. **(7 marks)**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Exchange current density ($i_0$)** is the bidirectional current at equilibrium; it is the **primary indicator of catalyst activity**.
- ✅ **Butler–Volmer equation** is the master equation of electrode kinetics: $i = i_0[\exp(\alpha_a F\eta/RT) - \exp(-\alpha_c F\eta/RT)]$.
- ✅ **Tafel slope** $b = 2.303 RT/(\alpha F)$; a **smaller b** and a **larger $i_0$** both mean a better catalyst.
- ✅ **Tafel plot:** $\eta$ vs $\log i$ is linear at high $\eta$; slope gives $b$, intercept gives $i_0$.
- ✅ **Volcano plot & Sabatier principle:** Optimal binding of intermediates is key; Pt sits near the peak for ORR.
- ✅ **ORR is the rate-limiting reaction** in PEMFCs because its $i_0$ is ~$10^{-10}$ A/cm², about **6–7 orders of magnitude smaller** than that of HOR.
- ✅ **Strategies to improve kinetics:** (1) Catalyst selection, (2) Alloying (Pt₃Ni, PtCo), (3) Nanostructuring, (4) Support engineering, (5) Single-atom catalysts, (6) Optimized operating conditions.
- ✅ **A 10× increase in $i_0$ reduces $\eta_{act}$ by ~60–140 mV** depending on T and α.
- ✅ **Numerical constant to memorize:** $RT/F = 0.0257$ V at 25°C, $0.0304$ V at 80°C; $2.303 RT/F = 0.0592$ V at 25°C.
- ✅ **Always check whether $i/i_0 \ll 1$ (linear regime) or $i/i_0 \gg 1$ (Tafel regime)** before applying the appropriate Butler–Volmer limiting form.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** For a 14-mark question, the examiner typically awards 5–6 marks for theory/concepts and 6–7 marks for derivation/calculation. **Always show the regime check** (linear vs Tafel) and clearly state which Butler–Volmer form you are using. Drawing a labeled polarization curve or Tafel plot earns easy extra marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrode Design Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrode Design Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of a Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC) or any electrochemical energy conversion device, an **electrode** is a heterogeneous, multi-functional interface where electrochemical half-reactions (oxidation at the **anode**, reduction at the **cathode**) take place. It is *not* a simple metallic conductor — it is a porous, three-dimensional composite structure engineered to simultaneously host electronic conduction, ionic conduction, and gas/liquid transport while providing catalytically active sites.

The **active layer** of a fuel cell electrode is therefore formally defined as the **Electrochemically Active Region** where the **Triple Phase Boundary (TPB)** is established — the microscopic locus at which the **reactant gas**, the **electrolyte (proton-conducting ionomer)**, and the **electron-conducting catalyst/support** all meet within a critical reaction-zone distance (typically $< 10\,\mu\text{m}$ from the membrane).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEPOT743 / Module 2):**
> *"Electrode Design Basics — types, materials, microstructure, catalyst layer, GDL, TPB, MEA architecture, electrode kinetics."*

### 1.2 The Three Functional Layers of a Modern Fuel Cell Electrode

A working gas-diffusion electrode is built up of **three functional layers**, sandwiched between the membrane and the bipolar plate flow-field:

| # | Layer | Function | Typical Material | Thickness |
|---|-------|----------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** | Distributes reactant gas uniformly; removes product water; conducts electrons laterally to the bipolar plate; mechanical support | Carbon paper / carbon cloth (e.g., Toray TGP-H) | **190 – 400 µm** |
| 2 | **Microporous Layer (MPL)** | Bridges GDL & catalyst layer; reduces electrical contact resistance; controls water management at the pore-mouth | Carbon black (Vulcan XC-72) + PTFE binder | **10 – 50 µm** |
| 3 | **Catalyst Layer (CL)** | The actual electrochemical reaction site; contains Pt nanoparticles supported on carbon, mixed with proton-conducting ionomer (Nafion) | Pt/C + Nafion ionomer | **5 – 30 µm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Catalyst Loading Standards (KTU/Industry Reference):**
> - State-of-art automotive PEMFC target: **0.10 – 0.20 mg Pt/cm²** (DOE 2025 target).
> - Conventional Pt loading: **0.2 – 0.4 mg Pt/cm²**.
> - Note that **80 % of total Pt** is loaded at the **cathode** because the Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) is ~6 orders of magnitude slower than the Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Three-Way Traffic Junction"

Imagine a busy city intersection where three different types of vehicles must converge at *exactly one* point to complete a delivery:

- 🚗 **Electrons** (delivered by carbon-black-supported catalyst particles — the road network)
- 🚲 **Protons (H⁺)** (delivered by the Nafion ionomer — a dedicated bicycle lane)
- 🚚 **Reactant gas (H₂ or O₂)** (delivered through the pores — a delivery truck lane)

If any one of the three "vehicles" fails to arrive at the same instant at a particular spot, **no reaction takes place** there. The **Triple Phase Boundary (TPB)** is precisely that microscopic intersection where all three lanes coexist. A *good electrode design* maximizes the **TPB length per unit volume** — i.e., it builds a vast, interconnected network of these intersections, just like a smart city maximizes signalised crossings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Catalyst Particles Are Nano-sized (~3 nm):**
> Reducing Pt particle size exponentially increases the **electrochemically active surface area (ECSA)** per gram of precious metal. A 3 nm Pt sphere has roughly **\mathbf{\approx 70\,m^2/g}** ECSA, whereas a 30 nm particle drops to ~7 m²/g. *Smaller is more efficient — but only down to ~2 nm, below which Pt oxidation (PtO formation) and particle sintering degrade performance.*

### 1.4 Why Electrode Design Is the *Heart* of Fuel Cell Performance

A PEMFC stack's voltage efficiency at any operating current $j$ is governed by:

$$\eta_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{thermo}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$

- $\eta_{\text{act}}$ → **Activation overpotential** — dominated by *cathode catalyst kinetics* → **Electrode design directly controls this.**
- $\eta_{\text{ohmic}}$ → Membrane + contact resistance → *partly controlled by electrode-membrane interface.*
- $\eta_{\text{conc}}$ → Mass-transport losses → **Controlled by GDL/MPL porosity & hydrophobicity.**

Therefore, **>60 % of performance losses in a PEMFC originate from electrode design choices**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Requirements of an Ideal Electrode

An optimally designed fuel-cell electrode must satisfy **six simultaneous engineering constraints**:

1. **High Electronic Conductivity** — $\sigma_e \geq 100\,\text{S/cm}$ (carbon-black-supported catalyst gives $\approx 1$–$10\,\text{S/cm}$ effective).
2. **High Protonic Conductivity** — continuous ionomer (Nafion) network with $\sigma_{H^+} \geq 0.05\,\text{S/cm}$ at 80 °C, 100 % RH.
3. **Adequate Gas Porosity** — $\varepsilon \approx 30$ – $60\,\%$ for H₂/O₂ transport.
4. **Catalytic Activity** — high ECSA (m² Pt / g Pt) and high **exchange current density $j_0$**.
5. **Chemical / Electrochemical Stability** — resistant to oxidation (carbon support corrosion at $>0.8\,\text{V}$), Pt dissolution, and contaminant poisoning (CO, S).
6. **Water Management Capability** — balanced hydrophobic (PTFE) / hydrophilic (ionomer) pore structure.

### 2.2 The Triple Phase Boundary — Quantitative Definition

The TPB is **not** a 2-D surface but a **3-D percolating network** of contact points. Quantitatively:

$$\text{TPB length}\,[\text{cm/cm}^2] = f(\phi_{\text{Pt}},\;\phi_{\text{ionomer}},\;\phi_{\text{pore}},\;d_{\text{Pt}})$$

where $\phi_i$ are the volume fractions of Pt, ionomer, and pores in the CL. Optimal CL composition is typically:

$$\phi_{\text{Pt/C}} : \phi_{\text{ionomer}} : \phi_{\text{pore}} \approx 40 : 30 : 30\;\text{(vol \%)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Percolation Theory Insight (KTU favourite):**
> Below the **percolation threshold** of any one phase, that phase becomes disconnected — and the reaction rate **collapses to zero**. This is why electrode designers always quote **"above the percolation threshold"** when characterising ionomer content.

### 2.3 Butler-Volmer Framework — The Mathematical Core

The fundamental kinetic equation governing *any* electrode-electrolyte interface is the **Butler-Volmer equation**:

$$j = j_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

where:

- $j$ — net current density $[\text{A/cm}^2]$
- $j_0$ — exchange current density $[\text{A/cm}^2]$ (the fundamental kinetic figure-of-merit)
- $\eta = E - E_{\text{eq}}$ — activation overpotential $[\text{V}]$
- $\alpha_a,\;\alpha_c$ — anodic / cathodic transfer coefficients (dimensionless), with $\alpha_a + \alpha_c = 1$ for a single-electron step
- $F = 96485\,\text{C/mol}$ — Faraday constant
- $R = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$ — universal gas constant
- $T$ — absolute temperature $[\text{K}]$

### 2.4 Limiting Cases (Examination Gold)

| Regime | Condition | Simplified Equation | Physical Meaning |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Low overpotential** | $\vert \eta \vert \ll RT/F \approx 25.7\,\text{mV}$ | $j \approx j_0 \dfrac{F(\alpha_a + \alpha_c)\eta}{RT}$ | Linear (ohmic-like) kinetics near equilibrium |
| **High anodic overpotential** | $\eta \gg RT/\alpha_a F$ | $j \approx j_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$ | Anodic branch dominates |
| **High cathodic overpotential** | $\vert \eta \vert \gg RT/\alpha_c F$ | $j \approx -j_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)$ | Cathodic branch dominates |

### 2.5 Tafel Equation — The High-Overpotential Approximation

Taking the logarithm of the high-$\eta$ branch:

$$\eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha F}\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) = a + b\log_{10}(j)$$

with the **Tafel slope** $b$ (in V/decade):

$$b = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}$$

At $T = 353\,\text{K}$ (80 °C, typical PEMFC operating temperature) and $\alpha = 0.5$:

$$b = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{0.5 \times 96485} \approx 0.140\,\text{V/decade}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:**
> For ORR on Pt/C in PEMFC at 80 °C, the experimentally observed cathodic Tafel slope is **\mathbf{\approx 70 - 75\,mV/decade}** at low current density, transitioning to **$\approx 120\,\text{mV/decade}$** at high current density — a classic KTU viva question.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete cheat-sheet** of equations that examiners can ask you to *state, derive, or apply* in PEPOT743 Module 2. Memorise every row.

| # | Equation | Symbol Meanings | Typical Numerical Value / Unit |
|---|----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Butler-Volmer | $j,\;j_0,\;\alpha,\;\eta,\;F,\;R,\;T$ | See derivation in §3 |
| 2 | Tafel form | $\eta = a + b\log j$ | $b \approx 70 - 120\,\text{mV/dec}$ |
| 3 | Tafel slope | $b = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}$ | At 80 °C, $\alpha=0.5 \Rightarrow b \approx 140\,\text{mV/dec}$ |
| 4 | Exchange current density — Pt HOR | $j_0 \approx 10^{-3}$ – $10^{-1}\,\text{A/cm}^2_{\text{Pt}}$ | Fast reaction |
| 5 | Exchange current density — Pt ORR | $j_0 \approx 10^{-9}$ – $10^{-10}\,\text{A/cm}^2_{\text{Pt}}$ | Slow reaction (problem!) |
| 6 | Nernst equation | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{a_{\text{ox}}}{a_{\text{red}}}\right)$ | Used for equilibrium potential |
| 7 | Nernst voltage for H₂/O₂ cell | $E_{\text{cell}} = 1.229 - 0.000846\,(T-298) + \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$ | $T$ in Kelvin, $P$ in atm |
| 8 | Total polarisation loss | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{thermo}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$ | Sum of three losses |
| 9 | Activation loss (Tafel) | $\eta_{\text{act}} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{j}{j_0}\right)$ | mV at typical $j$ |
| 10 | Ohmic loss (Ohm's law in electrolyte) | $\eta_{\text{ohm}} = j \cdot ASR$ | ASR in $\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$ |
| 11 | ECSA (Electrochemically Active Surface Area) | $\text{ECSA} = \dfrac{Q_{H-\text{des}}}{210\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2 \cdot m_{\text{Pt}}}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{g}$ Pt |
| 12 | Pt utilisation | $U_{\text{Pt}} = \dfrac{\text{ECSA}_{\text{real}}}{\text{ECSA}_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | Target $\geq 70\,\%$ |
| 13 | Faradaic efficiency | $\eta_F = \dfrac{I_{\text{actual}}}{I_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | Should be $\approx 100\,\%$ |
| 14 | Overpotential definition | $\eta = E_{\text{electrode}} - E_{\text{eq}}$ | Sign convention: cathodic $\eta < 0$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electrode design principles derived here are **directly applied** in:

- 🚗 **Automotive PEMFC stacks** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO) — ultra-low Pt loading targets.
- 🔋 **Unitised Regenerative Fuel Cells (URFC)** — same electrode must run both fuel-cell and electrolyser modes.
- ✈️ **Aerospace primary PEMFC** — high current-density electrodes for compact stacks.
- ⚡ **PEM water electrolysers** — IrO₂/RuO₂ anode (oxygen evolution), Pt/C cathode (hydrogen evolution).
- 🧪 **CO₂ reduction electrolysers** — Cu-based catalyst electrodes for CO₂ → hydrocarbons.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Butler-Volmer Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 2. Examiners almost always award full marks if you write each numbered step clearly with annotation.

**Starting premise — Arrhenius-type activated kinetics for a single elementary electron-transfer step:**

The forward (reduction) and reverse (oxidation) reactions at an electrode are:

$$\text{Ox} + ne^- \;\underset{k_a}{\overset{k_c}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; \text{Red}$$

**Step 1 — Write the elementary rate constants using activation energies.**

At equilibrium, the free-energy barrier is symmetric. When an overpotential $\eta$ is applied, the cathodic barrier is *lowered* and the anodic barrier is *raised* by a fraction $\alpha$ of the electrical energy:

$$k_c = k^0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F (E - E_{\text{eq}})}{RT}\right)$$

$$k_a = k^0 \exp\!\left(+\frac{\alpha_a F (E - E_{\text{eq}})}{RT}\right)$$

where $\alpha_c + \alpha_a = 1$ (symmetry factor sum rule) and $k^0$ is the standard heterogeneous rate constant $[\text{cm/s}]$.

**Step 2 — Express partial current densities via Faraday's first law.**

The cathodic (reduction) partial current density is proportional to the surface concentration of the oxidised species times $k_c$:

$$j_c = -F k_c C_{\text{Ox}}(0)$$

The anodic (oxidation) partial current density is:

$$j_a = +F k_a C_{\text{Red}}(0)$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium, the net current is zero, but each partial current equals the exchange current density $j_0$.**

By definition at $E = E_{\text{eq}}$:

$$j_0 = F k^0 C_{\text{Ox}}(0) = F k^0 C_{\text{Red}}(0)$$

This is the **fundamental kinetic benchmark** of the electrode.

**Step 4 — Introduce the overpotential $\eta \equiv E - E_{\text{eq}}$.**

Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$j_c = -j_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

$$j_a = +j_0 \exp\!\left(+\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Net current density is the algebraic sum (anodic minus cathodic, by sign convention).**

$$j = j_a - j_c = j_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - j_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $j_0$ to obtain the canonical Butler-Volmer equation.**

$$\boxed{\;j = j_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]\;}$$

This completes the derivation. Every term has now been *introduced from physical first principles*.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Tafel Equation (High-Overpotential Limit)

**Step 1 — Apply the high-anodic-overpotential condition.**

When $\eta \gg \dfrac{RT}{\alpha_a F}$ (typically $> 50\,\text{mV}$), the first exponential in the Butler-Volmer equation dominates the second (the cathodic branch becomes negligible):

$$j \approx j_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) = \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}$$

**Step 3 — Solve explicitly for $\eta$.**

$$\eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha_a F}\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to base-10 logarithm and define the Tafel slope.**

Using $\ln(x) = 2.303\,\log_{10}(x)$:

$$\eta = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$$

Define the **Tafel slope** $b \equiv \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F}$ and the **Tafel intercept** $a \equiv -\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F}\log_{10}(j_0)$:

$$\boxed{\;\eta = a + b\,\log_{10}(j)\;}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration at $T = 353\,\text{K}$ and $\alpha_a = 0.5$.**

$$b = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{0.5 \times 96485}$$

Let me expand this multiplication explicitly:

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353 = 2.303 \times 2934.802 = 6759.85$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.5 \times 96485 = 48242.5$$

$$b = \frac{6759.85}{48242.5} \approx 0.1401\,\text{V/decade} = 140.1\,\text{mV/decade}$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation.** A Tafel slope of $140\,\text{mV/dec}$ means **for every 10-fold increase in current density, the activation overpotential grows by 140 mV**. A *smaller* Tafel slope means a *better* electrocatalyst.

### 3.3 Derivation of ECSA from CV Integration

The Electrochemically Active Surface Area is measured via **Hydrogen Underpotential Deposition (H-UPD)** on Pt:

$$\text{ECSA}\;[\text{m}^2/\text{g}] = \frac{Q_{H-\text{des}}\;[\text{C}]}{210\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2_{\text{Pt}} \times m_{\text{Pt}}\;[\text{g}]}$$

**Step 1 — Integrate the H-desorption peak from a cyclic voltammogram** to obtain the charge $Q_{H-\text{des}}$ (after subtracting the double-layer charging baseline).

**Step 2 — Divide by the standard charge density** for a full monolayer of H on polycrystalline Pt: **$q^* = 210\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2_{\text{Pt}}$**.

**Step 3 — Divide by the Pt mass loading** $m_{\text{Pt}}$ to normalise per gram.

**Worked numerical example (KTU-style):**

Suppose $Q_{H-\text{des}} = 12.6\,\text{mC}$, electrode geometric area $= 1\,\text{cm}^2$, Pt loading $= 0.2\,\text{mg/cm}^2$.

$$\text{ECSA} = \frac{12.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{C}}{210 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{C/cm}^2 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{g}}$$

$$= \frac{12.6 \times 10^{-3}}{4.2 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.0 \times 10^{5}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{g} = 30\,\text{m}^2/\text{g}$$

This is a healthy value — it confirms good Pt dispersion.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Anode vs Cathode Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is high-yield for KTU Module-2 questions.** Examiners often ask *"Compare anode and cathode requirements in a PEMFC."* Use the matrix below as your answer skeleton.

| Parameter | **Anode (HOR side)** | **Cathode (ORR side)** |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Half-reaction | $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ | $\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ |
| Standard potential | $0\,\text{V vs SHE}$ | $+1.229\,\text{V vs SHE}$ |
| Reaction kinetics | **Fast** — Pt is excellent | **Sluggish** — main bottleneck |
| Exchange current density $j_0$ | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-1}\,\text{A/cm}^2$ | $10^{-9}$ – $10^{-10}\,\text{A/cm}^2$ |
| Typical Pt loading | $0.05$ – $0.10\,\text{mg/cm}^2$ | $0.20$ – $0.30\,\text{mg/cm}^2$ |
| Catalyst of choice | Pt/C, Pt-Ru/C (if CO present) | Pt/C, Pt-Co, Pt-Ni, Pt-Fe (alloys) |
| Product | Protons (delivered to membrane) | Water (must be removed) |
| Water management | Dry | Must prevent flooding |
| Dominant loss | CO poisoning (if reformate H₂) | Activation + mass-transport |

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Electrode Catalyst Choices

| Catalyst | Application | $j_0$ relative to Pt | Tafel slope (mV/dec) | Cost | Stability |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|----------------------|------|-----------|
| **Pt/C** | Reference (PEMFC) | $1\times$ | 70 – 120 | Very high | Good |
| **Pt-Ru/C** | Anode (CO tolerance) | $\sim 5\times$ HOR | 60 – 80 | Very high | Good |
| **Pt-Co/C** | Cathode (ORR) | $\sim 3\times$ ORR | 70 – 90 | High | Moderate (Co leaching) |
| **Pt-Ni/C** | Cathode (ORR) | $\sim 2$–$3\times$ ORR | 70 – 90 | High | Moderate |
| **Pt-Fe-N/C** | Cathode (ORR) | $\sim 4\times$ ORR | 80 – 100 | High | Low–Moderate |
| **Fe-N-C (M-N-C)** | Cathode (PEMFC, non-PGM) | $\sim 1\times$ ORR | 80 – 110 | Low | Poor (H₂O₂ attack) |
| **IrO₂ / RuO₂** | Anode (PEM electrolyser OER) | High OER | 40 – 60 | Very high | Good |

### 3.6 Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA) — The Integrated Product

The MEA is the **integrated laminate** that brings membrane and electrodes together. The hot-pressing sequence is:

$$\text{MEA} = \underbrace{\text{Anode GDL} + \text{Anode CL}}_{\text{Anode electrode}} + \underbrace{\text{Membrane (Nafion)}}_{\text{Electrolyte}} + \underbrace{\text{Cathode CL} + \text{Cathode GDL}}_{\text{Cathode electrode}}$$

**Typical hot-press parameters (KTU lab data):**

$$T_{\text{press}} = 130 - 140\,°\text{C} \quad ; \quad P_{\text{press}} = 50 - 100\,\text{kg/cm}^2 \quad ; \quad t_{\text{press}} = 90 - 180\,\text{s}$$

A **decoupled electrode (DE)** or **CCM (Catalyst-Coated Membrane)** approach is preferred industrially because it places the ionomer–catalyst interface *directly* on the membrane — this minimises proton-transport resistance between electrode and membrane.
