## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Q2(a)]**
*Define the Triple Phase Boundary (TPB) in a fuel cell electrode. Why is the maximisation of TPB length a central design goal?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Triple Phase Boundary (TPB)** is the microscopic, three-dimensional locus within the catalyst layer of a fuel cell electrode where the **three essential phases** of the electrochemical reaction coexist simultaneously:

1. The **electron-conducting phase** — Pt nanoparticles supported on carbon black.
2. The **proton-conducting phase** — Nafion ionomer (or analogous electrolyte).
3. The **reactant-gas-supplying phase** — open mesopores delivering H₂ or O₂.

Only at the TPB can an electron, a proton, and a reactant molecule simultaneously meet to participate in the half-reaction. TPB is therefore the **effective reaction zone** of the electrode. **Maximisation of TPB length per unit volume** is a central design goal because the electrochemical reaction rate per unit geometric area is **directly proportional** to the active TPB length — a longer TPB yields higher current density at a given overpotential. This is achieved by:

- Using **nano-sized Pt particles** (~3 nm) to increase surface area.
- Optimising the **ionomer-to-catalyst ratio** (typically 30:40:30 vol %) so that ionomer is above its percolation threshold yet not blocking pores.
- Ensuring **uniform dispersion** of Pt on the carbon support.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for three phases, 1 mark for design-strategy explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1(c)]**
*With a neat sketch, describe the structure of a Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA) used in a PEMFC.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA)** is the heart of a PEMFC. It is a five-layer laminate produced by hot-pressing anode and cathode gas-diffusion electrodes onto a proton-exchange membrane (typically Nafion 117 or Nafion 212).

**Five-layer structure (centre → outward):**

1. **Anode Catalyst Layer** (5 – 30 µm) — Pt/C + Nafion ionomer.
2. **Anode Microporous Layer** (10 – 50 µm) — Carbon black + PTFE.
3. **Anode Gas Diffusion Layer** (190 – 400 µm) — Carbon paper / cloth (e.g., Toray TGP-H).
4. **Proton Exchange Membrane** (50 – 175 µm) — Nafion, conducts H⁺ from anode to cathode.
5. **Cathode Catalyst Layer** (5 – 30 µm) — Pt/C + Nafion (higher Pt loading).
6. **Cathode Microporous Layer** (10 – 50 µm).
7. **Cathode Gas Diffusion Layer** (190 – 400 µm).

A **sketch (to be drawn)** would show the symmetric sandwich: GDL — MPL — CL — Membrane — CL — MPL — GDL, with arrows showing H₂ entry from one side, O₂/air entry from the other, and H⁺ migration across the membrane.

**Standard hot-press conditions:** $T = 130 - 140\,°\text{C},\; P = 50 - 100\,\text{kg/cm}^2,\; t = 90 - 180\,\text{s}$.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for naming layers in correct order, 1 mark for materials of each layer, 1 mark for hot-press parameters.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Model PEPOT743 Module 2]**

**(a) Derive the Butler-Volmer equation starting from the Arrhenius-type rate expression. Clearly define all symbols and state the high-overpotential simplification that leads to the Tafel equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the elementary one-electron transfer step.**

$$\text{Ox} + e^- \;\underset{k_a}{\overset{k_c}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; \text{Red}$$

**Step 2 — Apply potential-dependent activation to write rate constants.**

At the equilibrium potential $E_{\text{eq}}$, the activation barrier is symmetric with height $\Delta G^{\ddagger,0}$. When an overpotential $\eta = E - E_{\text{eq}}$ is applied, the barrier is split asymmetrically:

$$\Delta G^{\ddagger}_c = \Delta G^{\ddagger,0} - \alpha_c F \eta \quad \text{(cathodic barrier lowered)}$$

$$\Delta G^{\ddagger}_a = \Delta G^{\ddagger,0} + \alpha_a F \eta \quad \text{(anodic barrier raised)}$$

where $\alpha_a + \alpha_c = 1$ (symmetry factor relation).

Therefore:

$$k_c = k^0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right), \qquad k_a = k^0 \exp\!\left(+\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Express partial current densities via Faraday's law.**

$$j_c = -F k_c C_{\text{Ox}}(0), \qquad j_a = +F k_a C_{\text{Red}}(0)$$

**Step 4 — Define exchange current density at equilibrium.**

$$j_0 = F k^0 C_{\text{Ox}}(0) = F k^0 C_{\text{Red}}(0)$$

**Step 5 — Net current = anodic minus cathodic partial currents.**

$$j = j_a - j_c = j_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)\right] \quad \quad \text{...(Butler-Volmer)}$$

**Step 6 — Apply high-overpotential limit.**

When $\vert \eta \vert \gg RT/\alpha F$ (typically $> 50\,\text{mV}$ at 80 °C), one exponential dominates:

$$j \approx j_0 \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}\right) \quad \text{(assuming cathodic ORR)}$$

**Step 7 — Take logarithm to obtain the Tafel equation.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) = \frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT} \;\Rightarrow\; \eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha F}\ln\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right) = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$$

Defining the **Tafel slope** $b = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}$ and intercept $a = -b\log_{10}(j_0)$:

$$\boxed{\;\eta = a + b\,\log_{10}(j)\;}$$

*(Marks split: 1 for each of steps 1-5; 1 for step 6; 1 for step 7.)*

---

**(b) Discuss the various components of a fuel cell electrode and the materials used for each. Explain why cathode Pt loading is higher than anode Pt loading in a PEMFC. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A PEMFC electrode is a three-layer functional composite:

**1. Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL):** Distributes reactant gas uniformly over the active area, removes product water, conducts electrons laterally to the bipolar plate, and provides mechanical strength. **Materials:** Carbon paper (Toray TGP-H-060, SGL Sigracet) or carbon cloth, typically impregnated with PTFE (5 – 30 wt %) for hydrophobicity.

**2. Microporous Layer (MPL):** Bridges the coarse GDL and the fine catalyst layer. Reduces interfacial electrical contact resistance and improves water management. **Materials:** Carbon black (Vulcan XC-72) mixed with PTFE binder (20 – 40 wt %), coated onto GDL.

**3. Catalyst Layer (CL):** The actual reaction site containing Pt nanoparticles on carbon support, blended with Nafion ionomer to provide protonic connectivity. **Materials:** Pt (3 – 5 nm particles) on carbon black (Vulcan XC-72R or Ketjenblack), 20 – 40 wt % Pt, mixed with Nafion ionomer at I/C ratio ~0.6 – 1.0.

**Why cathode Pt loading is higher:**

(a) **Kinetic asymmetry:** The Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) at the cathode is intrinsically $\sim 10^6$ times **slower** than the Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction (HOR) at the anode. Exchange current density for ORR is $j_0 \approx 10^{-9}\,\text{A/cm}^2_{\text{Pt}}$, while for HOR it is $j_0 \approx 10^{-3}\,\text{A/cm}^2_{\text{Pt}}$.

(b) **To match current densities** (since the same current must flow through both electrodes per cell), the cathode requires proportionally more Pt sites — hence the **asymmetric loading** (typically 0.2 – 0.3 mg Pt/cm² at cathode vs 0.05 – 0.10 mg Pt/cm² at anode).

(c) **Higher activation overpotential** at cathode demands more catalytic sites to keep $\eta_{\text{act}}$ within acceptable limits (e.g., $< 50\,\text{mV}$ at $1\,\text{A/cm}^2$).

(d) **Pt-alloy strategies** (Pt-Co, Pt-Ni, Pt-Fe, core-shell Pt-Ni) are deployed at the cathode specifically to enhance ORR activity, allowing reduced total Pt loading toward the **DOE 2025 target of 0.10 mg Pt/cm² total**.

*(Marks split: 2 for layer description, 1 for materials, 2 for kinetic argument with $j_0$ values, 1 for asymmetric loading justification, 1 for Pt-alloy note.)*

---

### **QUESTION B — [Alternative 14-Mark Choice — Module 2]**

**(a) Explain the Tafel equation and its significance in electrocatalyst evaluation. Calculate the Tafel slope for an ORR catalyst at 80 °C assuming $\alpha_c = 0.5$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Significance of the Tafel equation in electrocatalysis:**

The Tafel equation $\eta = a + b\log_{10}(j)$ is the cornerstone diagnostic tool in fuel-cell electrode research. Its importance is **four-fold**:

(i) **Tafel slope $b$** identifies the **rate-determining step (RDS)**. For ORR on Pt, $b \approx 60\,\text{mV/dec}$ suggests the first electron transfer is RDS; $b \approx 120\,\text{mV/dec}$ suggests a chemical step after electron transfer is RDS.

(ii) **Exchange current density $j_0$** (extracted as the intercept of the linear Tafel plot at $\eta = 0$) measures **intrinsic catalytic activity**. A higher $j_0$ at fixed $b$ indicates a superior catalyst.

(iii) **Comparison metric** — Tafel plots of different catalysts on a single graph allow rapid visual ranking.

(iv) **Diagnostic of mass-transport interference** — deviation from linearity at high $j$ signals onset of concentration polarisation.

**Calculation of Tafel slope at $T = 353\,\text{K}$, $\alpha_c = 0.5$:**

$$b = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_c F}$$

Substituting:

- $R = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$
- $T = 353\,\text{K}$
- $\alpha_c = 0.5$
- $F = 96485\,\text{C/mol}$

$$b = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 353}{0.5 \times 96485}$$

**Step-by-step arithmetic:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.303 \times 8.314 = 19.147$$
$$19.147 \times 353 = 6759.0$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 0.5 \times 96485 = 48242.5$$
$$b = \frac{6759.0}{48242.5} = 0.1401\,\text{V/decade} = 140.1\,\text{mV/decade}$$

**Interpretation:** A Tafel slope of $\approx 140\,\text{mV/decade}$ for a single-electron-transfer RDS at the cathode is the *theoretical limit*. Experimentally observed values are $70$–$120\,\text{mV/decade}$ on Pt-based catalysts, reflecting complex multi-step ORR kinetics.

*(Marks split: 2 for significance points, 2 for formula, 2 for calculation, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

**(b) With the help of a polarisation curve, explain the three major voltage losses in a PEMFC and discuss how electrode design addresses each loss. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The operating voltage of a PEMFC is:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{thermo}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$

The **polarisation curve** (voltage $V$ vs current density $j$) shows three distinct loss regions:

**Region 1 — Activation Loss (low $j$, $0$–$100\,\text{mA/cm}^2$):**
A sharp initial voltage drop caused by the **sluggish ORR kinetics** at the cathode.
**Electrode-design solution:** Increase ECSA through Pt nanoparticle dispersion, use Pt-alloy catalysts (Pt-Co, Pt-Ni), develop Pt-skin or core-shell structures, increase ionomer content for better proton access, ensure uniform catalyst distribution to maximise TPB.

**Region 2 — Ohmic Loss (middle $j$, $100$–$800\,\text{mA/cm}^2$):**
A linear voltage drop governed by $V_{\text{ohm}} = j \times \text{ASR}$, where ASR (area-specific resistance) includes membrane resistance, contact resistance, and electrode in-plane resistance.
**Electrode-design solution:** Use thinner, high-conductivity membranes (Nafion 211 ≈ 25 µm instead of Nafion 117 ≈ 175 µm); minimise contact resistance by optimising hot-press pressure; ensure proper catalyst-ionomer distribution to avoid "dead zones" of low proton conductivity.

**Region 3 — Concentration Loss (high $j$, $>800\,\text{mA/cm}^2$):**
A sudden voltage fall-off as **reactant mass-transport** becomes rate-limiting — typically O₂ diffusion through the GDL and liquid water flooding of pores.
**Electrode-design solution:** Optimise GDL porosity (typically 70 – 80 %) and PTFE content (5 – 30 wt %); use MPL to reduce pore-flooding; tune channel/land ratio of bipolar plate; ensure adequate gas stoichiometry (air ≥ 2.0, H₂ ≥ 1.2); design interdigitated flow fields for forced convection at high current density.

**Summary linkage table:**

| Loss Region | Dominant Cause | Electrode Design Response |
|-------------|----------------|---------------------------|
| Activation | Slow ORR kinetics | Pt-alloy catalysts, high ECSA, optimised ionomer |
| Ohmic | Membrane + contact resistance | Thin membrane, optimised hot-pressing |
| Concentration | Mass-transport limitation of O₂ / H₂O | GDL/MPL porosity tuning, flow-field design |

*(Marks split: 1 for $V_{\text{cell}}$ equation, 2 for three regions described, 3 for electrode-design mitigation per region, 1 for summary table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors** — Cathodic overpotential is **negative** (per the $E - E_{\text{eq}}$ definition); many students confuse signs in the Butler-Volmer equation and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Tafel slope direction** — A *smaller* Tafel slope means a *better* catalyst (less overpotential per decade of current), not larger.
> 3. **Wrong units for ECSA** — Use **m²/g Pt**, not cm²/g Pt. The conversion factor is $10^4$.
> 4. **Forgetting the symmetry factor sum rule** — Always state $\alpha_a + \alpha_c = 1$ in derivations.
> 5. **In hot-press parameters** — Temperature is **130–140 °C**, not 100 °C (which damages Nafion).
> 6. **Equating GDL and MPL** — They have distinct functions; MPL has finer pores and is denser than GDL.
> 7. **Wrong Butler-Volmer form in ORR** — Since ORR is cathodic, students sometimes flip the exponentials; memorise the **standard form given in §3.1**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. It is your Module-2 last-day revision sheet.**

✅ **Core definitions to memorise verbatim:**
- Electrode
- Catalyst Layer (CL)
- Microporous Layer (MPL)
- Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)
- Triple Phase Boundary (TPB)
- Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA)
- Electrochemically Active Surface Area (ECSA)
- Exchange Current Density ($j_0$)
- Tafel slope ($b$)
- Activation overpotential ($\eta_{\text{act}}$)

✅ **Must-know equations (derivable from first principles):**
- Butler-Volmer equation (with full derivation)
- Tafel equation (high-$\eta$ limit)
- Tafel slope formula $b = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}$
- Nernst equation
- ECSA formula
- $V_{\text{cell}}$ polarisation equation

✅ **Numerical constants to memorise:**
- $F = 96485\,\text{C/mol}$
- $R = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$
- $RT/F \approx 25.7\,\text{mV}$ at 25 °C
- $RT/F \approx 30\,\text{mV}$ at 80 °C (PEMFC operating)
- Standard H₂/O₂ cell potential = $1.229\,\text{V}$
- Pt charge density for H-UPD = $210\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2$

✅ **Design parameters (the "5 W's" of electrodes):**
- **What material** — Pt/C, Pt-alloys, M-N-C (non-PGM)
- **What loading** — 0.05 (anode) – 0.3 (cathode) mg Pt/cm²
- **What thickness** — CL: 5 – 30 µm; MPL: 10 – 50 µm; GDL: 190 – 400 µm
- **What ionomer ratio** — I/C ≈ 0.6 – 1.0; ionomer vol fraction ≈ 30 %
- **What porosity** — GDL ≈ 70 – 80 %; CL ≈ 30 – 50 %

✅ **Why cathode ≠ anode (asymmetry reasoning):**
- ORR is $10^6 \times$ slower than HOR → $j_0$ at cathode is much smaller.
- Hence cathode needs higher Pt loading and Pt-alloy engineering.

✅ **Key manufacturing process:**
- Hot-press MEA at $130 - 140\,°\text{C},\;50 - 100\,\text{kg/cm}^2,\;90 - 180\,\text{s}$.

✅ **Typical experimentally observed Tafel slopes (commit to memory):**
- ORR on Pt/C at 80 °C: **70 – 75 mV/dec** (low $j$) → **120 mV/dec** (high $j$)
- HOR on Pt/C: **30 mV/dec** at low $j$
- OER on IrO₂: **40 – 60 mV/dec**

✅ **Layer thickness mnemonic — "30-50-400":**
- CL ~ 30 µm
- MPL ~ 50 µm
- GDL ~ 400 µm
*(Order: outermost-innermost from bipolar plate to membrane.)*

✅ **Percolation threshold reminder:** Iomer volume fraction in CL must exceed ~30 % to form a continuous proton-conducting network — below this, the CL behaves like an electronic conductor only and the reaction collapses.

✅ **Cross-link to Module 3:** The polymer-electrolyte membrane you study next (Nafion structure, equivalent weight, water uptake) determines the **proton conductivity** that controls the **ohmic loss** in the polarisation curve — these two modules are tightly coupled in the KTU exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aqueous
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Aqueous ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **PEPOT743 – Polymers & Fuel Cell Technology (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**, the topic **"Aqueous"** refers to the family of **Aqueous Electrolyte Fuel Cells (AEFCs)** — electrochemical energy conversion devices in which the ion-conducting medium separating the anode and cathode half-cells is a **liquid-phase aqueous electrolyte** (an alkaline solution such as KOH, or an acid such as $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$), as opposed to a solid polymer membrane or a molten salt/molten carbonate. The two principal categories under this classification are: 1. **Alkaline Fuel Cells (AFC)** — using an aqueous solution of **potassium hydroxide (KOH)** as the electrolyte (typically 30–85 wt%). 2. **Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells (PAFC)** — using **concentrated liquid phosphoric acid ($\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$)** immobilized in a porous silicon carbide (SiC) matrix. Both belong to the **"Low-Temperature Fuel Cell"** family (operating between $\mathbf{60^{\circ}\text{C}}$ and $\mathbf{220^{\circ}\text{C}}$), where water exists in its **liquid aqueous phase** inside the electrolyte — hence the umbrella designation "Aqueous Fuel Cells." > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Anchor (PEPOT743, Module 3):** The classification of fuel cells based on the **electrolyte state (aqueous vs. non-aqueous / molten / solid)** is a direct KTU examination focus. Students must explicitly identify whether an electrolyte is aqueous (liquid water present at operating temperature), molten (e.g., MCFC at $650^{\circ}\text{C}$), or solid (e.g., SOFC at $800\text{–}1000^{\circ}\text{C}$). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Oceans Separated by a Sponge Wall" Intuition Imagine two glass aquariums placed side by side, but instead of being open, they are separated by a **sponge wall soaked in salt water**: - The **left aquarium (anode side)** contains bubbles of hydrogen gas ($\text{H}_2$) being gently bubbled in. - The **right aquarium (cathode side)** contains bubbles of oxygen (or air) being bubbled in. - The **sponge wall (aqueous electrolyte)** allows only **positive ions (cations like $\text{K}^+$, $\text{H}^+$, or $\text{OH}^-$)** to drift through — but it **blocks electrons and neutral gases**. The hydrogen atoms, upon touching the sponge wall, split apart: - The **protons ($\text{H}^+$)** swim through the wet sponge toward the oxygen side. - The **electrons ($e^-$)**, being too big/charged negatively to pass through, are forced to take the **long detour through an external wire** — and on that wire journey they power a light bulb. This is, in essence, **how every aqueous fuel cell works**: a wet ionic highway ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$-based electrolyte) coupled to an external electronic detour (the load). --- ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall) | Parameter | Symbol | Value / Range | |---|---|---| | Faraday constant | $F$ | $\mathbf{96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}}$ | | Universal gas constant | $R$ | $\mathbf{8.314\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}}$ | | Standard temperature & pressure | STP | $\mathbf{298.15\ \text{K},\ 1\ \text{atm}}$ | | Standard cell EMF ($\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ in acid) | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | $\mathbf{1.229\ \text{V}}$ | | Standard cell EMF ($\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ in base) | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell, base}}$ | $\mathbf{1.229\ \text{V}}$ (same magnitude) | | KOH electrolyte concentration (AFC) | — | $\mathbf{30\ \text{to}\ 85\ \text{wt}\%}$ | | $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ concentration (PAFC) | — | $\mathbf{\sim 100\ \text{wt}\%}$ (concentrated) | | Operating T (AFC) | $T_{\text{op}}$ | $\mathbf{60\ \text{to}\ 250^{\circ}\text{C}}$ | | Operating T (PAFC) | $T_{\text{op}}$ | $\mathbf{150\ \text{to}\ 220^{\circ}\text{C}}$ | | Charge carrier in AFC | — | $\mathbf{OH^-}$ (hydroxide ion) | | Charge carrier in PAFC | — | $\mathbf{H^+}$ (proton) | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic Tip for KTU:** "**A**queous → **A**lkaline **O**r **A**cid" — the two main families are AFC (Alkaline, $\text{OH}^-$ carrier) and PAFC (Phosphoric Acid, $\text{H}^+$ carrier). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why "Aqueous" Matters in Fuel Cell Engineering A fuel cell is classified as **aqueous** purely based on the **thermodynamic phase of its electrolyte at operating temperature**. The decision to use an aqueous electrolyte has cascading consequences across the entire cell architecture: #### 2.1.1 Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC) — Step-by-Step Operating Logic 1. **Hydrogen Fuel Supply (Anode Inlet):** Pure $\text{H}_2$ (often derived from electrolysis or reformed hydrocarbons) is humidified and fed to the anode flow field. 2. **Anode Half-Reaction (HOR — Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction) in Alkaline Medium:** - The hydroxide ion ($\text{OH}^-$) reacts with hydrogen to produce water and releases electrons. 3. **Ionic Conduction:** $\text{OH}^-$ ions migrate through the aqueous KOH electrolyte (or matrix-supported liquid KOH) from cathode to anode. 4. **Cathode Half-Reaction (ORR — Oxygen Reduction Reaction) in Alkaline Medium:** - Oxygen from air combines with water and incoming electrons to regenerate $\text{OH}^-$. 5. **Water Management Challenge:** Water is **produced at the anode** in AFC — so anode flooding is a real risk; AFCs require careful water removal from the anode exhaust. 6. **Electron Path:** Electrons travel externally from anode → load → cathode, generating usable DC electrical work. #### 2.1.2 Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC) — Step-by-Step Operating Logic 1. **Hydrogen Supply (Anode):** Reformed natural gas / methanol / biogas (after CO cleanup to <10 ppm) provides $\text{H}_2$-rich feed. 2. **Anode HOR in Acidic Medium:** $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ 3. **Ionic Conduction:** $\text{H}^+$ ions (protons) migrate through the concentrated $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ electrolyte from anode to cathode. 4. **Cathode ORR in Acidic Medium:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ 5. **Water Management:** Water is **produced at the cathode** in PAFC — cathode flooding risk; product water must be removed as steam. 6. **Electrolyte Immobilization:** The liquid $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ is held in a porous **SiC (silicon carbide) matrix** to prevent leakage and maintain mechanical integrity. --- ### 2.2 The Electrochemistry — Nernst, Butler–Volmer, and Tafel for Aqueous Cells #### 2.2.1 Thermodynamic Reversible Voltage (Nernst Equation) For the overall $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ reaction: $$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$$ $$\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$ The standard reversible EMF is: $$E^{\circ}_{\text{rev}} = \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} = \frac{237{,}200\ \text{J mol}^{-1}}{2 \times 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}} = \mathbf{1.229\ \text{V}}$$ The **Nernst equation** gives the operating (reversible) voltage under non-standard partial pressures: $$E_{\text{rev}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{rev}} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\ P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$$ For liquid water product ($a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 1$): $$E_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(T)}{2(96{,}485)}\ln\left(P_{\text{H}_2}\, P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}\right)\ \text{V}$$ If the water product is vapor (e.g., at $T > 100^{\circ}\text{C}$), $a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ becomes the partial pressure ratio $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}/P^{\circ}$, lowering $E_{\text{rev}}$. #### 2.2.2 Activation Overpotential (Butler–Volmer Equation) The actual cell voltage drops below $E_{\text{rev}}$ due to kinetic losses at the electrodes: $$\eta_{\text{act}} = \frac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\left(\frac{j}{j_0}\right)$$ where: - $\eta_{\text{act}}$ = activation overpotential (V) - $\alpha$ = charge-transfer coefficient (typically $\mathbf{0.5}$ for symmetric reactions) - $j$ = actual current density ($\text{A cm}^{-2}$) - $j_0$ = exchange current density ($\text{A cm}^{-2}$) For high $j$ (Tafel approximation): $$\eta_{\text{act}} = a + b\log_{10}(j), \quad b = \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha n F}$$ For the **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** in aqueous media, $j_0$ is notoriously low ($\sim 10^{-10}\ \text{A cm}^{-2}$ on Pt in acid), making cathode activation loss the **dominant voltage loss** in both AFC and PAFC. #### 2.2.3 Ohmic (Resistance) Loss $$\eta_{\text{ohmic}} = j \cdot R_{\text{internal}}$$ The ionic resistance $R_{\text{int}}$ depends on electrolyte conductivity $\sigma$: $$\sigma_{\text{KOH}(30\%)} \approx \mathbf{0.55\ \text{S cm}^{-1}}\ \text{at } 80^{\circ}\text{C}$$ $$\sigma_{\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4(100\%)} \approx \mathbf{0.05\ \text{S cm}^{-1}}\ \text{at } 200^{\circ}\text{C}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The **lower conductivity of phosphoric acid** compared to KOH is precisely why PAFC operates at higher temperature ($200^{\circ}\text{C}$) to boost $\sigma$, while AFC can deliver reasonable currents even at $\sim 80^{\circ}\text{C}$. #### 2.2.4 Concentration (Mass-Transport) Loss $$\eta_{\text{conc}} = \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{j_L}{j_L - j}\right)$$ where $j_L$ = limiting current density. Aqueous cells suffer from **flooding** (liquid water blocking pore space), which drastically lowers $j_L$. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table) > **Note to Student:** This is the consolidated "cheat-sheet" for aqueous fuel cell numerical problems. | # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$ | Thermodynamic reversible voltage | $V$; $n=2$ for $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ | | 2 | $E_{\text{rev}} = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$ | Nernst open-circuit voltage | V | | 3 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ} = -237.2\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ | Gibbs free energy of reaction | $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ | | 4 | $\Delta H^{\circ} = -285.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ | Enthalpy change (HHV basis) | $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ | | 5 | $\eta_{\text{act}} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{j}{j_0}\right)$ | Butler–Volmer activation overpotential | V | | 6 | $\eta_{\text{act}} \approx a + b\log_{10}(j)$ | Tafel approximation (high $j$) | V | | 7 | $b = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha nF}$ | Tafel slope | $\text{V dec}^{-1}$ | | 8 | $\eta_{\text{ohmic}} = j\,R_{\text{int}}$ | Ohmic voltage loss | V | | 9 | $\sigma_{\text{ionic}}$ function of $T,\ c$ | Electrolyte conductivity | $\text{S cm}^{-1}$ | | 10 | $\eta_{\text{conc}} = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{j_L}{j_L-j}\right)$ | Mass-transport overpotential | V | | 11 | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{rev}} - \eta_{\text{act,a}} - \eta_{\text{act,c}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$ | Actual cell voltage | V | | 12 | $\varepsilon_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \dfrac{237.2}{285.8} = \mathbf{83\%}$ | Reversible (HHV) thermodynamic efficiency | dimensionless | | 13 | $P_{\text{cell}} = V_{\text{cell}} \times I$ | Electrical power output | W | | 14 | $\eta_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^{\circ}_{\text{rev}}} \times \varepsilon_{\text{rev}}$ | Practical cell efficiency | dimensionless | --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Why Aqueous Fuel Cells Are Used | |---|---| | **NASA Space Missions (Apollo, Shuttle)** | AFCs supplied by **Apollo (AFC)** and Space Shuttle; pure $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ reactants produce **drinking water** as a by-product. | | **Stationary Power (PAFC — 200 kW units)** | PureCell® by Doosan (formerly UTC Power); used in hospitals, hotels for combined heat & power (CHP). | | **Backup Power for Telecom Towers (AFC)** | AFCs (AFC Energy, UK) refueled with green hydrogen for off-grid backup. | | **Forklift / Material Handling (AFC)** | Nuvera, Plug Power — indoor-safe (no acidic mist), zero emissions. | | **Submarines (German Type 212A, U-31)** | Siemens AFC systems providing silent underwater propulsion. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From First-Principle Thermodynamics to the Nernst Equation for an Aqueous $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ Cell We will derive the **Nernst equation** rigorously from the Gibbs free energy change of the cell reaction, step by step. #### Step 1 — Write the overall cell reaction For an aqueous fuel cell operating on pure hydrogen and pure oxygen, with liquid water as the product: $$\text{H}_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$ The number of electrons transferred per mole of $\text{H}_2$ consumed is $n = 2$. #### Step 2 — Write the general expression for Gibbs free energy under non-standard conditions For any reaction $aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$$ where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** and $\Delta G^{\circ}$ is the standard Gibbs free energy change. For our fuel cell reaction: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln\left(\frac{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$ > **Conversion Logic:** Since $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is a pure liquid at standard state, $a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}=1$ at low T. At high T where it is vapor, $a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}/P^{\circ}$. #### Step 3 — Relate $\Delta G$ to electrical work The maximum non-expansion (electrical) work obtainable from a galvanic cell equals the **negative** of the Gibbs free energy change: $$W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G = nFE_{\text{cell}}$$ Therefore: $$E_{\text{cell}} = \frac{-\Delta G}{nF}$$ #### Step 4 — Combine Steps 2 and 3 $$nFE_{\text{cell}} = -\Delta G^{\circ} - RT\ln\left(\frac{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$ Divide both sides by $nF$: $$E_{\text{cell}} = \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$ #### Step 5 — Identify the standard cell potential By definition: $$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$$ So the equation becomes: $$E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$ #### Step 6 — Invert the logarithm to the more conventional form Using the identity $-\ln(x/y) = +\ln(y/x)$: $$\boxed{E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)}$$ This is the **Nernst Equation** for an aqueous $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ fuel cell. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Calculating Reversible Cell Voltage > **KTU-Style Problem:** A PAFC operates at $T = 473\ \text{K}\ (200^{\circ}\text{C})$ with $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1.5\ \text{atm}$, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 0.21\ \text{atm}$ (air-fed), and water leaves as vapor at $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 1\ \text{atm}$. Compute $E_{\text{rev}}$. #### Solution Walkthrough **Step A — Identify data:** $$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 1.229\ \text{V},\quad T = 473\ \text{K},\quad n = 2$$ **Step B — Activity of water (vapor product):** $$a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = \frac{P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{P^{\circ}} = \frac{1\ \text{atm}}{1\ \text{atm}} = 1$$ (Since $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ is at standard pressure, $a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}=1$ in this case. **Note:** this assumption is only valid at saturation; a more rigorous treatment uses $P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}^{\text{sat}}(473\ \text{K}) \approx 15.3\ \text{atm}$.) **Step C — Plug into the Nernst equation:** $$E_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 + \frac{(8.314)(473)}{2(96{,}485)}\ln\!\left(\frac{(1.5)\,(0.21)^{1/2}}{1}\right)$$ **Step D — Evaluate the pre-factor (RT/nF):** $$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{(8.314)(473)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \frac{3932.5}{192{,}970} = 0.02038\ \text{V}$$ **Step E — Evaluate the logarithm:** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{1.5 \times \sqrt{0.21}}{1}\right) = \ln(1.5 \times 0.4583) = \ln(0.6874) = -0.3749$$ **Step F — Combine:** $$E_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 + (0.02038)(-0.3749) = 1.229 - 0.00764$$ $$\boxed{E_{\text{rev}} \approx 1.221\ \text{V}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Interpretation:** The reversible voltage drops by only ~8 mV from the standard value of 1.229 V, because the operating conditions are close to standard. If pure $\text{O}_2$ at 1 atm were used (instead of air), the value would be slightly higher, ~1.244 V. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Efficiency Calculation > **Problem:** A PAFC stack delivers $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.70\ \text{V}$ at operating point. Calculate the practical cell efficiency using the HHV of hydrogen. **Given:** - $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.70\ \text{V}$ - $E^{\circ}_{\text{rev}} = 1.229\ \text{V}$ - $\Delta H^{\circ} = 285.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ (HHV) - $\Delta G^{\circ} = 237.2\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ **Step A — Voltage efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{volt}} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^{\circ}_{\text{rev}}} = \frac{0.70}{1.229} = 0.5695 = 56.95\%$$ **Step B — Thermodynamic (HHV) efficiency:** $$\varepsilon_{\text{HHV}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 0.8300 = 83.00\%$$ **Step C — Overall practical efficiency:** $$\eta_{\text{cell}} = \eta_{\text{volt}} \times \varepsilon_{\text{HHV}} = (0.5695)(0.8300) = 0.4727$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{cell}} \approx 47.3\%}$$ > **Engineering Comment:** A real PAFC station typically delivers **40–45% electrical efficiency** (LHV basis) and up to **85% total CHP efficiency** when waste heat is recovered. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of AFC vs. PAFC | Parameter | **Alkaline Fuel Cell (AFC)** | **Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC)** | |---|---|---| | **Electrolyte** | Aqueous KOH (30–85 wt%) | Concentrated $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ (~100 wt%) | | **Electrolyte state** | Liquid aqueous | Liquid aqueous (immobilized in SiC matrix) | | **Charge carrier** | $\text{OH}^-$ | $\text{H}^+$ | | **Operating temperature** | $60\text{–}250^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $150\text{–}220^{\circ}\text{C}$ | | **Anode reaction** | $\text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^-$ | $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ | | **Cathode reaction** | $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^-$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | | **Water produced at** | Anode | Cathode | | **Electrolyte conductivity (typical)** | $\sim 0.55\ \text{S cm}^{-1}$ at 80°C | $\sim 0.05\ \text{S cm}^{-1}$ at 200°C | | **CO tolerance** | Very poor ($\text{CO}_2$ poisoning from air) | Moderate (must clean $\text{CO}$ to <10 ppm) | | **$\text{CO}_2$ tolerance** | **Very poor** — forms $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$ precipitate | Good (no $\text{CO}_2$ reaction with acid) | | **Catalyst cost** | Low (Ag, Ni, or non-precious possible) | High (Pt mandatory) | | **Electrical efficiency** | 50–60% | 40–45% | | **Power range** | 1 kW – 250 kW (submarines, fork-lifts) | 50 kW – 1 MW (CHP stationary) | | **Commercial examples** | Apollo, Space Shuttle, Siemens submarines, AFC Energy | Doosan PureCell® 400 kW, Fuji FP-100i | | **Key advantage** | Fast ORR kinetics in alkaline media, low-cost catalysts | $\text{CO}_2$-tolerant (can use air directly) | | **Key disadvantage** | $\text{CO}_2$ sensitivity, electrolyte leakage | Low conductivity, expensive Pt catalyst | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Architecture of an Aqueous Fuel Cell Stack System ```mermaid flowchart TB H2Supply["H2 SUPPLY UNIT
Reformer or Tank"] AirSupply["AIR BLOWER UNIT
Filtered CO2 free"] HX1["HUMIDIFIER
H2 Side"] HX2["HUMIDIFIER
Air Side"] AnodeFlow["ANODE FLOW FIELD
Bipolar Plate"] CathodeFlow["CATHODE FLOW FIELD
Bipolar Plate"] AEL["AQUEOUS ELECTROLYTE LAYER
KOH or H3PO4 in SiC"] AnodeCat["ANODE CATALYST
Pt or Ag or Ni"] CathodeCat["CATHODE CATALYST
Pt or Ag"] CellStack["CELL STACK ASSEMBLY
Series n cells 1 to 50"] WatSep["WATER SEPARATOR
and HEAT EXCHANGER"] DCInv["DC to AC INVERTER"] Load["ELECTRICAL LOAD
Grid or Motor"] Recirc["ELECTROLYTE RECIRCULATION
PUMP and FILTER"] H2Supply --> HX1 --> AnodeFlow AirSupply --> HX2 --> CathodeFlow AnodeFlow --> AnodeCat CathodeFlow --> CathodeCat AnodeCat <--> AEL CathodeCat <--> AEL AnodeFlow --> CellStack CathodeFlow --> CellStack CellStack --> WatSep --> DCInv --> Load AEL -.->|liquid path| Recirc Recirc -.->|return| AEL ``` --- ### 4.2 Half-Cell Reaction Flow — AFC (Alkaline) vs. PAFC (Phosphoric Acid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AFC["ALKALINE FUEL CELL AFC"] A1["Anode
H2 + 2OH- yields 2H2O + 2e-"] A2["OH- ions travel cathode to anode"] A3["Cathode
0.5O2 + H2O + 2e- yields 2OH-"] A1 --> A2 --> A3 end subgraph PAFC["PHOSPHORIC ACID FUEL CELL PAFC"] P1["Anode
H2 yields 2H+ + 2e-"] P2["H+ ions travel anode to cathode"] P3["Cathode
0.5O2 + 2H+ + 2e- yields H2O"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 end ``` --- ### 4.3 Voltage Loss Breakdown (Polarization Curve Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Erev = 1.229 V
Thermodynamic Limit"] B["Activation Loss
eta_act 0 to 0.4 V"] C["Ohmic Loss
eta_ohmic 0 to 0.1 V"] D["Concentration Loss
eta_conc 0 to 0.3 V"] E["Vcell = 0.6 to 0.8 V
Practical Operating Point"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the polarization curve:** A KTU examiner may ask you to identify the *three* distinct voltage loss regions on a current–voltage (or current–power density) plot. They correspond to: (1) **Activation** (steep drop at low $j$), (2) **Ohmic** (linear drop in mid $j$), (3) **Concentration** (sharp drop at high $j$). --- ### 4.4 Comparative Layered Cell Architecture (AFC vs. PAFC) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AFCstack["AFC SINGLE CELL STACK"] A1["Anode Bipolar Plate
Nickel plated"] A2["Anode Catalyst Layer
Pt or Raney Ni"] A3["Aqueous KOH
in asbestos or PVA matrix"] A4["Cathode Catalyst Layer
Pt or Ag"] A5["Cathode Bipolar Plate
Nickel plated"] A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 end subgraph PAFCstack["PAFC SINGLE CELL STACK"] P1["Anode Bipolar Plate
Graphite composite"] P2["Anode Catalyst
Pt on C"] P3["SiC Matrix with
100% H3PO4"] P4["Cathode Catalyst
Pt on C"] P5["Cathode Bipolar Plate
Graphite composite"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Q1. [3 Marks] — Define an aqueous fuel cell. Differentiate between Alkaline Fuel Cells (AFC) and Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cells (PAFC) based on the charge carrier and electrolyte.** **Model Answer:** An **aqueous fuel cell** is a low-temperature galvanic energy conversion device in which the electrolyte is a **liquid aqueous solution** (either alkaline or acidic) at the operating temperature, providing ionic conduction between the electrodes. | Feature | **AFC** | **PAFC** | |---|---|---| | Electrolyte | Aqueous KOH (alkaline) | Concentrated $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ (acidic) | | Charge carrier | $\text{OH}^-$ ion | $\text{H}^+$ ion (proton) | | Direction of ionic flow | Cathode → Anode | Anode → Cathode | | Water produced at | Anode | Cathode | **(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for comparison table.)** --- **Q2. [3 Marks] — State the Nernst equation for an $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ fuel cell operating in aqueous electrolyte and explain why the reversible cell voltage depends on partial pressures.** **Model Answer:** The Nernst equation for the overall cell reaction $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ is: $$E_{\text{rev}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{rev}} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$$ **Explanation:** At non-standard partial pressures of reactants, the Gibbs free energy change $\Delta G$ deviates from its standard value $\Delta G^{\circ}$ according to $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT\ln Q$. Since $E = -\Delta G/nF$, the cell EMF shifts logarithmically with the reactant activities. Higher $P_{\text{H}_2}$ or $P_{\text{O}_2}$ → more negative $\Delta G$ → higher $E_{\text{rev}}$. **(3 marks: 1 mark equation + 2 marks explanation.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Module 3 Internal Choice]** --- #### **Question A — [14 Marks]** **"Explain in detail the construction, working principle, electrochemistry, and performance characteristics of Alkaline Fuel Cells (AFC). Discuss its advantages, limitations, and applications."** ##### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]** Construction & Working Principle with Half-Cell Reactions **Model Solution:** **(i) Construction (3 marks):** An AFC consists of the following layers, sandwiched between bipolar plates: 1. **Anode:** Porous nickel electrode impregnated with Pt or Raney Ni catalyst. 2. **Cathode:** Porous silver-impregnated nickel or Pt catalyst electrode. 3. **Electrolyte:** 30–85 wt% aqueous KOH held in an **asbestos matrix** (modern versions use a **polymer-bonded KOH matrix** or a **PVA/KOH gel** to avoid health hazards of asbestos). 4. **Bipolar plates:** Nickel-plated stainless steel or carbon composite, with machined flow channels for $\text{H}_2$ (anode) and $\text{O}_2$ / air (cathode). 5. **External circuit:** Load connected between anode and cathode terminals. **(ii) Half-Cell Reactions (2 marks):** - **Anode (HOR):** $\text{H}_2 + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^-$ - **Cathode (ORR):** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^-$ - **Net:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ **(iii) Working Principle (2 marks):** $\text{H}_2$ is fed to the anode where it is oxidized, releasing electrons externally and water at the anode surface. $\text{OH}^-$ ions generated at the cathode migrate through the aqueous KOH electrolyte to the anode. Electrons flow externally through the load, performing electrical work. Pure water is the only exhaust product. ##### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]** Performance, Advantages, Limitations, Applications **(i) Performance Characteristics (2 marks):** - Operating temperature: $60\text{–}250^{\circ}\text{C}$ (typically $\sim 80^{\circ}\text{C}$). - Power density: $0.1\text{–}0.3\ \text{W cm}^{-2}$. - Electrical efficiency: 50–60% (highest among all fuel cell types due to fast ORR kinetics in alkaline media). - Cell voltage: $0.7\text{–}0.9\ \text{V}$ at typical operating current densities. **(ii) Advantages (2 marks):** 1. **Fast ORR kinetics** in alkaline media → high efficiency. 2. **Use of non-precious metal catalysts** (Ag, Ni, Raney Ni) possible → lower cost. 3. **No acid mist** — indoor safe (forklifts, submarines). 4. Pure water is a usable by-product (space missions). **(iii) Limitations (2 marks):** 1. **$\text{CO}_2$ poisoning:** Atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$ reacts with KOH to form $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$, which precipitates and blocks electrode pores. **Requires pure $\text{O}_2$ or scrubbed air.** 2. **Electrolyte leakage / carbonation** over time → limited lifetime of liquid electrolyte systems. 3. **Lower power density** than PEMFC for mobile applications. **(iv) Applications (1 mark):** - **Apollo & Space Shuttle** (NASA) — providing power + drinking water. - **German Type 212A submarines** (Siemens AFC). - **Forklifts** (Nuvera, Plug Power). - **Backup power for telecom towers** (AFC Energy). --- #### **Question B — [14 Marks] (Alternative Choice)** **"With the help of a neat polarization curve, explain the various voltage losses in a Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC). Derive the Nernst equation for an $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ aqueous cell and compute the reversible voltage at $T = 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ with $P_{\text{H}_2} = 3\ \text{atm}$ and $P_{\text{O}_2} = 1\ \text{atm}$."** ##### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]** Polarization Curve & Voltage Losses **Model Solution:** **(i) The actual cell voltage is given by:** $$V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{rev}} - \eta_{\text{act,a}} - \eta_{\text{act,c}} - \eta_{\text{ohmic}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$$ **(ii) Four Types of Voltage Losses (5 marks):** | Loss Type | Origin | Magnitude | Region on Curve | |---|---|---|---| | **Activation loss (anode + cathode)** | Kinetic barrier to electron transfer; especially severe at cathode due to sluggish ORR | $\sim 0.1\text{–}0.4\ \text{V}$ | Steep drop at low $j$ | | **Ohmic loss** | Ionic resistance of $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$ electrolyte + contact resistance | $\sim 0.05\text{–}0.10\ \text{V}$ | Linear drop in mid-$j$ | | **Concentration loss** | Mass-transport limitation of reactants to electrode surface; gas diffusion through flooded pores | $\sim 0.1\text{–}0.3\ \text{V}$ | Sharp drop at high $j$ | | **Crossover loss** (internal) | Fuel crossing through electrolyte → mixed potential | Small ($\sim$ few mV) | Affects OCV | **(iii) Diagram (2 marks):** Describe a typical $V_{\text{cell}}$ vs. $j$ curve with $E_{\text{rev}}$ as the y-intercept, showing three regions of declining voltage with increasing $j$, and a corresponding $P_{\text{cell}}$ vs. $j$ curve peaking at moderate current density. ##### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]** Derivation + Numerical Computation **(i) Derivation — Nernst Equation (3 marks):** (Provide the derivation from Steps 1–6 of Section 3.1 above. Each step 0.5 mark.) $$E_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$$ **(ii) Numerical Computation (4 marks):** **Given:** $T = 200^{\circ}\text{C} = 473\ \text{K}$, $P_{\text{H}_2} = 3\ \text{atm}$, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 1\ \text{atm}$, $a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 1$ (assume liquid-like activity; if vapor product, use saturation pressure correction — for full marks, state this assumption). **Step 1:** Compute $\dfrac{RT}{nF} = \dfrac{(8.314)(473)}{(2)(96{,}485)} = \dfrac{3932.5}{192{,}970} = 0.02038\ \text{V}$ **Step 2:** Compute the logarithm: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{(3)(1)^{1/2}}{1}\right) = \ln(3) = 1.0986$$ **Step 3:** Combine: $$E_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 + (0.02038)(1.0986)$$ $$E_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 + 0.02239 = \boxed{1.251\ \text{V}}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** The reversible voltage is **higher** than the standard value (1.229 V) because $P_{\text{H}_2} = 3\ \text{atm} > 1\ \text{atm}$, shifting the equilibrium toward higher EMF. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing water production site:** In AFC, water is produced at the **anode**; in PAFC, at the **cathode**. Reversing this will lose 2 marks. > 2. **Confusing charge carrier direction:** $\text{OH}^-$ moves **cathode → anode** in AFC; $\text{H}^+$ moves **anode → cathode** in PAFC. This is the reverse of each other. > 3. **Using LHV instead of HHV** for efficiency calculations — always **state which basis** (HHV = 285.8 kJ/mol; LHV = 241.8 kJ/mol) you are using. > 4. **Forgetting units** in the Nernst equation — $R$ in J/(mol·K), $T$ in Kelvin, $F$ in C/mol. > 5. **Ignoring the activity of water** when it is in vapor form at high T — leads to incorrect $E_{\text{rev}}$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > **For last-minute KTU exam revision — memorize these bullet points:** - ✅ **"Aqueous"** in Module 3 of PEPOT743 refers to fuel cells with a **liquid water-based electrolyte** at operating T → primarily **AFC** and **PAFC**. - ✅ AFC = **Alkaline** (KOH), charge carrier $\text{OH}^-$, water at **anode**, **$\text{CO}_2$ sensitive**, can use **non-Pt catalysts**. - ✅ PAFC = **Phosphoric Acid** ($\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$), charge carrier $\text{H}^+$, water at **cathode**, **$\text{CO}_2$ tolerant**, requires **Pt catalyst**. - ✅ Standard reversible EMF for $\text{H}_2$/$\text{O}_2$ = **1.229 V** (always remember this number!). - ✅ Nernst equation: $E_{\text{rev}} = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}\,P_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$. - ✅ $E^{\circ}_{\text{rev}} = -\Delta G^{\circ}/nF = 237200/(2 \times 96485) = 1.229\ \text{V}$. - ✅ **Activation overpotential** dominates at low current density (especially cathode ORR); **Ohmic** dominates in mid-range; **Concentration** dominates at high $j$. - ✅ **Butler–Volmer:** $\eta_{\text{act}} = \frac{RT}{\alpha nF}\ln(j/j_0)$; **Tafel:** $\eta = a + b\log_{10}j$, $b \approx 60\ \text{mV/dec}$ at 25°C with $\alpha = 0.5$, $n = 1$. - ✅ PAFC operates at **150–220°C**; AFC typically at **60–120°C**. - ✅ PAFC electrolyte (concentrated $\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$) is **immobilized in SiC matrix** to prevent leakage. - ✅ Efficiency formula: $\eta_{\text{cell}} = (V_{\text{cell}}/E^{\circ}) \times (\Delta G^{\circ}/\Delta H^{\circ})$. - ✅ AFC applications: **Apollo program, Space Shuttle, Type 212A submarines, forklifts**. - ✅ PAFC applications: **PureCell® 200/400 kW stationary CHP units**. - ✅ Voltage loss order (typical PAFC at $200\ \text{mA cm}^{-2}$): Activation $\sim 0.3\ \text{V}$ > Ohmic $\sim 0.05\ \text{V}$ > Concentration $\sim 0.02\ \text{V}$.

topics.title: Polymeric and Ceramic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polymeric and Ceramic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Polymeric and Ceramic Materials in Fuel Cells

In the context of **Fuel Cell Technology**, the terms *Polymeric* and *Ceramic* refer to the two principal classes of **solid electrolyte materials** that govern the charge-transport mechanism inside an electrochemical cell. The choice of electrolyte dictates the operating temperature regime, the type of charge carrier, and ultimately the classification of the fuel cell itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> - **Polymeric Electrolytes** are organic macromolecular membranes (e.g., perfluorosulfonic acid ionomers like **Nafion**) that conduct **protons (H⁺)** via a Grotthuss / vehicular mechanism when hydrated. They are the heart of the **PEMFC (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell)** operating at **60–100 °C**.
> - **Ceramic Electrolytes** are inorganic crystalline oxides (e.g., **YSZ – Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia, GDC – Gadolinium-Doped Ceria, LSGM – Lanthanum Strontium Magnesium Gallate**) that conduct **oxygen ions (O²⁻)** via a vacancy-hopping mechanism. They are the heart of the **SOFC (Solid Oxide Fuel Cell)** operating at **600–1000 °C**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Two Types of Highways"**
>
> Imagine ions as **vehicles** that need to travel from one electrode to the other to complete the electrical circuit.
>
> - **Polymeric Membrane (Nafion) = A Wet, Paved Highway for Tiny Cars (H⁺):**
>   Think of a sponge soaked in water. The polymer backbone (Teflon-like) provides mechanical strength, while the sulfonic acid (-SO₃H) side chains act like **water-attracting fuel stations**. Proton "cars" either hop from one water molecule to the next (**Grotthuss mechanism**, like a relay race baton pass) or ride piggyback on water clusters (**vehicular mechanism**, like a car on a ferry). The highway only works when it is **wet** — dry Nafion is a dead road.
>
> - **Ceramic Electrolyte (YSZ) = A Hot Crystal Lattice Tunnel for Oxygen Trucks (O²⁻):**
>   Picture a perfectly ordered crystal grid (like a 3D egg carton) that has a few **deliberate missing eggs** (oxygen vacancies). At high temperature, an oxygen ion from the cathode side **jumps into the nearest empty spot**, and the vacancy effectively moves in the opposite direction. This **vacancy-hopping** is much slower than proton hopping, so the tunnel must be **heated hot** to make the trucks move fast.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Highlights)

- **Target Ionic Conductivity:** $\sigma \geq \mathbf{0.01 \text{ S cm}^{-1}}$ (for both electrolyte families, to minimize ohmic losses).
- **PEMFC Operating Window:** **60–100 °C**, **1–3 bar** pressure.
- **SOFC Operating Window:** **600–1000 °C**, **1 atm** pressure.
- **Electronic Transference Number ($t_e$):** $t_e < \mathbf{0.01}$ (the electrolyte must be a **pure ionic conductor**, not an electron conductor, otherwise it short-circuits internally).
- **Nafion Equivalent Weight (EW):** **1100 g mol⁻¹** (industry standard).
- **YSZ Composition (typical):** **8 mol% Y₂O₃ in ZrO₂** (gives highest oxygen-ion conductivity in the ZrO₂–Y₂O₃ system).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Hook for KTU Viva:**
> "**Polymer = Wet & Cool, Ceramic = Dry & Hot.**" — Both conduct ions, but the polymer needs water, and the ceramic needs fire.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Charge Transport in Polymeric Electrolytes (PEM)

The ionic conductivity of a hydrated polymer membrane is governed by three coupled variables: **temperature, water content (λ), and the membrane's intrinsic equivalent weight**.

- **Step 1 — Microstructure:** Nafion has a phase-separated morphology — a hydrophobic fluorocarbon backbone and hydrophilic sulfonic acid clusters. Water pools in the hydrophilic clusters forming percolation channels.
- **Step 2 — Proton Dissociation:**
  $$-\text{SO}_3\text{H} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow -\text{SO}_3^- + \text{H}_3\text{O}^+$$
- **Step 3 — Two Parallel Transport Mechanisms:**
  - **Grotthuss (Structure Diffusion):** Proton hops along a hydrogen-bonded water network. Activation energy $\approx \mathbf{0.1\text{–}0.2 \text{ eV}}$. Dominant at low λ.
  - **Vehicular Diffusion:** Proton drags H₃O⁺ / H₅O₂⁺ as a "vehicle." Activation energy $\approx \mathbf{0.3\text{–}0.4 \text{ eV}}$. Dominant at high λ.
- **Step 4 — Water Management:** Conductivity drops catastrophically below a relative humidity of ~50% because the percolation network collapses.

### 2.2 Charge Transport in Ceramic Electrolytes (SOFC)

The ionic conductivity in ceramics follows a **thermally activated vacancy-hopping** model:

- **Step 1 — Doping Creates Vacancies:** Adding Y₂O₃ to ZrO₂ substitutes Y³⁺ on Zr⁴⁺ sites. Charge neutrality forces the formation of oxygen vacancies (Kröger-Vink notation):
  $$\text{Y}_2\text{O}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{ZrO}_2} 2\text{Y}'_{\text{Zr}} + \text{V}^{\bullet\bullet}_{\text{O}} + 3\text{O}^{\times}_{\text{O}}$$
- **Step 2 — Temperature Activates Hopping:** At high $T$, O²⁻ ions overcome the saddle-point energy barrier and jump into neighboring vacancies.
- **Step 3 — Conductivity vs. Dopant Concentration:** Conductivity peaks at ~8 mol% Y₂O₃. Lower doping → not enough vacancies; higher doping → defect clustering traps vacancies.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / When to Use | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sigma = n \, q \, \mu$ | Conductivity from carrier density and mobility (general) | S cm⁻¹ |
| 2 | $\sigma = \dfrac{n \, q^2 \, D}{k_B T}$ | **Nernst–Einstein Equation** — links conductivity to diffusion coefficient $D$ | S cm⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\sigma T = \sigma_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | **Arrhenius form for ceramic electrolytes** (plot $\ln(\sigma T)$ vs $1/T$ to get $E_a$) | S cm⁻¹·K |
| 4 | $\sigma = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{T} \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | Alternative Arrhenius form when $\sigma$ alone is plotted | S cm⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\lambda = \dfrac{n(\text{H}_2\text{O})}{n(\text{SO}_3\text{H})}$ | Hydration number of a PEM (water molecules per sulfonic site) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $t_i = \dfrac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_{\text{total}}}$ | Ionic transference number (must approach **1** for an electrolyte) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $R_{\text{ohm}} = \dfrac{L}{\sigma \, A}$ | Ohmic resistance of an electrolyte slab of thickness $L$ and area $A$ | Ω |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{ohm}} = i \, R_{\text{ohm}} = \dfrac{i \, L}{\sigma}$ | Ohmic overpotential loss (drives the KTU "thickness vs. conductivity" trade-off) | V |
| 9 | $E_{\text{Nernst}} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{H}_2}\, p_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}\right)$ | Thermodynamic open-circuit voltage of an SOFC | V |
| 10 | $D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | Diffusion coefficient (Arrhenius) | cm² s⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World Application):**
> - In **PEMFC stacks** for cars (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO), a thinner Nafion membrane (~**15 µm**) is used to lower $\eta_{\text{ohm}}$, but it must be reinforced with a porous PTFE mechanical support — a direct consequence of the $L/\sigma$ trade-off in **Formula 8**.
> - In **SOFC stationary power plants** (Bloom Energy, Siemens), YSZ is used as a **~200 µm thick electrolyte** and the cell is operated at 800 °C to push $\sigma$ above **0.1 S cm⁻¹**.

### 2.4 Why the Two Materials Behave So Differently — A Summary

| Property | Polymeric (Nafion) | Ceramic (YSZ) |
|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| Charge carrier | H⁺ | O²⁻ |
| Carrier source | Dissociated -SO₃H + H₂O | Oxygen vacancies from Y³⁺ doping |
| Transport mechanism | Grotthuss + Vehicular | Vacancy hopping |
| Activation energy | 0.1–0.4 eV | ~0.8–1.0 eV |
| Operating T | 60–100 °C | 600–1000 °C |
| Conductivity @ op. T | ~0.1 S cm⁻¹ | ~0.1 S cm⁻¹ |
| Water requirement | **Mandatory** | None (water-free) |
| Mechanical form | Flexible thin film | Brittle sintered pellet |
| Failure mode | Membrane drying, chemical attack | Cracking, cathode delamination |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note (Viva Voice):**
> Derivations below are the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivations** in the *Part B* section of ESE under Module 3. Practice them by hand on A4 sheets.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius Conductivity Law for Ceramic Electrolytes

**Statement:** Show that ionic conductivity in a vacancy-hopping ceramic electrolyte follows $\sigma T = \sigma_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ and identify the physical meaning of the pre-exponential factor.

**Assumptions:**
1. Ionic transport is a thermally activated process with a single saddle-point energy barrier $E_a$.
2. Defect concentration is fixed by dopant level (temperature-independent in the dilute regime).
3. The jump attempt frequency follows the lattice vibration frequency $\nu_0$.

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Probability of a successful hop over the barrier:**
According to the Boltzmann statistics, the probability that an oxygen ion at a lattice site acquires energy $\geq E_a$ at temperature $T$ is:

$$
P = \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Net jump frequency of the ion:**
If the ion vibrates with attempt frequency $\nu_0$ (typically the Debye frequency, $\sim 10^{13}$ Hz), the net number of successful jumps per second is:

$$
\nu = \nu_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Diffusion coefficient via Einstein–Smoluchowski relation:**
For 3D random walk with jump distance $a$ (lattice parameter), the diffusion coefficient is:

$$
D = \tfrac{1}{6} \Gamma a^2
$$

where $\Gamma$ is the jump rate per ion. Substituting $\Gamma = z \, \nu$ (with $z$ being the number of equivalent nearest-neighbor vacancy sites, $z=6$ for cubic lattice):

$$
D = \tfrac{1}{6} \, z \, \nu_0 \, a^2 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

Let us define the pre-factor $D_0 = \tfrac{1}{6} z \, \nu_0 \, a^2$. Then:

$$
D = D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Nernst–Einstein equation:**
The Nernst–Einstein equation connects $D$ and $\sigma$ for a single ionic species of charge $q = 2e$ and number density $n$:

$$
\sigma = \frac{n \, q^2}{k_B T} \, D
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $D$ into the Nernst–Einstein equation:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{n \, q^2}{k_B T} \, D_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange into the canonical Arrhenius form:**

$$
\sigma \, T = \underbrace{\left(\frac{n \, q^2 \, D_0}{k_B}\right)}_{\sigma_0} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma T = \sigma_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)\;}
$$

**Physical interpretation of the pre-exponential factor:**

$$
\sigma_0 = \frac{n \, q^2 \, D_0}{k_B} = \frac{n \, q^2 \, z \, \nu_0 \, a^2}{6 \, k_B}
$$

This bundles together (i) the carrier density $n$ set by dopant concentration, (ii) the charge squared $q^2$, and (iii) the attempt frequency × jump distance squared. The activation energy $E_a$ in the exponent is the **saddle-point migration enthalpy** of the O²⁻ ion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ohmic Overpotential of an Electrolyte Slab

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the ohmic voltage loss across a solid electrolyte of thickness $L$ and ionic conductivity $\sigma$ carrying a uniform current density $i$.

**Step 1 — Ohm's law in differential form:**

$$
J = \sigma \, E
$$

where $J$ is the ionic current density (A m⁻²) and $E$ is the local electric field (V m⁻¹).

**Step 2 — For a uniform slab, $J$ = constant and $E$ = constant:**

$$
E = \frac{J}{\sigma} = \frac{i}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage drop across the thickness $L$ (integrate constant field over the distance):**

$$
V_{\text{drop}} = \int_0^L E \, dx = E \cdot L = \frac{i \, L}{\sigma}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{ohm}} = \frac{i \, L}{\sigma}\;}
$$

**Engineering implication:** To halve the ohmic loss, one may either (a) halve the thickness $L$ (modern PEMs have dropped from 175 µm → 15 µm) or (b) double the conductivity $\sigma$ (research focus on high-σ ceramics like LSGM and Barium-Zirconate-Cerate proton conductors).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A planar SOFC uses an **8 mol% YSZ** electrolyte of thickness **L = 200 µm**. The operating temperature is **T = 1000 K**. Given: $E_a$ = 0.9 eV, $\sigma_0$ = $1.5 \times 10^6$ S cm⁻¹·K, current density $i$ = 0.5 A cm⁻². Calculate (a) the ionic conductivity, (b) the ohmic overpotential, (c) the power loss per cm² of cell area.

**(a) Conductivity:**

$$
\sigma T = \sigma_0 \exp(-E_a / k_B T)
$$

Substitute values: $E_a = 0.9 \text{ eV} = 0.9 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J = $1.442 \times 10^{-19}$ J; $k_B T = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 1000$ = $1.381 \times 10^{-20}$ J.

$$
\frac{E_a}{k_B T} = \frac{1.442 \times 10^{-19}}{1.381 \times 10^{-20}} = 10.44
$$

$$
\sigma T = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times e^{-10.44} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \times 2.91 \times 10^{-5} = 43.6 \text{ S cm}^{-1}\text{K}
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{43.6}{1000} = 0.0436 \text{ S cm}^{-1}
$$

**(b) Ohmic overpotential (using $\eta_{\text{ohm}} = iL/\sigma$ with L in cm):**

$$
L = 200 \, \mu\text{m} = 0.02 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{ohm}} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.02}{0.0436} = 0.229 \text{ V} = 229 \text{ mV}
$$

**(c) Power loss per cm²:**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = i \times \eta_{\text{ohm}} = 0.5 \times 0.229 = 0.115 \text{ W cm}^{-2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Error:** Students often forget to convert **µm → cm** when using $\sigma$ in S cm⁻¹. The "1 µm = 10⁻⁴ cm" conversion is the **#1 source of unit-error marks lost** in derivations. **Always write the unit of $L$ in the same system as $\sigma$.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for KTU Records:**
> Per the V10 protocol, Mermaid block diagrams are used to depict the **functional architecture** of polymeric and ceramic electrolyte systems rather than physical crystal sketches (which are better shown in lab manuals).

### 4.1 Comparative Functional Architecture — Polymeric vs Ceramic Electrolyte Fuel Cells

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PEMFC[POLYMERIC PEMFC - 60 to 100 C]
        A1[Anode H2] --> B1[PEMFC Anode Catalyst Pt]
        B1 --> C1{Nafion PEM}
        C1 -->|H plus| D1[PEMFC Cathode Catalyst Pt]
        D1 --> E1[Cathode O2]
        C1 -.- F1[H2O management - humidifier inlet outlet]
        C1 -.- G1[λ hydration - must be 5 to 14]
    end

    subgraph SOFC[CERAMIC SOFC - 600 to 1000 C]
        A2[Anode H2] --> B2[SOFC Anode Ni YSZ cermet]
        B2 --> C2{YSZ GDC LSGM}
        C2 -->|O two minus| D2[SOFC Cathode LSM LSCF]
        D2 --> E2[Cathode O2]
        C2 -.- F2[Heat management - prereformer]
        C2 -.- H2[Vacancy hopping - 8 mol percent Y2O3]
    end

    classDef poly fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef cer fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#993300,color:#000
    class A1,B1,C1,D1,E1,F1,G1 poly
    class A2,B2,C2,D2,E2,F2,H2 cer
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU Viva Script):**
- The **blue flow** traces the **proton (H⁺)** current in a low-temperature PEMFC.
- The **orange flow** traces the **oxygen ion (O²⁻)** current in a high-temperature SOFC.
- The dotted lines (F1, G1 / F2, H2) indicate the **subsystems that exist *only* for that fuel-cell type** — water management for PEMFC, heat management for SOFC.

---

### 4.2 Transport-Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Proton[H plus transport in Nafion - Polymeric]
        P1[SO3H site releases H plus] --> P2{Pathway selection}
        P2 -->|Low lambda| P3[Grotthuss - hopping H2O network]
        P2 -->|High lambda| P4[Vehicular - H3O plus H5O2 plus rides]
        P3 --> P5[Proton reaches cathode]
        P4 --> P5
    end

    subgraph Oxygen[O two minus transport in YSZ - Ceramic]
        O1[Dopant Y2O3 creates O vacancy] --> O2[Cathode O2 minus enters]
        O2 --> O3[Hop into adjacent vacancy]
        O3 --> O4[Vacancy effectively migrates to cathode]
        O4 --> O5[O2 minus reaches anode - reacts with H2]
    end

    classDef proto fill:#d4f1d4,stroke:#1a6b1a,color:#000
    classDef ox fill:#fff0cc,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 proto
    class O1,O2,O3,O4,O5 ox
```
