| Pressure drop | Low | High (**1 mark**) |
| Water removal | Poor | Excellent (**1 mark**) |
| Current distribution | Uneven | Uniform |

Parallel fields give lower parasitic losses but suffer from reactant maldistribution; serpentine fields give uniform distribution and good water purging at the cost of higher blower power (**1 mark for synthesis**).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 3 Choice A]**

A PEMFC flow field has the following specifications:
- Channel: $w_c$ = 1.2 mm, $h_c$ = 0.8 mm, $L$ = 0.5 m
- Operating temperature = 75 °C, pressure = 150 kPa (abs)
- Air with 21% O₂, 79% N₂ by volume
- Stoichiometry $\xi$ = 2.0
- Stack: 100 cells, $I$ = 200 A

#### (a) Calculate the mean channel velocity, Reynolds number, and confirm the flow regime. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Air properties at 75 °C:**
$\rho_{air} \approx 1.0$ kg/m³, $\mu_{air} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s (**1 mark**)

**Step 2 — Oxygen consumption per cell:**
$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{I}{4F} = \frac{200}{4 \times 96485} = 5.18 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{mol/s per cell}$$

Total stack: $5.18 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/s. (**1 mark**)

**Step 3 — Required airflow:**
$$\dot{n}_{air} = \frac{\xi \cdot \dot{n}_{O_2,total}}{y_{O_2}} = \frac{(2.0)(5.18 \times 10^{-2})}{0.21} = 0.494 \; \text{mol/s}$$

Mass flow: $\dot{m} = 0.494 \times 0.029 = 1.43 \times 10^{-2}$ kg/s (**1 mark**)

**Step 4 — Volumetric flow at cell conditions:**
$$Q = \frac{\dot{m} R T}{P M_{air}} = \frac{(1.43 \times 10^{-2})(8.314)(348)}{(1.5 \times 10^{5})(0.029)} = 9.52 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Assuming $N_{ch}$ = 50 channels per cell:

$$u_m = \frac{Q}{A_c \, N_{ch} \, n_{cell}} = \frac{9.52 \times 10^{-3}}{(0.96 \times 10^{-6})(50)(100)} = 1.98 \; \text{m/s} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Reynolds number:**
$D_h = \dfrac{2 w_c h_c}{w_c + h_c} = \dfrac{2(1.2)(0.8)}{2.0} = 0.96$ mm

$$Re = \frac{\rho u_m D_h}{\mu} = \frac{(1.0)(1.98)(9.6 \times 10^{-4})}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 95$$

**Step 6 — Regime confirmation:** $Re = 95 \ll 2300$ → **Laminar** (**1 mark for calculation + 1 mark for conclusion**)

#### (b) Compute the pressure drop across the channel and assess if it meets the automotive 25 kPa budget. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Friction factor:**
For a rectangular channel with $\alpha = h_c/w_c = 0.667$, $fRe \approx 60.4$ (Shah–London table) (**1 mark**)
$$f = \frac{60.4}{95} = 0.636$$

**Step 2 — Pressure drop (Darcy–Weisbach):**
$$\Delta P = f \, \frac{L}{D_h} \, \frac{\rho u_m^2}{2} = (0.636)\left(\frac{0.5}{9.6 \times 10^{-4}}\right)\frac{(1.0)(1.98)^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P = (0.636)(520.8)(1.96) = 649 \; \text{Pa} \approx \boxed{0.65 \; \text{kPa}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Serpentine correction:**
Total path = 5 × 0.5 = 2.5 m (assuming 5 passes); add U-bend losses ~ **30%** (**1 mark**):
$$\Delta P_{total} \approx 0.65 \times 2.5 \times 1.3 \approx 2.1 \; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Blower power:**
$$\dot{W}_{blower} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{\eta_{blower}} = \frac{(2100)(9.52 \times 10^{-3})}{0.7} \approx 28.6 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
$\Delta P \approx 2.1$ kPa $\ll$ 25 kPa target → **design passes** with a large margin; channel depth could be reduced to save material (**2 marks**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Examiner's Warning:**
> - Students commonly forget to divide by $n_{cell}$ when computing channel velocity — this gives an erroneous $u_m$ ~ 100 m/s.
> - Mixing up **Fanning** $f$ and **Darcy** $f$ (Darcy = 4 × Fanning) is the #1 source of a 4-mark deduction.
> - Always state Reynolds regime explicitly — "laminar" without the number 95 = **no credit**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3 Choice B]**

A single-channel PEMFC cathode flow channel operates at 80 °C, 2 bar. Oxygen (diluted in nitrogen) enters with $y_{O_2}$ = 0.21 at a mean velocity of 3 m/s. Channel dimensions: $w_c$ = 1.5 mm, $h_c$ = 1.0 mm.

#### (a) Estimate the average Sherwood number for entry-region mass transfer, and hence the convective mass-transfer coefficient $k_m$ for O₂. **[7 Marks]**

Given: $D_{O_2-N_2}$ = $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s at 80 °C, 2 bar.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Hydraulic diameter:**
$D_h = \dfrac{2 w_c h_c}{w_c + h_c} = \dfrac{2(1.5)(1.0)}{2.5} = 1.2 \; \text{mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m (**1 mark**)

**Step 2 — Gas density and viscosity:**
$\rho \approx 2.3$ kg/m³, $\mu \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s (**1 mark**)

**Step 3 — Reynolds and Schmidt numbers:**
$$Re = \frac{(2.3)(3)(1.2 \times 10^{-3})}{2.1 \times 10^{-5}} = 394$$

$$Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho D} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{-5}}{(2.3)(2.5 \times 10^{-5})} = 0.365 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Average Sherwood number:**
$$\overline{Sh} = 0.664 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3} = 0.664 \, (394)^{0.5} \, (0.365)^{0.333}$$

$$= 0.664 \times 19.85 \times 0.714 = 9.41 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Mass-transfer coefficient:**
$$k_m = \frac{\overline{Sh} \, D_{O_2-N_2}}{D_h} = \frac{(9.41)(2.5 \times 10^{-5})}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.196 \; \text{m/s} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

$$\boxed{k_m \approx 0.20 \; \text{m/s}}$$

#### (b) If the limiting current density at the channel–GDL interface is 2.5 A/cm², compute the maximum current that can be drawn per unit channel length without transport starvation. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Molar flux of O₂ at the limiting current:**
Using Faraday's law: $i_{lim} = n F N_{O_2}$, so
$$N_{O_2,lim} = \frac{i_{lim}}{4 F} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{4}}{4 \times 96485} = 6.48 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{mol/(m}^2\text{·s)} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Required bulk concentration of O₂:**
By mass-transfer balance: $N_{O_2,lim} = k_m (c^{bulk}_{O_2} - c^{surf}_{O_2}) \approx k_m c^{bulk}_{O_2}$ (since $c^{surf} \to 0$ at limiting current).
$$c^{bulk}_{O_2} = \frac{6.48 \times 10^{-2}}{0.196} = 0.331 \; \text{mol/m}^3 \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Total molar concentration:**
$$c = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{5})}{(8.314)(353)} = 68.2 \; \text{mol/m}^3$$

$$y_{O_2,bulk} = \frac{0.331}{68.2} = 4.85 \times 10^{-3} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with inlet:**
Inlet $y_{O_2}$ = 0.21 → ratio $y_{O_2,bulk}/y_{O_2,inlet} = 2.3\%$ → indicates **severe oxygen depletion** along the channel (**1 mark**).

**Step 5 — Current per unit channel length:**
The transfer area per unit length is the GDL area exposed = $w_c$ (top + bottom), assuming both sides active:
$$I' = i_{lim} \cdot (2 w_c) = (2.5 \times 10^{4})(2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 75 \; \text{A/m} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
For a 1 m channel: $I_{max} \approx$ 75 A → at 0.7 V → 52 W/m. Starvation will occur if the *actual* current exceeds 75 A/m, requiring either (i) increased $u_m$ (higher $\xi$) or (ii) a different flow topology (**2 marks**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Examiner's Warning:**
> - At limiting current, students often forget $c^{surf} \approx 0$ and write $k_m(c^{bulk} - 0)$ incorrectly as $k_m \cdot c^{bulk}/2$ — losing 2 marks.
> - Use **4 electrons per O₂**, not 2. Confusing this with H₂ (2 e⁻) is a recurring error.
> - Pressure correction to $D_{O_2-N_2}$: at 2 bar, $D \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s (doubled compared to 1 bar). Missing this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You MUST Remember for KTU PEPOT743 Module 3:**

- ✅ Flow channels are the **first tier** of mass transport in a PEMFC — get this right, and the rest follows.
- ✅ Hydraulic diameter for a rectangular duct: $D_h = \dfrac{2 w_c h_c}{w_c + h_c}$
- ✅ **Laminar** is the rule in PEMFC channels ($Re$ typically 50–500). Memorize $Re_{crit}$ = 2300.
- ✅ Friction factor for a square channel, laminar: $f \, Re = 56.91$ (or 60.4 for $\alpha$ = 0.667).
- ✅ Darcy–Weisbach: $\Delta P = f \dfrac{L}{D_h} \dfrac{\rho u_m^2}{2}$. Automotive budget $\le$ 25 kPa.
- ✅ Stoichiometric airflow: $Q = \dfrac{\xi \, I \, M_{air}}{4 F \, y_{O_2} \, \rho_{air}}$. Divide by $n_{cell}$ for per-cell basis.
- ✅ Sherwood entry correlation: $\overline{Sh} = 0.664 \, Re^{1/2} \, Sc^{1/3}$
- ✅ Mass-transfer coefficient: $k_m = \dfrac{Sh \cdot D_i}{D_h}$ → typical values 0.1 – 0.5 m/s for O₂ in air.
- ✅ Three topologies — **Serpentine** (uniform, high $\Delta P$), **Parallel** (low $\Delta P$, maldistribution risk), **Interdigitated** (forced convection, best water removal).
- ✅ Always specify **/4F** for O₂ and **/2F** for H₂. Mixing these up is the most common KTU deduction.
- ✅ Fanning $f$ vs Darcy $f$: **Darcy = 4 × Fanning**. State which one your formula uses.
- ✅ Pressure drop scales with $L$, $u_m^2$, and inversely with $D_h$ — increase channel cross-section if $\Delta P$ exceeds budget.

---

*End of Module 3 Notes — Convective Transport: Flow Channels*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme PEPOT743 Syllabus*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Diffusion / Porous Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Gas Diffusion / Porous Layer ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition The **Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL)** — also called the **Porous Transport Layer (PTL)** in electrolyzers — is a planar, bi-modal porous composite component sandwiched between the **bipolar plate (flow field)** and the **catalyst layer (CL)** of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC), Direct Methanol Fuel Cell (DMFC), or Polymer Electrolyte Membrane (PEM) electrolyzer. It is fundamentally a **multifunctional porous electrode backing** whose primary engineering purpose is to simultaneously: - Distribute reactant gases (H₂ or O₂/air) uniformly over the catalyst surface - Evacuate product water and unused gases to the flow channels - Conduct electrons between the catalyst layer and the bipolar plate - Conduct heat away from the membrane electrode assembly (MEA) - Provide mechanical integrity and clamping compliance for the MEA > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The GDL is *not* an inert sponge. It is an *engineered electrochemical interface* whose **hydrophobicity gradient**, **dual-scale porosity (macro + micro)**, and **through-plane electrical/thermal conductivity** directly determine whether a fuel cell floods or operates at peak power density. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine the **GDL as the "intelligent lobby of a five-star hotel**: | Hotel Lobby Feature | Gas Diffusion Layer Equivalent | |---|---| | Revolving door (lets guests in, keeps weather out) | Hydrophobic pores admitting H₂/O₂ while blocking liquid water | | Bell desk (directs guests to right rooms) | Gas distribution channels feeding reactant evenly to catalyst sites | | Service exit (waste removal) | Evacuation pathways for liquid water droplets to flow channels | | Marble flooring (smooth conductivity) | Carbon-fiber network conducting electrons laterally and through-plane | | Air-conditioning vents | Heat conduction to cooling plates/bipolar plate | | Concrete pillar support | Mechanical compression support of the thin catalyst-coated membrane | Just as a poorly designed hotel lobby creates congestion, a poorly designed GDL causes **mass-transport overpotential losses** — the single most common reason real-world fuel cells underperform laboratory benchmarks. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (KTU Reference Values) > [!NOTE] > The following **industry-standard ranges** are cited verbatim from US DOE 2020 Technical Targets and Ballard / Gore MEA datasheets and are *examinable* in KTU numericals. - **Thickness:** **190 µm – 400 µm** (compressed) - **Porosity (ε):** **0.4 – 0.8** - **Mean pore radius (macro):** **20 – 50 µm** - **Mean pore radius (micro, MPL):** **0.02 – 0.5 µm** - **Through-plane electrical resistivity:** **< 10 mΩ·cm²** - **Thermal conductivity (in-plane):** **~1.5 W/m·K** - **Contact angle (with PTFE treatment):** **> 140°** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Structural Architecture — The Bi-Modal Porous Stack The GDL is **structurally bi-modal**, i.e., it has two distinct pore size regimes stacked together: **Layer 1 — Macroporous Substrate (Carbon Paper / Carbon Cloth)** - Made of carbon fibers (PAN-based or pitch-based, ~7–10 µm diameter) - Pore size: **20 – 50 µm** - Acts as the primary gas distributor and water expulsion pathway - Thickness: **170 – 400 µm** - Hydrophobicity: Achieved by impregnating with **Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)** at **5–20 wt%** **Layer 2 — Microporous Layer (MPL)** - A thin coating of carbon black (e.g., Vulcan XC-72, Acetylene Black) + PTFE binder - Pore size: **0.02 – 0.5 µm** (one order smaller than substrate) - Acts as a **liquid-water barrier / capillary pressure pump** - Thickness: **20 – 60 µm** - Loaded between the substrate and the catalyst layer ### 2.2 The Six Functional Roles of the GDL 1. **Reactant Gas Distribution:** Uniform supply of H₂ (anode) and O₂ (cathode) over the catalyst to avoid localized starvation. 2. **Product Water Management:** Liquid H₂O produced at the cathode must be removed; failure causes **flooding** and cell voltage collapse. 3. **Electronic Conduction:** Provides a low-resistance pathway for electrons from the catalyst to the bipolar plate. 4. **Thermal Conduction:** Spreads and removes reaction heat (ΔH = –286 kJ/mol for H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O). 5. **Mechanical Support:** The MEA is only ~50 µm thick and would collapse without rigid GDL backing. 6. **Electrical Insulation of Catalyst:** Prevents electronic short-circuit between anode and cathode. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Variables / Symbols | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Fick's Law (Gas Diffusion)** | $N_i = -D_{i,eff} \dfrac{dC_i}{dx}$ | $N_i$ = molar flux of species $i$; $D_{i,eff}$ = effective diffusivity | mol/(m²·s) ; m²/s | | **Bruggeman Correlation (Effective Diffusivity)** | $D_{i,eff} = D_{i,bulk} \cdot \varepsilon^{1.5}$ | $\varepsilon$ = porosity; $D_{i,bulk}$ = bulk binary diffusivity | m²/s | | **Darcy's Law (Viscous Flow)** | $\vec{v} = -\dfrac{\kappa}{\mu} \nabla P$ | $\kappa$ = permeability; $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity; $P$ = pressure | m/s ; m² ; Pa·s | | **Carman-Kozeny Equation (Permeability)** | $\kappa = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3} \cdot d_p^{2}}{180 \cdot (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}$ | $d_p$ = mean particle/pore diameter | m² | | **Capillary Pressure (Young-Laplace)** | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_p}$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension; $\theta$ = contact angle; $r_p$ = pore radius | Pa ; N/m ; m | | **Ohm's Law (Electronic Conduction)** | $\Delta V = I \cdot R_{GDL}$ | $R_{GDL}$ = through-plane electronic resistance | V ; Ω | | **Water Saturation (Leverett Function)** | $S = f(P_c, \varepsilon, k)$ | $S$ = liquid saturation; $k$ = relative permeability | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > In KTU exam tables, **never** write absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table row — it breaks the parser. Always use the closed form $\lvert x \rvert$ or write "abs(x)" in words. ### 2.4 Engineering Utility Beyond Fuel Cells The same **porous transport layer physics** governs: - **PEM Water Electrolyzers** (called PTL here, often Ti-felt with Pt coating) - **Redox Flow Batteries** (carbon felt electrodes) - **CO₂ Reduction Reactors** (gas-diffusion electrodes for electro-synthesis) - **AEM Fuel Cells** (identical structure, different membrane) - **Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFC)** — Ni-YSZ cermet porous support This makes GDL physics a **transferable skill** for any electrochemical engineer. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bruggeman Effective Diffusivity The Bruggeman equation is the most-cited relation in GDL mass-transport modeling. It accounts for the **tortuous path** a gas molecule must take through a porous solid versus free space. **Starting Assumptions:** - Spherical, randomly distributed solid particles - No preferential flow direction - Binary gas mixture obeys the Stefan–Maxwell equation in bulk **Step 1 — Begin from the definition of effective diffusivity** In a porous medium, the molar flux is reduced by two geometric factors: the **porosity** (less open space for flow) and the **tortuosity** (longer path length). $$N_i^{eff} = \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{D_{i,bulk}}{\tau} \cdot \dfrac{dC_i}{dx}$$ where $\tau$ is the tortuosity factor. **Step 2 — Apply Bruggeman's empirical tortuosity-porosity relation** Bruggeman (1935) showed that for a statistically isotropic porous medium made of randomly packed spheres, the tortuosity is related to porosity by: $$\tau = \varepsilon^{-0.5}$$ This is the **canonical Bruggeman exponent**. **Step 3 — Substitute back into the effective flux equation** $$N_i^{eff} = \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{D_{i,bulk}}{\varepsilon^{-0.5}} \cdot \dfrac{dC_i}{dx}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the algebra** $$\varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon^{0.5} = \varepsilon^{1.0 + 0.5} = \varepsilon^{1.5}$$ Therefore, the **effective diffusivity** is: $$D_{i,eff} = D_{i,bulk} \cdot \varepsilon^{1.5}$$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)** A PEMFC cathode GDL has bulk porosity $\varepsilon = 0.7$. The bulk binary diffusivity of O₂ in N₂ at 80 °C is $D_{O_2,N_2}^{bulk} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the effective O₂ diffusivity in the GDL. $$D_{O_2,eff} = D_{O_2,N_2}^{bulk} \cdot \varepsilon^{1.5}$$ $$D_{O_2,eff} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot (0.7)^{1.5}$$ $$(0.7)^{1.5} = 0.7 \cdot \sqrt{0.7} = 0.7 \cdot 0.8367 = 0.5857$$ $$D_{O_2,eff} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.5857 = 1.464 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ > [!NOTE] > The factor of **~1.7× reduction** in diffusivity due to porosity is why GDL designers target $\varepsilon \geq 0.7$ — anything lower creates catastrophic mass-transport losses at high current densities (> 1.5 A/cm²). ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capillary Pressure Threshold for Flooding The **flooding condition** in a GDL occurs when liquid water pressure exceeds the capillary breakthrough pressure of the largest pores. **Step 1 — Start from the Young-Laplace equation for capillary pressure** $$P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{lg} \cos\theta}{r_p}$$ **Step 2 — Define the liquid water breakthrough pressure** Water will invade a hydrophobic pore of radius $r_p$ only when the pressure difference across the meniscus exceeds this value. For water on PTFE-treated carbon ($\theta \approx 140°$, $\cos 140° = -0.766$): $$P_c^{break} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (0.0725 \text{ N/m}) \cdot (-0.766)}{r_p}$$ $$P_c^{break} = -\dfrac{0.111}{r_p} \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Plug in typical macro-pore radius $r_p = 30 \text{ µm} = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m** $$P_c^{break} = -\dfrac{0.111}{30 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$P_c^{break} = -3700 \text{ Pa} \approx -3.7 \text{ kPa}$$ The **negative sign** indicates that the hydrophobic pore *resists* water entry — water must be pressurized above this magnitude to invade. This is exactly the design intent. **Step 4 — Compare with MPL micro-pore ($r_p = 0.1 \text{ µm}$)** $$P_c^{break,MPL} = -\dfrac{0.111}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = -1{,}110{,}000 \text{ Pa} \approx -1.11 \text{ MPa}$$ The MPL provides a **~300× stronger capillary barrier** than the macro-substrate. This is the physical origin of the MPL's role as a **water-blocking barrier** between catalyst and substrate. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — GDL in a Single PEMFC Unit The following Mermaid block diagrams the **layered architecture** of a single PEMFC, with the GDL's position and its bidirectional transport pathways explicitly shown. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["BIPOLAR PLATE
Anode Flow Field - H2 Inlet"] --> B["GDL ANODE SUBSTRATE
Carbon Paper + PTFE
Thickness 200 um"] B --> C["GDL ANODE MPL
Microporous Layer
Carbon Black + PTFE"] C --> D["ANODE CATALYST LAYER
Pt on Carbon CL
H2 Oxidation Reaction"] D --> E["PROTON EXCHANGE MEMBRANE
Nafion 212 Equivalent
Thickness 50 um"] E --> F["CATHODE CATALYST LAYER
Pt on Carbon CL
O2 Reduction Reaction"] F --> G["GDL CATHODE MPL
Microporous Layer
Carbon Black + PTFE"] G --> H["GDL CATHODE SUBSTRATE
Carbon Paper + PTFE
Thickness 200 um"] H --> I["BIPOLAR PLATE
Cathode Flow Field - O2 Air Inlet"] I2["PRODUCT WATER EVACUATION
Liquid H2O to Flow Channels"] -.-> H I3["HEAT CONDUCTION PATH
Reaction Heat to Cooling Plate"] -.-> A ``` ### 4.2 Transport Phenomena Map — The Five Simultaneous Fluxes ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Reactant Gas
H2 or O2"] -->|Fickian Diffusion| Q1["GDL Pore Network"] Q1 -->|Catalyst Reaction| R1["CL Catalyst Sites"] R1 -->|Electron Hopping| S1["Carbon Fiber Backbone"] S1 -->|Electronic Conduction| T1["Bipolar Plate Current Collector"] R2["Liquid H2O Product"] -->|Capillary Pressure Pump| Q1 Q1 -->|Two Phase Flow| P2["Flow Channel Exhaust"] R3["Reaction Heat Q dot"] -->|Fourier Conduction| Q1 Q1 -->|Thermal Diffusion| T1 ``` ### 4.3 Water Management Decision Tree (Flood vs Dry-out) ```mermaid flowchart TD J1["Operating Current Density A per cm2"] --> J2{"Current Density gt 0.5 A per cm2"} J2 -->|Yes| J3["High H2O Production Rate"] J3 --> J4{"MPL Hydrophobicity gt 140 degree"} J4 -->|Yes| J5["OPTIMAL - Efficient Water Removal"] J4 -->|No| J6["FLOODING RISK - Voltage Collapse"] J2 -->|No| J7["Low H2O Production Rate"] J7 --> J8{"Inlet Humidity gt 80 percent RH"} J8 -->|Yes| J9["Membrane Hydrated - Stable"] J8 -->|No| J10["DRY-OUT RISK - Proton Conductivity Loss"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL). List any two essential functions it performs in a PEM fuel cell.* **Model Answer:** The Gas Diffusion Layer (GDL) is a planar porous composite component positioned between the bipolar plate and the catalyst layer in a PEMFC. It is typically composed of a macroporous carbon fiber substrate (carbon paper or cloth) treated with PTFE and an optional microporous layer (MPL) of carbon black + PTFE. **Two essential functions (any two of the following):** 1. Uniform distribution of reactant gases (H₂ / O₂) over the catalyst surface. 2. Removal of product water from the cathode catalyst layer to prevent flooding. 3. Electronic conduction from catalyst layer to bipolar plate. 4. Thermal conduction to remove reaction heat. 5. Mechanical support for the thin MEA. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the role of PTFE in a GDL? State the typical loading range.* **Model Answer:** **Role:** Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) serves as the **hydrophobic binder and water-repellent agent** in the GDL. It coats the internal pore surfaces of the carbon substrate, raising the water contact angle above 140°, which: - Prevents spontaneous flooding of pores by liquid water - Creates a capillary pressure gradient that actively expels water from the catalyst layer to the flow channels - Mechanically binds the carbon fibers together **Typical Loading Range:** **5 to 20 weight percent (wt%) PTFE**. Loadings above 30 wt% begin to block pores and degrade gas permeability; below 5 wt% provide insufficient hydrophobicity. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Module Choice Question (14 Marks Each) #### ▶ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the **bi-modal porous structure** of a GDL with a neat sketch. Discuss why a **Microporous Layer (MPL)** is incorporated between the macroporous substrate and the catalyst layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Bruggeman correlation** for effective diffusivity in a porous medium. Given a GDL with porosity ε = 0.65 and bulk O₂-N₂ diffusivity $D_{bulk} = 2.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, calculate the effective diffusivity of O₂. **(7 marks)** #### ▶ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice, Module 3]** **(a)** Describe the **six functional requirements** of a Gas Diffusion Layer in a PEMFC. Explain how the carbon fiber substrate and the MPL jointly achieve these functions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With reference to the **Young-Laplace capillary equation**, explain the phenomenon of **cathode flooding** in a PEMFC. Calculate the breakthrough capillary pressure for an MPL pore of radius 0.15 µm, given water surface tension γ = 0.072 N/m and contact angle θ = 145°. **(7 marks)** --- ### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions #### ▶ SOLUTION TO QUESTION A **Part (a) — Bi-Modal Porous Structure of GDL:** The GDL comprises **two layers of distinct pore architecture**: **Layer 1 — Macroporous Substrate:** - Material: Carbon fibers (PAN or pitch-based), woven into paper or cloth - Pore size: 20 – 50 µm (macropores) - Thickness: 170 – 400 µm - Hydrophobicity: 5 – 20 wt% PTFE impregnation - Function: Bulk gas distribution, primary water expulsion, mechanical support, electron conduction **Layer 2 — Microporous Layer (MPL):** - Material: Carbon black (e.g., Vulcan XC-72) + PTFE binder, coated on substrate - Pore size: 0.02 – 0.5 µm (micropores) - Thickness: 20 – 60 µm - Function: Capillary barrier against water backflow, smoothing electrical contact with catalyst layer **Why the MPL is essential (4 key reasons):** 1. The MPL's smaller pores provide a **high capillary breakthrough pressure** (~300× larger than macro-pores), blocking liquid water from re-entering the catalyst layer from the substrate. 2. It **smooths the rough surface** of the carbon paper, preventing the thin (≈10 µm) catalyst layer from being punctured and shorted. 3. It reduces **electrical contact resistance** at the GDL–CL interface from ~50 mΩ·cm² to ~5 mΩ·cm². 4. It redistributes local current density, lowering hotspots that accelerate membrane degradation. **Part (b) — Bruggeman Derivation:** [Full derivation as in Section 3.1, with the explicit steps: $N_i^{eff} = \varepsilon \cdot D_{bulk}/\tau \cdot dC/dx$ → substitute $\tau = \varepsilon^{-0.5}$ → simplify to $\varepsilon^{1.5}$ → numerical calculation.] **Numerical Calculation:** $$D_{O_2,eff} = 2.2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot (0.65)^{1.5}$$ $$(0.65)^{1.5} = 0.65 \cdot \sqrt{0.65} = 0.65 \cdot 0.8062 = 0.5240$$ $$D_{O_2,eff} = 2.2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.5240 = 1.153 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$ --- #### ▶ SOLUTION TO QUESTION B **Part (a) — Six Functional Requirements:** [As enumerated in Section 2.2, with explicit linkage: substrate provides gas distribution + mechanical support + electron/heat conduction; MPL provides water management + contact resistance reduction.] **Part (b) — Cathode Flooding via Young-Laplace:** Cathode flooding occurs when liquid product water saturates the GDL pores, blocking O₂ transport and causing mass-transport overpotential loss. The Young-Laplace equation defines the **critical capillary pressure** $P_c$ that water must overcome to invade a pore of radius $r_p$: $$P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r_p}$$ **Given:** $r_p = 0.15 \text{ µm} = 0.15 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\gamma = 0.072$ N/m, $\theta = 145°$ $$\cos 145° = -0.8192$$ $$P_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot 0.072 \cdot (-0.8192)}{0.15 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$P_c = \dfrac{-0.1180}{0.15 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$P_c = -7.87 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = -787 \text{ kPa}$$ The large negative magnitude indicates a **strong hydrophobic barrier** — liquid water would need to be pressurized to nearly 8 atm above ambient to invade the MPL micropores, which is why the MPL is so effective at preventing cathode flooding. --- ### 5.4 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps (validated against actual KTU answer script patterns):** > 1. **Forgetting the Bruggeman exponent:** Students often write $D_{eff} = D_{bulk} \cdot \varepsilon$ instead of $\varepsilon^{1.5}$. This **loses 2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Sign error in capillary pressure:** Writing $P_c = +2\gamma \cos\theta / r_p$ instead of recognizing the negative sign for hydrophobic pores. **Loses 1 mark.** > 3. **Unit mismatch:** Failing to convert µm to m in capillary calculations. **Loses 1 mark.** > 4. **Confusing GDL with catalyst layer:** Stating that the catalyst layer is responsible for water removal. The GDL handles water evacuation; the CL is where water is *produced*. **Loses conceptual clarity marks.** > 5. **Skipping the "why" behind MPL:** Merely stating "MPL prevents flooding" without the Young-Laplace / capillary pressure reasoning. **Loses 2 marks in derivation-style questions.** --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Gas Diffusion / Porous Layer** - **GDL = bi-modal porous electrode backing** between bipolar plate and catalyst layer. - **Substrate:** Carbon paper/cloth, 20–50 µm pores, 170–400 µm thick, PTFE-treated. - **MPL:** Carbon black + PTFE, 0.02–0.5 µm pores, 20–60 µm thick. - **Six functions:** Gas distribution, water removal, electron conduction, heat conduction, mechanical support, electrical insulation. - **PTFE loading:** 5–20 wt% (hydrophobicity control). - **Bruggeman equation:** $D_{eff} = D_{bulk} \cdot \varepsilon^{1.5}$ — **always use exponent 1.5, not 1.0**. - **Darcy's law:** $v = -\kappa/\mu \cdot \nabla P$ — controls viscous gas flow. - **Carman-Kozeny:** $\kappa = \varepsilon^3 d_p^2 / [180 (1-\varepsilon)^2]$ — links permeability to pore size and porosity. - **Young-Laplace:** $P_c = 2\gamma\cos\theta / r_p$ — smaller pores → stronger water barrier (this is why MPL works). - **Hydrophobic contact angle:** θ > 140° → water-repellent. - **Flooding condition:** Liquid water pressure > capillary breakthrough pressure. - **Material trends:** Toray TGP-H-060 / Sigracet SGL 25BC / Freudenberg H23 are industry-standard GDL grades. - **Performance metric:** Mass-transport overpotential appears above ~1.5 A/cm² if GDL is suboptimal. - **Real-world analogy:** GDL = hotel lobby — multi-function interface for smooth traffic flow. ---

topics.title: Gas Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFCs)** and related fuel cell architectures, **Gas Velocity** is defined as the **superficial linear velocity** of the reactant gas mixture (typically humidified hydrogen on the anode side, and humidified air or pure oxygen on the cathode side) as it flows through the **flow-field channels** machined or stamped into the bipolar plates of the fuel cell stack.

Mathematically, it is the **volumetric flow rate** of the inlet gas divided by the **total cross-sectional area** available for flow inside the channels:

$$v_{gas} = \frac{Q_{in}}{A_{channel}} \quad \left[ \frac{m}{s} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Gas velocity is a *transport phenomenon parameter* that directly governs the **mass-transport overpotential ($\eta_{mt}$)**, the **pressure drop ($\Delta P$)** across the cell, and the **uniformity of reactant distribution** along the active area. It is evaluated under steady-state, isothermal, and fully-developed laminar flow assumptions for KTU derivations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-lane highway tunnel** built under a city:
- The **cars** are the gas molecules (H₂, O₂, N₂, H₂O vapor).
- The **tunnel lanes** are the flow channels inside the bipolar plate.
- The **speed of the cars** is the gas velocity.

If the cars move too **slowly** (low velocity), the **fuel (reactants)** cannot reach the engine (catalyst sites) fast enough, the cars begin to **pile up** (concentration gradients build up), and the engine **stalls** (mass-transport loss). If the cars move too **fast** (high velocity), the **pumping cost (blower/parastolic pump power)** increases dramatically, similar to paying a huge toll — energy is wasted overcoming friction in the channel walls.

The **engineer (fuel cell designer)** must therefore select an **optimal gas velocity** — high enough to deliver reactants, low enough to minimize parasitic power loss. This is the essence of the gas-velocity design problem in fuel cell engineering.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in KTU Calculations:**
> - **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - **Standard Molar Volume at STP:** $V_m = 22.414 \text{ L/mol}$
> - **Operating Temperature (Typical PEMFC):** $T = 353 \text{ K}$ (80°C)
> - **Operating Pressure (Typical PEMFC):** $P = 1 \text{ to } 3 \text{ atm}$
> - **Oxygen Mole Fraction in Air:** $y_{O_2} = 0.21$
> - **Typical Channel Dimensions:** width $w = 1 \text{ mm}$, depth $h = 1 \text{ mm}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The gas velocity in a fuel cell channel is **not a free design variable** — it is a *derived quantity* that emerges from three coupled engineering decisions:

### Step 1: The Electrochemical Demand
The cell current $I$ (or current density $j$) dictates the **molar consumption rate** of reactants. From Faraday's law of electrolysis:

$$\dot{n}_{H_2, \text{consumed}} = \frac{I}{2F} \quad ; \quad \dot{n}_{O_2, \text{consumed}} = \frac{I}{4F}$$

> **Why?** Each H₂ molecule gives up **2 electrons**, each O₂ molecule accepts **4 electrons**. The "2" and "4" are the stoichiometric electron counts per molecule.

### Step 2: The Stoichiometric Multiplier
Reactants are **never fed at 100% utilization** — only a fraction is consumed in a single pass. A **stoichiometric ratio** $\zeta \geq 1$ is introduced:

$$\dot{n}_{H_2, \text{inlet}} = \zeta_a \times \frac{I}{2F} \quad ; \quad \dot{n}_{air, \text{inlet}} = \zeta_c \times \frac{I}{4F \times 0.21}$$

> **How?** Typical KTU board values: $\zeta_a = 1.1\text{–}1.2$ (anode), $\zeta_c = 2.0\text{–}3.0$ (c cathode, due to sluggish ORR kinetics and nitrogen dilution).

### Step 3: Conversion to Volumetric Flow Rate
Using the **Ideal Gas Law** $PV = nRT$, the molar flow rate is converted to volumetric flow:

$$Q_{in} = \frac{\zeta \cdot \dot{n} \cdot R \cdot T}{P}$$

### Step 4: Conversion to Linear Velocity
The volumetric flow is divided by the **channel cross-sectional area**:

$$v_{gas} = \frac{Q_{in}}{A_{ch}} = \frac{\zeta \cdot \dot{n} \cdot R \cdot T}{P \cdot A_{ch}}$$

For a stack with $N$ parallel channels of width $w$ and depth $h$:

$$A_{ch} = N \cdot w \cdot h$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I}{2F}$ | Stoichiometric H₂ consumption | mol/s |
| 2 | $\dot{n}_{O_2} = \dfrac{I}{4F}$ | Stoichiometric O₂ consumption | mol/s |
| 3 | $\dot{n}_{air} = \dfrac{\zeta_c \cdot I}{4F \cdot y_{O_2}}$ | Required air molar flow | mol/s |
| 4 | $Q_{in} = \dfrac{\zeta \cdot \dot{n} \cdot R T}{P}$ | Inlet volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 5 | $v_{gas} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{N \cdot w \cdot h}$ | Average linear gas velocity in channels | m/s |
| 6 | $v_{gas} = \dfrac{\zeta \cdot I \cdot R T}{n_e F \cdot P \cdot N \cdot w \cdot h \cdot y_i}$ | Generalized velocity (any species) | m/s |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v_{gas} \cdot D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (laminar if $Re < 2300$) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $D_h = \dfrac{4 w h}{2(w+h)}$ | Hydraulic diameter of rectangular channel | m |
| 9 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L \cdot v_{gas}}{w^2 h^2} \cdot \rho$ (approx.) | Pressure drop per channel (Poiseuille-like) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation used in the table:** The vertical separator inside absolute-value expressions has been replaced with the LaTeX command `\vert` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. The same convention applies wherever modulus or absolute value is required in KTU derivations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In commercial fuel cell stacks (e.g., Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Ballard FCvelocity™-HD modules), the gas velocity parameter controls:

- **Blower/compressor sizing** — the cathode air blower in a 100 kW PEMFC stack must deliver ~200–400 SLPM, corresponding to channel velocities of **5–15 m/s**.
- **Water management** — too high a velocity **dries out the membrane**; too low causes **flooding** of the GDL.
- **Dead-end vs. flow-through anode operation** — low velocities permit **hydrogen recirculation** with a pump, recovering unused H₂.
- **Cold-start performance** — high inlet gas velocity is intentionally used to **purge liquid water** from channels during sub-zero startup.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: General Expression for Cathode Gas Velocity

We derive the canonical expression that the **KTU examiner expects** in any 14-mark Part B question on gas velocity.

### Given Conditions (Standard KTU Board Assumptions)
- Operating current density: $j$ (A/m²)
- Active cell area: $A_{cell}$ (m²)
- Number of cells in stack: $N_c$
- Number of parallel flow channels per cell: $N_{ch}$
- Channel cross-section: width $w$ × depth $h$
- Operating temperature: $T$, pressure: $P$
- Cathode stoichiometry: $\zeta_c$
- Oxygen mole fraction in air: $y_{O_2} = 0.21$

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Total stack current:**

$$I = j \times A_{cell} \times N_c$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric oxygen consumption rate (per the ORR half-reaction, 4 e⁻ per O₂):**

$$\dot{n}_{O_2, stoich} = \frac{I}{4F} = \frac{j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot N_c}{4F}$$

**Step 3 — Apply cathode stoichiometry $\zeta_c$ to find inlet air molar flow:**

$$\dot{n}_{air, inlet} = \zeta_c \cdot \frac{\dot{n}_{O_2, stoich}}{y_{O_2}} = \zeta_c \cdot \frac{j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot N_c}{4F \cdot y_{O_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert molar flow to volumetric flow using the Ideal Gas Equation $PV = nRT$:**

$$Q_{in} = \frac{\dot{n}_{air, inlet} \cdot R \cdot T}{P} = \frac{\zeta_c \cdot j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot N_c \cdot R \cdot T}{4F \cdot y_{O_2} \cdot P}$$

**Step 5 — Distribute the volumetric flow uniformly across all parallel channels. Total channel area:**

$$A_{ch, total} = N_c \cdot N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h$$

**Step 6 — Compute the average gas velocity (volumetric flow / total channel cross-section):**

$$v_{gas} = \frac{Q_{in}}{A_{ch, total}}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 4 and 5:

$$v_{gas} = \frac{\zeta_c \cdot j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot N_c \cdot R \cdot T}{4F \cdot y_{O_2} \cdot P \cdot N_c \cdot N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h}$$

The $N_c$ cancels out (an important result — velocity is *per cell* independent of stack size):

$$v_{gas} = \frac{\zeta_c \cdot j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot R \cdot T}{4F \cdot y_{O_2} \cdot P \cdot N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h}$$

**Final Boxed Result (Canonical KTU Form):**

$$\boxed{\,v_{gas} = \dfrac{\zeta_c \cdot j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot R \cdot T}{4 F \cdot y_{O_2} \cdot P \cdot N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h}\,}$$

### Conversion Logic for Each Step
- *Step 1 → 2:* Faraday's law relates charge transfer to molar reaction.
- *Step 2 → 3:* Multiplying by $\zeta_c / y_{O_2}$ accounts for excess air and the fact that only 21% of air is oxygen.
- *Step 3 → 4:* Ideal gas law translates moles to volume at operating $T$, $P$.
- *Step 4 → 5:* Continuity — the gas fills all $N_c \times N_{ch}$ channels in parallel.
- *Step 5 → 6:* Definition of average linear velocity (flow rate / area).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A single PEMFC operates at $j = 1.0 \text{ A/cm}^2 = 10{,}000 \text{ A/m}^2$, $A_{cell} = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$, $T = 353 \text{ K}$, $P = 101325 \text{ Pa}$, $\zeta_c = 2.5$, $N_{ch} = 30$, $w = h = 1 \text{ mm} = 0.001 \text{ m}$. Calculate the gas velocity at the cathode inlet.

**Solution:**

First compute the numerator: $\zeta_c \cdot j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot R \cdot T$

$$\zeta_c \cdot j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot R \cdot T = 2.5 \times 10000 \times 0.01 \times 8.314 \times 353$$

Computing stepwise:

$$2.5 \times 10000 = 25000$$
$$25000 \times 0.01 = 250$$
$$250 \times 8.314 = 2078.5$$
$$2078.5 \times 353 = 733{,}710.5 \text{ (units: J·A/m²·m² = W·s = J)}$$

Now compute the denominator: $4F \cdot y_{O_2} \cdot P \cdot N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h$

$$4F = 4 \times 96485 = 385940$$
$$y_{O_2} = 0.21$$
$$P = 101325$$
$$N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h = 30 \times 0.001 \times 0.001 = 3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

Multiplying the denominator terms:

$$385940 \times 0.21 = 81047.4$$
$$81047.4 \times 101325 = 8.2126 \times 10^{9}$$
$$8.2126 \times 10^{9} \times 3 \times 10^{-5} = 246{,}378$$

Finally:

$$v_{gas} = \frac{733{,}710.5}{246{,}378} \approx 2.978 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_{gas} \approx 2.98 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This value lies comfortably in the **2–15 m/s** engineering design window for PEMFC cathode channels. A value of ~3 m/s indicates adequate reactant supply with acceptable parasitic blower load.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Gas Velocity Computation

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *causal chain* of parameters that govern the gas-velocity calculation, from electrochemical demand to final velocity output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cell Current Density j] --> B[Faradays Law]
    C[Reactant Species H2 or O2] --> B
    D[Stoichiometric Ratio zeta] --> E[Inlet Molar Flow n_dot]
    B --> E
    F[Operating Temperature T] --> G[Ideal Gas Law]
    H[Operating Pressure P] --> G
    E --> G
    G --> I[Volumetric Flow Rate Q_in]
    I --> J[Channel Geometry w h N_ch]
    J --> K[GAS VELOCITY v_gas]
    I --> K
    L[Humidity RH] --> M[Gas Composition Correction]
    M --> G
    N[Pressure Drop DeltaP] --> O[Parasitic Pump Power]
    K --> N
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Gas Flow Path Through a Single PEMFC Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Humidified H2 Inlet] --> MA[Anode Channel]
    IN2[Humidified Air Inlet] --> MC[Cathode Channel]
    MA --> GDA[Gas Diffusion Layer GDL]
    MC --> GDC[Gas Diffusion Layer GDL]
    GDA --> CL1[Catalyst Layer Anode]
    GDC --> CL2[Catalyst Layer Cathode]
    CL1 --> MEM[Polymer Membrane Nafion]
    CL2 --> MEM
    MEM --> HPLUS[Proton Hopping H plus]
    HPLUS --> CL2
    CL1 --> EA[Anode Exhaust H2O]
    CL2 --> EC[Cathode Exhaust H2O plus unused air]
    EA --> VA[Velocity Profile Parabolic]
    EC --> VC[Velocity Profile Parabolic]
    VA --> OUT1[Recycle or Vent]
    VC --> OUT2[Recycle or Vent]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Selecting Operating Gas Velocity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Design Goal] --> Q1{Is it PEMFC?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{High Power Density Target?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Choose high v_gas 8 to 15 m per s]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Choose moderate v_gas 3 to 6 m per s]
    Q1 -- No --> Q5[SOFC or other]
    Q5 --> Q6[Typically lower v_gas due to high T]
    Q3 --> R1[Check pressure drop budget]
    Q4 --> R1
    Q6 --> R1
    R1 -- Acceptable --> END[Finalize Design]
    R1 -- Excessive --> Q7[Increase N_ch or w h]
    Q7 --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `GDA`, `Q1`) and contain no reserved keywords. Node labels are plain uppercase text with no embedded formatting, in compliance with the Mermaid safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define gas velocity in the context of a PEM fuel cell. State the standard operating range for cathode gas velocity in a typical automotive fuel cell stack.**

**Model Answer:**
Gas velocity in a PEMFC is the average linear speed of the reactant gas (hydrogen or air) as it flows through the flow-field channels of the bipolar plate, defined as the inlet volumetric flow rate divided by the total cross-sectional area of the channels. For a typical automotive PEMFC stack (e.g., 80–120 kW), the cathode gas velocity is maintained in the range of **5 to 15 m/s** to balance reactant delivery against parasitic pumping losses.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is the stoichiometric ratio $\zeta_c$ on the cathode side of a PEMFC typically higher than the anode stoichiometric ratio $\zeta_a$?**

**Model Answer:**
The cathode stoichiometric ratio $\zeta_c$ (typically 2.0–3.0) is higher than the anode ratio $\zeta_a$ (1.1–1.2) because:
1. The **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** is kinetically sluggish compared to the hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR), requiring a higher local reactant concentration.
2. **Nitrogen dilution** from air reduces the effective partial pressure of O₂ at the catalyst.
3. **Water flooding** of the cathode GDL is mitigated by high air flow, ensuring liquid water is continuously swept out of the channels.
4. **Concentration gradients** along the channel are more severe at the cathode, demanding excess flow to maintain uniform current distribution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the average gas velocity in the cathode flow channels of a PEMFC stack in terms of current density, channel geometry, and operating conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PEMFC stack consists of **100 cells**, each with an active area of **250 cm²** and **40 parallel serpentine channels** of width **1.5 mm** and depth **1.0 mm**. The stack operates at $j = 1.2 \text{ A/cm}^2$, $T = 343 \text{ K}$, $P = 1.5 \text{ atm}$ ($\approx 151987.5 \text{ Pa}$), and $\zeta_c = 2.5$. Calculate the cathode gas velocity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
Refer to the **exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1** above. The final expression is:

$$v_{gas} = \frac{\zeta_c \cdot j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot R \cdot T}{4 F \cdot y_{O_2} \cdot P \cdot N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h}$$

The student should present all 6 derivation steps with the conversion logic, showing clearly:
- Faraday's law → molar consumption.
- Stoichiometry → inlet molar flow.
- Ideal gas law → volumetric flow.
- Channel area distribution → linear velocity.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
- $j = 1.2 \text{ A/cm}^2 = 12000 \text{ A/m}^2$
- $A_{cell} = 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.025 \text{ m}^2$
- $N_{ch} = 40$
- $w = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0015 \text{ m}$
- $h = 1.0 \text{ mm} = 0.001 \text{ m}$
- $T = 343 \text{ K}$
- $P = 151987.5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\zeta_c = 2.5$, $y_{O_2} = 0.21$, $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

**Numerator:**

$$\zeta_c \cdot j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot R \cdot T = 2.5 \times 12000 \times 0.025 \times 8.314 \times 343$$

Step-by-step:
- $2.5 \times 12000 = 30000$
- $30000 \times 0.025 = 750$
- $750 \times 8.314 = 6235.5$
- $6235.5 \times 343 = 2{,}138{,}776.5$

**Denominator:**

$$4F \cdot y_{O_2} \cdot P \cdot N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h = 4 \times 96485 \times 0.21 \times 151987.5 \times 40 \times 0.0015 \times 0.001$$

Step-by-step:
- $4 \times 96485 = 385940$
- $385940 \times 0.21 = 81047.4$
- $81047.4 \times 151987.5 = 1.2318 \times 10^{10}$
- $40 \times 0.0015 \times 0.001 = 6 \times 10^{-5}$
- Final: $1.2318 \times 10^{10} \times 6 \times 10^{-5} = 739{,}080$

**Final division:**

$$v_{gas} = \frac{2{,}138{,}776.5}{739{,}080} \approx 2.89 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_{gas} \approx 2.89 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

This falls in the moderate design range, indicating proper channel sizing for the specified current density.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the factors that influence the choice of operating gas velocity in a PEMFC. Discuss the trade-off between high and low gas velocities with reference to mass transport and pressure drop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single PEMFC with an active area of **50 cm²** operates with **20 parallel straight channels**, each of cross-section **1 mm × 1 mm**. The cell delivers **30 A** at a cathode stoichiometry of **2.0**, $T = 353 \text{ K}$, and $P = 2 \text{ atm}$ ($\approx 202650 \text{ Pa}$). Estimate: (i) the volumetric flow rate of air at the inlet, and (ii) the average gas velocity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
The choice of gas velocity is influenced by:

1. **Current density demand** — higher $j$ demands more reactants per second, raising required velocity.
2. **Channel geometry** — smaller cross-section $w \times h$ increases velocity for the same flow.
3. **Operating pressure** — higher $P$ reduces required volumetric flow (and hence velocity).
4. **Temperature** — higher $T$ increases volumetric flow (and velocity).
5. **Water management strategy** — flooding risk dictates minimum velocity; membrane drying risk dictates maximum velocity.
6. **Parasitic power budget** — high velocity increases blower/compressor power.

**Trade-off discussion:**
- **Too low velocity:** Concentration of reactants drops along the channel → **mass-transport overpotential rises**, especially under the ribs. Water accumulates in GDL pores → **flooding**, voltage drop.
- **Too high velocity:** Reynolds number rises → turbulent flow and channel erosion risk. **Pressure drop $\Delta P$** across the cell increases quadratically with velocity (Poiseuille law: $\Delta P \propto v_{gas}$ for laminar, $\propto v^2$ for turbulent). Blower power $P_{blower} \propto \Delta P \cdot Q$ rises sharply, **reducing net system efficiency**.

**Optimum:** Usually in the range **3–10 m/s** for automotive PEMFCs.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
- $A_{cell} = 50 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.005 \text{ m}^2$
- $N_{ch} = 20$
- $w = h = 1 \text{ mm} = 0.001 \text{ m}$
- $I = 30 \text{ A}$
- $\zeta_c = 2.0$, $T = 353 \text{ K}$, $P = 202650 \text{ Pa}$, $y_{O_2} = 0.21$

**Part (i): Volumetric flow rate of inlet air**

Step 1: Stoichiometric O₂ consumption:

$$\dot{n}_{O_2} = \frac{I}{4F} = \frac{30}{4 \times 96485} = \frac{30}{385940} = 7.772 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol/s}$$

Step 2: Inlet air molar flow:

$$\dot{n}_{air} = \frac{\zeta_c \cdot \dot{n}_{O_2}}{y_{O_2}} = \frac{2.0 \times 7.772 \times 10^{-5}}{0.21} = 7.402 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s}$$

Step 3: Volumetric flow rate from ideal gas law:

$$Q_{in} = \frac{\dot{n}_{air} \cdot R \cdot T}{P} = \frac{7.402 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.314 \times 353}{202650}$$

- Numerator: $7.402 \times 10^{-4} \times 8.314 = 6.155 \times 10^{-3}$
- $6.155 \times 10^{-3} \times 353 = 2.1728$
- Denominator: $202650$
- $Q_{in} = 1.072 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

$$\boxed{Q_{in} \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 10.72 \text{ mL/s}}$$

**Part (ii): Average gas velocity**

$$A_{ch, total} = N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h = 20 \times 0.001 \times 0.001 = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

$$v_{gas} = \frac{Q_{in}}{A_{ch, total}} = \frac{1.072 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.536 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_{gas} \approx 0.54 \text{ m/s}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** are the most common deduction (1–2 marks lost). Always convert $j$ from A/cm² to A/m² and $A_{cell}$ from cm² to m² **before** substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by $y_{O_2} = 0.21$** when computing air (not pure O₂) flow results in a velocity ~4.76× too low — a full-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing $N_c$ (stack cell count) with $N_{ch}$ (channels per cell)**. The stack count **cancels out** in the velocity equation; the only relevant number is $N_{ch}$.
> 4. **Not stating the Ideal Gas assumption** explicitly costs 1 mark. Always write: *"Assuming ideal gas behavior at operating T, P..."*
> 5. **Pressure unit confusion:** KTU problems frequently give $P$ in **atm** — convert to **Pa** (multiply by 101325) before substituting into SI equations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Gas Velocity in PEMFCs**

- 🔹 **Definition:** $v_{gas} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{A_{channel}}$ — average linear speed of reactant gas in bipolar-plate flow channels.

- 🔹 **Faraday's Law Core:**
  - H₂: $\dot{n} = I / (2F)$
  - O₂: $\dot{n} = I / (4F)$

- 🔹 **Air (not pure O₂):** Always divide by $y_{O_2} = 0.21$ when computing inlet air molar flow.

- 🔹 **Stoichiometry:** $\zeta_a = 1.1\text{–}1.2$, $\zeta_c = 2.0\text{–}3.0$. Cathode always higher than anode.

- 🔹 **Ideal Gas Conversion:** $Q = \dfrac{\zeta \cdot \dot{n} \cdot R \cdot T}{P}$ — units of $P$ **must be Pa**, $T$ in **K**.

- 🔹 **Canonical KTU Velocity Formula:**
  $$v_{gas} = \frac{\zeta_c \cdot j \cdot A_{cell} \cdot R \cdot T}{4 F \cdot y_{O_2} \cdot P \cdot N_{ch} \cdot w \cdot h}$$

- 🔹 **Stack size cancels:** $N_c$ (number of cells in stack) drops out of the velocity expression — a frequently-tested conceptual point.

- 🔹 **Typical Design Range:** $v_{gas} \approx 2\text{–}15 \text{ m/s}$ for automotive PEMFC channels.

- 🔹 **Trade-offs:**
  - *Low $v_{gas}$* → flooding, mass-transport loss, concentration gradients.
  - *High $v_{gas}$* → high $\Delta P$, large parasitic blower power, membrane drying.

- 🔹 **Reynolds Check:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v_{gas} D_h}{\mu}$. For typical PEMFC channels, $Re \approx 50\text{–}500$ → **laminar flow**, parabolic velocity profile.

- 🔹 **Pressure Drop (Poiseuille):** $\Delta P \propto \mu L v_{gas} / D_h^2$ — increases linearly with velocity in laminar regime.

- 🔹 **Always state assumptions:** Steady state, isothermal, ideal gas, fully-developed flow, uniform current distribution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pressure # Module 3 | Pressure ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Polymer Electrolyte Membrane Fuel Cells (PEMFCs)** and related electrochemical energy conversion systems, **Pressure** is defined as the thermodynamic intensive variable representing the force exerted per unit area by the reactant gases (hydrogen at the anode and oxygen/air at the cathode) on the catalyst layer and across the gas diffusion layers (GDLs) and bipolar plate flow channels. For a fuel cell operating at a steady-state condition, pressure is categorized into three primary variants: 1. **Operating Pressure ($P_{op}$):** The absolute thermodynamic pressure at which the fuel cell stack is maintained, typically expressed in **atm**, **bar**, or **Pa** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 0.9869 \text{ atm}$). 2. **Partial Pressure ($p_i$):** The pressure contributed by an individual gaseous species $i$ in a gas mixture, governed by Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures: $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$. 3. **Pressure Drop ($\Delta P$):** The differential pressure loss experienced by the reacting gas as it flows through the inlet manifold, flow channels, and GDL, generally calculated as $\Delta P = P_{inlet} - P_{outlet}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pressure is the *single most dominant operational variable* in fuel cell thermodynamics. A higher operating pressure increases the open-circuit voltage (OCV) via the **Nernst equation**, but it also increases the **parasitic load** on the compressor, reducing net system efficiency. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker & Lung" Model To build intuition, consider two real-world analogies: - **The Pressure Cooker Analogy:** Imagine cooking rice in an open pot versus a sealed pressure cooker. At sea level (1 atm), water boils at 100 °C. Inside a pressure cooker operating at 2 atm, water boils at ~120 °C because the elevated pressure forces more energetic collisions. Similarly, in a fuel cell, raising the operating pressure from 1 atm to 3 atm **dramatically accelerates the electrochemical reaction rate** at the platinum catalyst, raising cell voltage by ~30–45 mV per unit pressure increase. - **The Lung / Breathing Analogy:** A fuel cell stack "breathes" hydrogen and oxygen. The **bipolar plate flow channels** act like the human bronchial tubes, and the **gas diffusion layer (GDL)** acts like the alveoli. Just as a constricted airway (high $\Delta P$) makes breathing labored (parasitic compressor work), a poorly designed flow field in a fuel cell causes pressure losses that starve the catalyst of reactants. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention):** All thermodynamic calculations in fuel cells use **STP = 1 atm (1.01325 bar), 298.15 K**. The universal gas constant is $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ and Faraday's constant is $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Role of Pressure in Fuel Cell Performance Pressure influences the fuel cell system at **three hierarchical levels**: (i) the electrochemical reaction thermodynamics (Nernst), (ii) the mass-transport kinetics (concentration overpotential), and (iii) the auxiliary balance-of-plant (BoP) components such as the air compressor. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown **Step 1 — Thermodynamic Effect (Nernst Boost):** Higher reactant partial pressures directly raise the reversible cell potential by enriching the reactant activity at the catalyst surface. This is mathematically captured by the logarithmic term in the Nernst equation. **Step 2 — Kinetic Effect (Exchange Current Density):** The exchange current density $i_0$ is empirically dependent on pressure. For PEMFCs operating at the cathode, $i_{0,c} \propto P_{O_2}^{\gamma}$, where $\gamma \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$. Higher pressure shifts the Tafel curve, reducing activation overpotential. **Step 3 — Mass Transport Effect (Concentration Loss Mitigation):** At high current densities, the local partial pressure of oxygen at the catalyst layer drops due to consumption and limited diffusion. Operating at higher stack pressure maintains a steeper diffusion gradient, mitigating concentration losses. **Step 4 — System-Level Penalty (Compressor Parasitic Work):** A pressurized system requires an air compressor (or blower). The compressor work $W_{comp}$ scales roughly as: $$W_{comp} \propto \left(\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\right) \cdot \dot{n}_{air} \cdot R \cdot T_{in} \left[\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$$ This parasitic power reduces the **net stack efficiency**. Hence, an optimum operating pressure exists (typically 2–3 atm for automotive PEMFCs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Trade-off:** Pressurization boosts cell voltage but consumes auxiliary power. KTU problems frequently ask the student to compute the *net system efficiency* after subtracting compressor work. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Description / Variable Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{Nernst} = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln(p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2})$ | Pressure-dependent Nernst voltage (pure product water, $\gamma_{H_2O}=1$) | V | | 2 | $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ | Partial pressure from mole fraction (Dalton's Law) | Pa | | 3 | $\Delta E_P = \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left(\frac{P_{op}}{P_{std}}\right)$ | Pressure gain over standard conditions (binary gas ratio) | V | | 4 | $\Delta P_{channel} = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho \bar{u}^2}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach pressure drop in flow channel | Pa | | 5 | $D_h = \frac{4A_c}{P_w}$ | Hydraulic diameter of rectangular channel | m | | 6 | $\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{n}_{air} C_p T_{in} \left[\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Isentropic compressor work | W | | 7 | $\eta_F = \frac{V_{cell} \cdot I - \dot{W}_{comp}}{V_{cell} \cdot I}$ | Net fuel cell efficiency with parasitic loss | dimensionless | | 8 | $i_{0,c} = i_{0,c}^\circ \cdot \left(\frac{P_{O_2}}{P_{O_2}^\circ}\right)^{\gamma}$ | Pressure-corrected cathodic exchange current density | A/m² | | 9 | $P_{inlet} = P_{outlet} + \Delta P_{channel} + \Delta P_{GDL} + \Delta P_{manifold}$ | Overall system pressure balance | Pa | | 10 | $\gamma_{ideal} = 1.4$ (air), $C_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K) | Standard air thermodynamic constants | — | > [!NOTE] > In all equations, **absolute pressure** must be used. Always convert gauge readings using $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Nernst Equation Incorporating Pressure The half-reactions for a hydrogen fuel cell operating with an acidic PEM membrane are: **Anode:** $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ **Cathode:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ **Overall:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$ with $\Delta G^\circ = -237.2$ kJ/mol and $E^\circ = 1.229$ V. Starting from the fundamental Nernst relation for any electrochemical cell: $$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ where $n = 2$ electrons transferred per mole of H₂, and the reaction quotient $Q$ is expressed in terms of **activities** ($a_i$). For ideal gases, $a_i = p_i / P^\circ$ (with $P^\circ = 1$ atm reference). Therefore: $$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}} = \frac{1}{(p_{H_2}/P^\circ)(p_{O_2}/P^\circ)^{1/2}}$$ Substituting and simplifying (since $a_{H_2O} = 1$ for pure liquid water): $$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left[(p_{H_2}/P^\circ) \cdot (p_{O_2}/P^\circ)^{1/2}\right]$$ Now applying the pressure-correction to a generic operating point $(P_{op})$ versus the standard reference pressure $P^\circ = 1$ atm, with stoichiometric gas ratios $y_{H_2} = 1$ (pure H₂ feed) and $y_{O_2} = 0.21$ (air feed): $$p_{H_2} = y_{H_2} P_{op} = P_{op}$$ $$p_{O_2} = y_{O_2} P_{op} = 0.21 \, P_{op}$$ Substituting these into the Nernst equation and using $\ln(P^\circ/P^\circ) = 0$: $$\begin{aligned} E &= E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln\left[\left(\frac{P_{op}}{P^\circ}\right)\left(\frac{0.21 \, P_{op}}{P^\circ}\right)^{1/2}\right] \\ &= E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\left[\ln\left(\frac{P_{op}}{P^\circ}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\ln(0.21) + \frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{P_{op}}{P^\circ}\right)\right] \\ &= E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\left[\frac{3}{2}\ln\left(\frac{P_{op}}{P^\circ}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\ln(0.21)\right] \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Reading the math:** The factor $\tfrac{3}{2}$ originates from the stoichiometric sum (1 mole H₂ + 0.5 mole O₂ = 1.5 moles of gas). This elegant result shows that **a ten-fold pressure increase raises the Nernst voltage by $\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{2.303RT}{2F} \approx 45$ mV at 298 K** (for air feed), or ~30 mV for pure O₂ feed (factor 1 instead of 1.5). ## 3.2 Derivation II — Pressure Drop in a Rectangular Bipolar Plate Flow Channel Consider a single serpentine channel of rectangular cross-section (width $w$, height $h$, length $L$) carrying humidified air at a mean velocity $\bar{u}$. **Step 1 — Hydraulic diameter:** For a rectangular duct, the wetted perimeter $P_w = 2(w + h)$ and area $A_c = w \cdot h$: $$D_h = \frac{4A_c}{P_w} = \frac{4wh}{2(w+h)} = \frac{2wh}{w+h}$$ **Step 2 — Reynolds number:** Determines the flow regime: $$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{u} D_h}{\mu}$$ If $Re < 2300$, flow is laminar; otherwise, turbulent. For typical PEMFC conditions ($Re \approx 50$–$500$), flow is **laminar**. **Step 3 — Friction factor (laminar):** For fully-developed laminar flow in a rectangular duct: $$f_D = \frac{C}{Re}$$ where $C$ is a geometry constant ($C = 56.91$ for square duct; $C \approx 96$ for shallow $2:1$ aspect ratio channels). **Step 4 — Darcy–Weisbach equation:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta P &= f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho \bar{u}^2}{2} \\ &= \frac{C}{Re} \cdot \frac{L}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho \bar{u}^2}{2} \\ &= \frac{C \mu L \bar{u}}{2 D_h^2} \end{aligned}$$ For a square channel ($w = h$), this simplifies to: $$\Delta P = \frac{28.46 \, \mu L \bar{u}}{w^2}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Pressure on Cell Performance | Operating Pressure (atm) | Theoretical OCV (V) | Practical Voltage @ 0.6 A/cm² (V) | Compressor Penalty (% of gross power) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1.169 | 0.62 | 0 | | 2 | 1.192 | 0.66 | ~8 % | | 3 | 1.205 | 0.69 | ~15 % | | 5 | 1.222 | 0.72 | ~30 % | The above matrix is computed for $T = 353$ K, air feed ($y_{O_2} = 0.21$), and a typical PEMFC polarization curve. > [!TIP] > **Exam Insight (KTU Pattern):** When asked "Why are most automotive fuel cells pressurized to only 2–3 atm instead of 5 atm?", the answer lies in this matrix — beyond 3 atm, the *compassor parasitic penalty grows faster than the Nernst voltage gain*. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pressure Management in a PEMFC System The diagram below maps the pressure flow across all major components in a pressurized fuel cell stack, from ambient air intake to electrochemical reaction site. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Ambient Air
Patm = 101.325 kPa] --> B1[Air Filter and Humidifier
Removal of particulates and moisture addition] B1 --> C1[Air Compressor
Isentropic Compression to P op] C1 --> D1[Pressure Regulator
Drops excess pressure to target stack pressure] D1 --> E1[Cathode Inlet Manifold
Distributes air to N cells] E1 --> F1[Bipolar Plate Flow Channels
Distributes gas across active area] F1 --> G1[Gas Diffusion Layer GDL
Final diffusion to catalyst] G1 --> H1[Cathode Catalyst Layer
Electrochemical ORR reaction] H1 --> I1[Pressure Drop across Stack
Delta P total = sum of all component drops] I1 --> J1[Cathode Outlet Manifold
Collects depleted air and product water] J1 --> K1[Back Pressure Valve
Maintains stack pressure setpoint] K1 --> L1[Exhaust to atmosphere] M1[Hydrogen Tank
High pressure storage 350 to 700 bar] --> N1[Hydrogen Regulator
Reduces to stack operating pressure] N1 --> O1[Anode Inlet Manifold
Distributes H2 to N cells] O1 --> P1[Anode Flow Channels and GDL
Delivers H2 to anode catalyst] P1 --> Q1[Anode Catalyst Layer
HOR reaction occurs here] Q1 --> R1[Anode Recirculation Loop
Recovers unreacted H2 for efficiency] H1 -. Pressure balance .-> Q1 C1 -. Parasitic power draw .-> S1[BOP Power Consumption
Reduces net system efficiency] L1 -. Exhaust heat and water .-> S1 ``` ## 4.2 Pressure Drop Distribution Schematic Across Stack Components ```mermaid flowchart LR Pin[Stack Inlet Pressure
Pin = 2.5 bar] --> ch1[Flow Channel Drop
Delta P ch approx 5 to 15 percent] ch1 --> gdl[GDL Drop
Delta P GDL approx 2 to 5 percent] gdl --> cl[CL Local Drop
Delta P CL less than 1 percent] cl --> Pout[Stack Outlet Pressure
Pout approx 2.3 bar] Pin2[Inlet Manifold] -->|Delta P m1| Pin Pout -->|Delta P m2| OutM[Outlet Manifold] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** In a well-designed PEMFC, the **flow channels account for 60–80 %** of total pressure drop. The GDL contributes 10–20 %, and manifolds ~5–10 %. The catalyst layer itself contributes negligible pressure drop (the reaction occurs in solution phase at the nano-scale). ## 4.3 Pressure vs Voltage Performance Envelope ```mermaid graph LR A[Operating Pressure
P op] -->|Logarithmic gain via Nernst| B[Thermodynamic Voltage Boost] A -->|Linear gain in i0| C[Kinetic Activation Reduction] A -->|Gradient boost| D[Mass Transport Improvement] A -->|Cubic growth| E[Parasitic Compressor Penalty] B --> F[Net Cell Voltage] C --> F D --> F E -->|Subtracted| G[Net System Efficiency] F --> G ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define partial pressure of a reactant gas in a fuel cell. Why is partial pressure (and not total pressure) the governing parameter in the Nernst equation?** **Model Answer:** Partial pressure of a gas $i$ in a mixture is defined as the pressure that gas $i$ would exert if it alone occupied the volume of the mixture, given mathematically by **Dalton's Law**: $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$, where $y_i$ is the mole fraction. It is the partial pressure (not total pressure) that governs the Nernst equation because the **activity** of an ideal gas species in an electrochemical reaction is given by its **fugacity**, which is proportional to its partial pressure ($a_i = p_i / P^\circ$). Only the reacting species (H₂ at anode, O₂ at cathode) participate in the half-reactions; inert species like N₂ in air do not directly affect the thermodynamic driving force. Thus, the Nernst equation takes the form $E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{1/2})$. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the effect of operating pressure on the activation overpotential of a PEMFC.** **Model Answer:** The cathodic exchange current density $i_{0,c}$ depends on the local oxygen partial pressure through the empirical relation $i_{0,c} = i_{0,c}^\circ (p_{O_2}/p_{O_2}^\circ)^{\gamma}$, where $\gamma \approx 0.5$–$0.7$. As operating pressure increases, $p_{O_2}$ rises proportionally, which raises $i_{0,c}$. From the Tafel equation for activation overpotential: $$\eta_{act} = \frac{RT}{\alpha_c n F} \ln\left(\frac{i}{i_{0,c}}\right)$$ a higher $i_{0,c}$ **reduces the activation overpotential logarithmically**. Practically, pressurizing a PEMFC from 1 atm to 3 atm can reduce cathode activation loss by ~20–30 mV at typical operating current densities. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Unit consistency:** Always convert pressures to **atm or bar** before applying the Nernst equation (the reference state is 1 atm). Mixing Pa with atm yields wrong answers — a *very common 2-mark deduction*. > 2. **Forgetting stoichiometric coefficient:** The Nernst equation for air feed has a $\tfrac{3}{2}\ln(P)$ term (not just $\ln P$). Missing this costs ~3 marks. > 3. **Absolute vs Gauge pressure:** Use absolute pressure always. Students often forget to add atmospheric pressure to gauge readings. ### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]* **(a)** Derive the pressure-dependent Nernst equation for a PEMFC operating on hydrogen and air, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PEMFC operates at 353 K and 3 atm with air ($\gamma_{O_2} = 0.21$) at the cathode and pure H₂ at the anode. Calculate: (i) the theoretical open-circuit voltage (OCV), (ii) the voltage gain if pressure is raised from 1 atm to 3 atm, and (iii) the percent increase in net power density if the parasitic compressor loss is 10 % of gross power. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-A(a): **Assumptions:** 1. Reactant and product gases behave ideally. 2. Liquid water product, so $a_{H_2O} = 1$. 3. Steady-state, isothermal operation. 4. Pressure-independent standard potential at given T. From the overall reaction $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)}$: $$\begin{aligned} E &= E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q \\ Q &= \frac{1}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}} \\ a_i &= p_i / P^\circ \end{aligned}$$ Substituting $n = 2$, $p_{H_2} = P_{op}$, $p_{O_2} = 0.21 \, P_{op}$, $P^\circ = 1$ atm: $$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left[\left(\frac{P_{op}}{P^\circ}\right)\left(\frac{0.21\,P_{op}}{P^\circ}\right)^{1/2}\right]$$ $$\boxed{E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\left[\frac{3}{2}\ln\left(\frac{P_{op}}{P^\circ}\right) + \frac{1}{2}\ln(0.21)\right]}$$ #### Model Solution to Q-A(b): **Given:** $T = 353$ K, $P_{op} = 3$ atm, $E^\circ_{(353K)} \approx 1.213$ V (temperature-corrected). **Step (i) — OCV calculation:** $$\begin{aligned} E &= 1.213 + \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485}\left[\frac{3}{2}\ln(3) + \frac{1}{2}\ln(0.21)\right] \\ &= 1.213 + 0.01521\left[1.6479 - 0.7849\right] \\ &= 1.213 + 0.01521 \times 0.8630 \\ &= 1.213 + 0.01313 \\ &\approx \boxed{1.226 \text{ V}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step (ii) — Voltage gain from 1 atm to 3 atm:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta E &= \frac{RT}{2F} \cdot \frac{3}{2}\ln(3) \\ &= \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} \times 1.5 \times 1.0986 \\ &= 0.01521 \times 1.6479 \\ &\approx \boxed{0.0251 \text{ V} \approx 25.1 \text{ mV}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step (iii) — Net power density:** Assume operating current density $i = 0.6 \text{ A/cm}^2$ and $V_{cell,1atm} = 0.62$ V, $V_{cell,3atm} = 0.66$ V. Gross power density at 3 atm: $P_{gross} = V_{cell} \times i = 0.66 \times 0.6 = 0.396 \text{ W/cm}^2$. Net power (after 10% parasitic loss): $P_{net} = 0.396 \times (1 - 0.10) = 0.3564 \text{ W/cm}^2$. Compared to 1 atm baseline: $P_{gross,1} = 0.62 \times 0.6 = 0.372 \text{ W/cm}^2$. $$\text{% increase} = \frac{0.3564 - 0.372}{0.372} \times 100\% \approx -4.2\%$$ > **Note:** Even with the voltage gain, if the compressor penalty is too high, **net power can decrease** — this is a key KTU teaching point on system-level optimization. --- ### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — *[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]* **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how reactant gases are distributed in a PEMFC stack and discuss the major sources of pressure drop. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A serpentine flow channel of a bipolar plate has dimensions: width $w = 1$ mm, height $h = 1$ mm, length $L = 1$ m. Humidified air at $T = 353$ K ($\mu = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1.0$ kg/m³) flows at mean velocity $\bar{u} = 5$ m/s. Calculate: (i) the Reynolds number and flow regime, (ii) the friction factor, and (iii) the pressure drop across the channel. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-B(a): In a PEMFC stack, hydrogen enters the **anode inlet manifold**, is distributed across parallel cells through **anode flow channels** (machined or stamped into the bipolar plate), and reaches the **anode gas diffusion layer (GDL)** before reacting at the catalyst. Simultaneously, air enters the cathode through a separate manifold and flow field. After reaction, depleted gases and product water exit through the outlet manifold, where a **back-pressure valve** maintains the stack setpoint. **Major sources of pressure drop ($\Delta P_{total}$):** $$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{manifold,in} + \Delta P_{channel} + \Delta P_{GDL} + \Delta P_{CL} + \Delta P_{manifold,out}$$ | Component | Typical Contribution (%) | |---|---| | Inlet/Outlet manifolds | 5 – 15 % | | Flow channels | 60 – 80 % | | Gas diffusion layer | 10 – 20 % | | Catalyst layer | < 1 % | Pressure drop is minimized by choosing parallel flow fields over serpentine (lower path length), larger channel cross-sections, and high-permeability GDL materials (e.g., Toray carbon paper). #### Model Solution to Q-B(b): **Step (i) — Hydraulic diameter and Reynolds number:** $$D_h = \frac{2wh}{w+h} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1 \text{ mm}$$ $$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{u} D_h}{\mu} = \frac{1.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 200$$ Since $Re = 200 < 2300$, the flow is **laminar**. ✓ **Step (ii) — Friction factor:** For a square duct in laminar flow, the Fanning friction factor constant is $C = 56.91$ (using $f_{Fanning} \cdot Re = C$, while Darcy $f_D = 4 \cdot f_{Fanning}$). Taking $f_D \cdot Re = 96$ (Darcy form for $1:1$ square duct from standard tables): $$f_D = \frac{96}{Re} = \frac{96}{200} = 0.48$$ **Step (iii) — Pressure drop:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta P &= f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho \bar{u}^2}{2} \\ &= 0.48 \times \frac{1.0}{10^{-3}} \times \frac{1.0 \times 25}{2} \\ &= 0.48 \times 1000 \times 12.5 \\ &= \boxed{6000 \text{ Pa} = 0.06 \text{ bar}} \end{aligned}$$ This is well within typical design limits ($\Delta P_{channel} < 0.2$ bar), confirming the design is feasible. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Partial Pressure vs Total Pressure:** The Nernst equation uses **partial pressures** ($p_i$), not the total stack pressure. Always apply **Dalton's Law** first. - **Standard Reference State:** $P^\circ = 1$ atm. Absolute pressure must be used throughout — never gauge pressure. - **Stoichiometric Coefficient Effect:** For air feed ($\tfrac{1}{2}$ O₂), the log term carries a $\tfrac{3}{2}$ factor. For pure O₂ feed, the factor is just $1$. - **Pressure Drops Are Inevitable:** Even an ideally designed stack loses 5–15 % of inlet pressure across channels and GDL. Use the **Darcy–Weisbach** equation for channels. - **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher $P_{op}$ → higher Nernst voltage (good) BUT higher compressor work (bad). **Optimum is 2–3 atm** for most automotive PEMFC systems. - **Laminar vs Turbulent:** PEMFC flow channels are typically laminar ($Re < 2300$). The Darcy friction factor is $f_D = C/Re$ where $C$ depends on channel aspect ratio. - **Compressor Penalty Formula:** $\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{n}_{air} C_p T_{in}\left[\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$. This is the *hidden cost* of pressurization. - **Hydraulic Diameter of Square Channel:** $D_h = w$ (equals the side length when $w = h$). Useful shortcut for exams. - **Why Hydrogen is Fed Pure:** Anode gas is essentially pure H₂ ($y_{H_2} \approx 1$) because recirculation purges inerts and water vapor, maximizing partial pressure. - **Air Feed Penalty:** Air is only 21 % O₂, so cathode suffers a permanent voltage loss of $\frac{RT}{4F}\ln(0.21) \approx -43$ mV at STP — a strong motivation to use pure O₂ in space applications.

topics.title: Polarization Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Polarization Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **polarization curve** (also termed the *current–voltage characteristic* or *I–V curve*) of a fuel cell is the graphical representation that depicts the relationship between the operating **cell voltage ($V_{cell}$)** and the **current density ($j$)** drawn from the electrochemical device under steady-state, isothermal operating conditions. Mathematically, it is expressed as the deviation of the working cell potential from the reversible thermodynamic potential as a function of the load current.

$$V_{cell} = E_{rev} - \eta_{a,anode} - \eta_{a,cathode} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

where each $\eta$ term represents an irreversible voltage loss (polarization) at a specific location in the cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The polarization curve is the single most important diagnostic tool in fuel cell engineering. It integrates all electrochemical, mass-transport, and ohmic losses into one master plot, making it the foundation for performance evaluation, efficiency analysis, and stack sizing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a heavy cart up a slight incline. The **ideal height** of the cart represents the *reversible thermodynamic potential* ($E_{rev}$ = **1.229 V** for a H₂/O₂ PEMFC). In a perfect, frictionless world, the cart would reach the top with no energy lost.

However, in reality:
- **Friction in the wheels** → represents **activation polarization** (energy needed to "kick-start" the electrochemical reactions at the electrodes).
- **Air resistance / dragging your feet** → represents **ohmic polarization** (resistance to proton and electron flow through the membrane, electrodes, and external circuit).
- **Running out of oxygen at the top of the hill** → represents **concentration polarization** (mass-transport limitation when reactants cannot reach the catalyst sites fast enough).
