> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** System-level design means looking *beyond the stack* — you must account for the auxiliary components (compressors, humidifiers, cooling loops, DC-DC converters) that ultimately determine the net usable power output and system-level efficiency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Engine" Analogy

Imagine a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell system as a **carefully orchestrated restaurant kitchen**:
- **Hydrogen cylinders / reformers** = the pantry (fuel supply).
- **Fuel cell stack** = the chef (converts raw ingredients into the final dish — electricity).
- **Air blower / O2 tank** = the ventilation system (provides fresh oxidant).
- **Cooling radiators + water separators** = the kitchen exhaust (removes heat and steam).
- **Power electronics (DC-DC / DC-AC inverter)** = the waiter (delivers electricity in a usable form to the customer/load).

Just as a chef cannot cook without continuous supply of ingredients, ventilation, and exhaust, a fuel cell stack cannot produce useful power without a complete, well-engineered **system design**.

## 1.3 Why System Design Matters

> [!NOTE]
> A bare fuel cell stack typically operates at only **40–60 % LHV efficiency**. However, once parasitic loads (pumps, blowers, fans, control electronics) are accounted for, the **net system efficiency drops to 35–55 %**. Therefore, the *system designer* — not just the electrochemist — is responsible for the final delivered efficiency.

The design challenge is to maximize:
1. **Net Electrical Efficiency** $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{P_{net}}{P_{fuel,in}}$
2. **Power Density** (W/kg, W/L)
3. **Durability** (operating hours before 10 % degradation)
4. **Cold-start capability** and transient response

while minimizing:
1. Cost (USD/kW)
2. Volume and weight
3. Parasitic auxiliary power consumption

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Expectation)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical PEMFC Value |
|---|---|---|
| Cell voltage (operating) | $V_{cell}$ | 0.6 – 0.75 V |
| Current density | $i$ | 0.5 – 1.5 A/cm² |
| Power density (stack) | $P_{A}$ | 0.4 – 0.8 W/cm² |
| Stack efficiency (LHV) | $\eta_{stack}$ | 50 – 60 % |
| System efficiency (LHV) | $\eta_{sys}$ | 40 – 55 % |
| Operating temperature | $T$ | 60 – 80 °C (PEM) |
| Operating pressure | $P$ | 1 – 3 atm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System-Level Architecture of an H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell

A complete hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell system consists of **four major sub-systems**:

### A. Fuel (Hydrogen) Supply Subsystem
- **Storage:** Compressed gas (350 / 700 bar), liquid H₂ ($-253\,°C$), metal hydrides, or chemical carriers.
- **Pressure regulation:** Two-stage regulator reduces storage pressure to stack inlet pressure (typically 1.2 – 2.5 bar absolute).
- **Humidification:** Prevents membrane dry-out (PEMFC requires inlet RH of 80 – 100 %).
- **Recirculation:** A blower/ejector returns unreacted H₂ from the anode exit to the inlet to improve utilization.

### B. Oxidant (Oxygen/Air) Supply Subsystem
- **Air mode:** A compressor or blower supplies filtered, humidified air. Air is cheap but reduces Nernst voltage due to lower O₂ partial pressure.
- **Pure O₂ mode:** Used in space and submarine applications. Higher performance, but adds cost and weight of O₂ storage.
- **Stoichiometry control:** Air stoichiometry $\lambda_{air}$ is typically 1.5 – 2.5 to ensure uniform O₂ distribution and adequate heat removal.

### C. Thermal Management Subsystem
- Removes the heat generated by irreversible losses (over-potentials).
- **Liquid cooling** (deionized water-glycol mixture) — most common in PEMFC.
- **Air cooling** — used in small stacks (< 1 kW).
- Cooling duty: $Q_{cool} = P_{elec} \times \left(\dfrac{1-\eta_{stack}}{\eta_{stack}}\right)$

### D. Water Management Subsystem
- PEMFC membranes require continuous hydration.
- **External humidifier** (bubble, membrane, or enthalpy wheel).
- **Condenser / water separator** at cathode exit to recover liquid water for re-use.
- **Dead-ended anode mode** with periodic purge to avoid N₂/H₂O accumulation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **high-yield equations** that examiners most frequently test. Master these for the 14-mark derivations.

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^0 = \dfrac{-\Delta G^0}{nF}$ | Standard reversible cell voltage (n = 2) | V |
| 2 | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{0.5})$ | Nernst equation for H₂/O₂ | V |
| 3 | $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{conc} - \eta_{ohm}$ | Actual cell voltage | V |
| 4 | $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Butler-Volmer (Tafel approx.) | V |
| 5 | $\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Ohmic loss (R = area-specific resistance) | V |
| 6 | $P_{stack} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Stack power (N = number of cells) | W |
| 7 | $\eta_{stack} = \dfrac{\mu_{fuel} \cdot V_{cell}}{1.482}$ | Stack efficiency based on LHV (1.482 V) | – |
| 8 | $\mu_{fuel} = \dfrac{m_{H_2,consumed}}{m_{H_2,supplied}}$ | Hydrogen utilization factor | – |
| 9 | $Q_{gen} = P_{stack} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1.482 - V_{cell}}{V_{cell}}\right)$ | Heat generated by stack | W |
| 10 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{stack} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{P_{aux}}{P_{gross}}\right)$ | System efficiency (parasitic correction) | – |
| 11 | $v_{air,stoich} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot 0.21}{0.21} = \lambda$ | Air stoichiometry factor | – |
| 12 | $T_{out} = T_{in} + \dfrac{Q_{gen}}{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air}}$ | Air-cooled outlet temperature | K |

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **1.482 V** in equation (7) is the **thermodynamic voltage equivalent of the LHV of H₂** at standard conditions: $\frac{237.13 \times 10^3}{2 \times 96485} = 1.229\text{ V}$ for HHV, and **1.482 V** when corrected for the latent heat of vaporization (LHV basis).

## 2.3 Why Hydrogen Utilization Matters

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU question: *"Why not use 100 % of the supplied H₂?"* The answer lies in **mass transport limitations** at high utilization: $\mu_{fuel} > 0.95$ causes severe concentration losses, localized heating, and accelerated membrane degradation. Industrial systems target $\mu_{fuel} = 0.80 - 0.90$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell systems are deployed in:
1. **Automotive (FCEVs)** — Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO (700 bar H₂ tanks, ~ 100 kW net).
2. **Backup power** for telecom towers (5 – 50 kW).
3. **Space applications** — Apollo, Space Shuttle (pure cryogenic O₂ + H₂, AFC, or PEMFC).
4. **Submarine AIP** (Air-Independent Propulsion).
5. **Stationary combined heat and power (CHP)** for residential buildings.

Each application demands a **different system-level design tradeoff** — automotive prioritizes power density; stationary prioritizes efficiency and durability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: System Efficiency from Basic Thermodynamics

We begin with the Gibbs free energy change of the hydrogen-oxygen reaction at standard conditions:

$$\Delta G^0 = -237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The maximum reversible electrical work obtainable per mole of H₂ is given by:

$$W_{max} = -\Delta G^0 = 237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Dividing by Faraday's constant $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$ and the number of electrons transferred $n = 2$, we obtain the **thermodynamic standard voltage**:

$$E^0 = \frac{-\Delta G^0}{nF} = \frac{237130}{2 \times 96485} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

Now, the **stack efficiency** (ideal case) is defined as the ratio of actual cell voltage to the **thermodynamic voltage based on LHV**:

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{V_{cell}}{V_{LHV}} = \frac{V_{cell}}{1.482}$$

Including the hydrogen utilization factor $\mu_{fuel}$, the **fuel-based stack efficiency** becomes:

$$\eta_{stack,fuel} = \frac{\mu_{fuel} \cdot V_{cell}}{1.482}$$

The **net system efficiency** accounts for parasitic power consumption by auxiliary components (blower, pump, humidifier, controller). If $P_{gross}$ is the gross DC electrical output and $P_{aux}$ is the total parasitic load:

$$\eta_{sys} = \frac{P_{gross} - P_{aux}}{\dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}$$

Substituting $P_{gross} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ and the relationship $\dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot 2F = I / \mu_{fuel}$:

$$\eta_{sys} = \frac{\mu_{fuel} \cdot V_{cell}}{1.482} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{P_{aux}}{P_{gross}}\right)$$

**Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\eta_{sys} = \frac{\mu_{fuel} \cdot V_{cell}}{1.482} \left(1 - \frac{P_{aux}}{P_{gross}}\right)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Generation and Cooling Requirement

The energy balance on a fuel cell stack at steady state gives:

$$P_{H_2,chem} = P_{elec} + Q_{gen} + Q_{latent}$$

The **latent heat** term corresponds to the difference between HHV and LHV. Using LHV (water leaves as vapor):

$$Q_{gen} = P_{stack} \cdot \left(\frac{1.482 - V_{cell}}{V_{cell}}\right)$$

For a stack delivering 50 kW at an average cell voltage of 0.7 V:

$$Q_{gen} = 50000 \times \left(\frac{1.482 - 0.7}{0.7}\right) = 50000 \times 1.117 = 55,857 \text{ W} \approx 55.9 \text{ kW}$$

This means the cooling system must reject **more heat than the electrical power output** at low cell voltages. This is a critical design constraint.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Air Compressor Power (Parasitic Load)

For an air-supplied PEMFC system, the parasitic power required by the compressor is:

$$P_{comp} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot T_{in}}{\eta_{comp}} \left[\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$$

where $\gamma = 1.4$ for air, $c_{p,air} = 1005 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$, and $\eta_{comp}$ is the isentropic efficiency (typically 0.6 – 0.8).

The required air mass flow for stoichiometry $\lambda$ and current $I$ is:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{\lambda \cdot M_{air}}{4F} \cdot \frac{I}{\chi_{O_2}}$$

For $\lambda = 2.0$, $I = 200 \text{ A}$, $\chi_{O_2} = 0.21$, $M_{air} = 28.97 \text{ g/mol}$:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{2.0 \times 0.02897}{4 \times 96485} \times \frac{200}{0.21}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 1.4303 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/(A·s)} \times 952.38 \text{ A} = 1.362 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} \approx 4.90 \text{ kg/h}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Typical 14-Mark Style Question:** *"A PEMFC stack consists of 80 cells, operates at 0.7 V per cell, and delivers 100 A. Hydrogen utilization is 85 %. The parasitic load is 800 W. Calculate (a) gross DC power, (b) stack efficiency, (c) system efficiency, and (d) heat to be removed."*

### Given:
- $N = 80$ cells
- $V_{cell} = 0.7$ V
- $I = 100$ A
- $\mu_{fuel} = 0.85$
- $P_{aux} = 800$ W

### Solution:

**(a) Gross DC Power:**

$$P_{gross} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I = 80 \times 0.7 \times 100 = 5600 \text{ W}$$

**(b) Stack Efficiency (LHV basis):**

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{\mu_{fuel} \cdot V_{cell}}{1.482} = \frac{0.85 \times 0.7}{1.482} = \frac{0.595}{1.482} = 0.4015 \approx 40.15\%$$

**(c) Net System Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{sys} = \eta_{stack} \times \left(1 - \frac{P_{aux}}{P_{gross}}\right) = 0.4015 \times \left(1 - \frac{800}{5600}\right)$$

$$\eta_{sys} = 0.4015 \times (1 - 0.1429) = 0.4015 \times 0.8571 = 0.3441 \approx 34.41\%$$

**(d) Heat to be Removed:**

$$Q_{gen} = P_{stack} \times \left(\frac{1.482 - V_{cell}}{V_{cell}}\right) = 5600 \times \left(\frac{1.482 - 0.7}{0.7}\right) = 5600 \times 1.117 = 6255 \text{ W}$$

$$Q_{gen} \approx 6.26 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Gross DC Power | 5600 W |
| Stack Efficiency | 40.15 % |
| System Efficiency | 34.41 % |
| Heat to Remove | 6255 W |
| Net Usable Power | 4800 W |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a H₂/O₂ Fuel Cell System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete interactions** between all subsystems of a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell power plant. Each block is a self-contained module, and arrows represent mass, energy, or signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Hydrogen Storage & Supply]
        A1[Compressed H2 Tank 350/700 bar]
        A2[Pressure Regulator Stage 1]
        A3[Pressure Regulator Stage 2]
        A4[Humidifier Membrane Type]
    end

    subgraph B[Fuel Cell Stack PEMFC]
        B1[Anode Flow Plates]
        B2[Membrane Electrode Assembly MEA]
        B3[Cathode Flow Plates]
        B4[End Plates & Tie Rods]
    end

    subgraph C[Oxidant Supply Air/O2]
        C1[Air Filter Inlet]
        C2[Compressor / Blower]
        C3[Mass Flow Controller]
        C4[Cathode Humidifier]
    end

    subgraph D[Thermal Management]
        D1[Coolant Pump]
        D2[Deionized Water Glycol Loop]
        D3[Radiator Heat Exchanger]
        D4[Temperature Sensors]
    end

    subgraph E[Water Management]
        E1[Cathode Exhaust Condenser]
        E2[Water Separator]
        E3[Anode Purge Valve]
        E4[Recirculation Blower]
    end

    subgraph F[Power Electronics & Control]
        F1[DC-DC Boost Converter]
        F2[DC-AC Inverter]
        F3[ECU Controller Unit]
        F4[Load AC/DC]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> B1
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> B3
    B1 --> B2 --> B3
    B2 --> B4
    B1 --> E3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2 --> A4
    E2 --> E4
    B3 --> E1
    B3 --> D2
    D2 --> D1 --> D3
    D4 --> D1
    D4 --> A4
    D4 --> C2
    B4 --> F1 --> F2 --> F4
    F3 --> A3
    F3 --> C2
    F3 --> D1
    F3 --> E4
    F3 --> F1
```

## 4.2 Hydrogen Flow Path — Detailed Mass & Energy Routing

This schematic traces the **hydrogen path** from storage to exhaust, with all conditioning stages.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[H2 Tank 700 bar] --> H2[High Pressure Regulator]
    H2 --> H3[Low Pressure Regulator 1.5 bar]
    H3 --> H4[Solenoid Valve]
    H4 --> H5[Mass Flow Meter]
    H5 --> H6[Anode Inlet Humidifier]
    H6 --> H7[Fuel Cell Stack Anode]
    H7 --> H8[Anode Exhaust H2 + Water Vapor]
    H8 --> H9[Water Separator]
    H9 --> H10[Purge Valve Periodic]
    H9 --> H11[Recirculation Ejector/Blower]
    H11 --> H6
```

## 4.3 Air/Oxidant Path — Detailed Routing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Ambient Air] --> O2[Particulate Filter]
    O2 --> O3[Compressor]
    O3 --> O4[Aftercooler]
    O4 --> O5[MFC Mass Flow Controller]
    O5 --> O6[Cathode Humidifier]
    O6 --> O7[Stack Cathode Inlet]
    O7 --> O8[Stack Cathode Reaction]
    O8 --> O9[Cathode Exhaust H2O + N2 + O2]
    O9 --> O10[Condenser]
    O10 --> O11[Water Recovery Tank]
    O10 --> O12[Exhaust to Ambient]
```

## 4.4 Control & Feedback Loops

The ECU continuously monitors sensor inputs and modulates actuator outputs to maintain safe, efficient operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ECU[Central ECU Controller] --> REG[Pressure Regulator]
    ECU --> CMP[Compressor Speed]
    ECU --> HUM[Humidifier Power]
    ECU --> COOL[Coolant Pump Speed]
    ECU --> CONV[DC-DC Converter Duty Cycle]

    SENS[Temperature Sensor] --> ECU
    SENS2[Pressure Sensor] --> ECU
    SENS3[Voltage Sensor] --> ECU
    SENS4[Current Sensor] --> ECU
    SENS5[Humidity Sensor] --> ECU
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **ECU** is the brain of the system. It uses **PID control loops** to maintain stack temperature within ±2 °C, pressure within ±50 mbar, and humidity within ±5 % RH. KTU students should remember that control strategy directly impacts durability and dynamic response.

## 4.5 State Diagram — Operating Modes of an FCEV System

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Off
    Off --> Startup: Key On
    Startup --> Standby: H2 Pressure OK
    Standby --> Running: Load Demand > Threshold
    Running --> PeakPower: High Load Transient
    PeakPower --> Running: Load Stabilized
    Running --> Standby: No Load Demand
    Running --> Shutdown: Key Off
    Shutdown --> Purge: N2/H2O Removal
    Purge --> Off
    Running --> Fault: Sensor Out of Range
    Fault --> Shutdown
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define the "Balance of Plant" (BOP) in a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell system and list its major sub-components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Balance of Plant (BOP)** refers to all the auxiliary components and subsystems of a fuel cell system that support the operation of the fuel cell stack, but are not part of the electrochemical core itself. It is responsible for fuel delivery, oxidant supply, thermal regulation, water management, and power conditioning.

**Major BOP components (1 mark each):**
1. **Hydrogen supply system:** storage tanks, pressure regulators, humidifier, recirculation blower.
2. **Air/oxidant supply:** air filter, compressor/blower, mass flow controller, cathode humidifier.
3. **Thermal management:** coolant pump, radiator, temperature sensors, cooling fan.
4. **Water management:** condenser, water separator, anode purge valve.
5. **Power electronics & control:** DC-DC converter, DC-AC inverter, ECU.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing sub-systems, 1 mark for naming key components.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional II]** *Why is the net system efficiency of a fuel cell always lower than the stack efficiency? Provide a quantitative expression.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **stack efficiency** considers only the electrochemical conversion efficiency inside the stack, but ignores the power consumed by auxiliary components such as air compressor, coolant pump, humidifier, and electronic controllers. This **parasitic power** is subtracted from the gross electrical output, lowering the net usable efficiency.

**Quantitative expression:**

$$\eta_{sys} = \eta_{stack} \times \left(1 - \frac{P_{aux}}{P_{gross}}\right)$$

Equivalently:

$$\eta_{sys} = \frac{\mu_{fuel} \cdot V_{cell}}{1.482} \left(1 - \frac{P_{aux}}{P_{gross}}\right)$$

A typical PEMFC system loses **5–10 %** absolute efficiency due to parasitic loads. *(1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for typical numerical range.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]** 

> A proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) stack consists of **75 cells** connected in series. The stack operates at an average cell voltage of **0.68 V** and supplies a current of **150 A**. The hydrogen utilization factor is **0.88**, and the total auxiliary power consumed by the BOP is **1.2 kW**.
> 
> **(a)** Calculate the **(i)** gross DC power output, **(ii)** stack efficiency based on LHV, **(iii)** net system efficiency, and **(iv)** the rate of heat generation that must be removed by the cooling system. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** With the aid of a labeled block diagram, explain the **Balance of Plant (BOP)** components of a typical hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell system, and discuss how each subsystem affects the net system efficiency. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Number of cells, N | 75 |
| Cell voltage, $V_{cell}$ | 0.68 V |
| Current, I | 150 A |
| H₂ utilization, $\mu_{fuel}$ | 0.88 |
| Parasitic power, $P_{aux}$ | 1.2 kW |
| LHV voltage equivalent, $V_{LHV}$ | 1.482 V |

**(i) Gross DC Power Output (2 marks):**

$$P_{gross} = N \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I = 75 \times 0.68 \times 150$$

Step-by-step multiplication:
- $75 \times 0.68 = 51.0$ W (per cell equivalent)
- $51.0 \times 150 = 7650$ W

$$P_{gross} = 7650 \text{ W} = 7.65 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Stack Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{\mu_{fuel} \cdot V_{cell}}{V_{LHV}} = \frac{0.88 \times 0.68}{1.482}$$

- Numerator: $0.88 \times 0.68 = 0.5984$ V
- Division: $0.5984 / 1.482 = 0.4038$

$$\eta_{stack} = 40.38\%$$

**(iii) Net System Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta_{sys} = \eta_{stack} \times \left(1 - \frac{P_{aux}}{P_{gross}}\right)$$

- $\frac{P_{aux}}{P_{gross}} = \frac{1200}{7650} = 0.1569$
- $1 - 0.1569 = 0.8431$
- $\eta_{sys} = 0.4038 \times 0.8431 = 0.3404$

$$\eta_{sys} = 34.04\%$$

**(iv) Heat to be Removed (1 mark):**

$$Q_{gen} = P_{gross} \times \left(\frac{V_{LHV} - V_{cell}}{V_{cell}}\right) = 7650 \times \left(\frac{1.482 - 0.68}{0.68}\right)$$

- $\frac{1.482 - 0.68}{0.68} = \frac{0.802}{0.68} = 1.1794$
- $Q_{gen} = 7650 \times 1.1794 = 9022$ W

$$Q_{gen} \approx 9.02 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> 
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to multiply $V_{cell}$ by $\mu_{fuel}$ in the efficiency formula. Full 2 marks deducted.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using HHV value (1.229 V) instead of LHV (1.482 V) when the question specifies LHV.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **stack efficiency** (electrochemical) with **system efficiency** (net usable). They are NOT the same.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Not showing unit conversions. Always write "W" or "kW" explicitly.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting to state the LHV basis explicitly. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark if not mentioned.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

A labeled block diagram showing the following interconnected sub-systems must be drawn:

1. **H₂ Storage** → **Regulator** → **Humidifier** → **Anode Inlet**
2. **Air Inlet** → **Filter** → **Compressor** → **Humidifier** → **Cathode Inlet**
3. **Stack** with MEA, bipolar plates, cooling channels
4. **Anode Exhaust** → **Separator** → **Recirculation/Purge**
5. **Cathode Exhaust** → **Condenser** → **Water Recovery**
6. **Cooling Loop**: Coolant Pump → Stack → Radiator → Coolant Pump
7. **Power Electronics**: Stack Output → DC-DC → Inverter → Load
8. **ECU** controlling all actuators and reading all sensors

**Description of BOP Subsystems and Their Efficiency Impact (4 marks):**

| BOP Subsystem | Function | Impact on System Efficiency |
|---|---|---|
| **H₂ Supply** | Pressure regulation, humidification | Leakage / incomplete utilization reduces $\mu_{fuel}$, lowering $\eta_{stack}$. |
| **Air Compressor** | Pressurizes oxidant | Major parasitic load (5–15 % of gross power); reduces $\eta_{sys}$ significantly. |
| **Humidifiers** | Maintain membrane hydration | Improper humidification → higher ohmic loss → lower $V_{cell}$ → lower $\eta_{stack}$. |
| **Cooling System** | Removes heat | Inadequate cooling → temperature rise → reduced $V_{cell}$ and accelerated degradation. |
| **Water Recovery** | Condenses and recycles water | Reduces external water tank mass in mobile applications; increases durability. |
| **Power Electronics** | Condition the DC output | DC-DC converter 90–95 % efficient; inverter 93–97 % efficient. Combined loss ≈ 5–10 %. |
| **ECU & Sensors** | Monitor and control | Negligible parasitic power (< 0.5 %), but critical for safety and durability. |

**Conclusion:** The net system efficiency is the product of the **electrochemical stack efficiency** and the **auxiliary subsystem efficiencies**. Designers must minimize parasitic loads (especially compressor power) to maximize delivered power.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

> A 5 kW PEMFC power module operates at a current density of **0.8 A/cm²**, with a cell area of **250 cm²** and an average cell voltage of **0.72 V**. The hydrogen utilization factor is **0.90** and the air stoichiometry is **2.0**. Ambient conditions are 25 °C, 1 atm.
> 
> **(a)** Calculate the **(i)** number of cells required, **(ii)** mass flow rate of H₂ supplied, **(iii)** mass flow rate of air supplied, and **(iv)** heat generation rate. (7 marks)
> 
> **(b)** Explain the **thermal management strategy** of a PEMFC system, comparing liquid cooling and air cooling, and discuss the role of coolant composition, flow rate, and inlet temperature on system performance. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Given:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Net system power, $P_{net}$ | 5000 W |
| Current density, $i$ | 0.8 A/cm² |
| Cell area, $A_{cell}$ | 250 cm² |
| Cell voltage, $V_{cell}$ | 0.72 V |
| $\mu_{fuel}$ | 0.90 |
| $\lambda_{air}$ | 2.0 |
| $M_{H_2}$ | 2.016 g/mol |
| $M_{air}$ | 28.97 g/mol |
| $V_{LHV}$ | 1.482 V |

**(i) Number of Cells (2 marks):**

Current per cell: $I = i \cdot A_{cell} = 0.8 \times 250 = 200$ A

Power per cell: $P_{cell} = V_{cell} \cdot I = 0.72 \times 200 = 144$ W

Number of cells: $N = \dfrac{P_{net}}{P_{cell}} = \dfrac{5000}{144} = 34.7 \approx 35$ cells

$$N = 35 \text{ cells}$$

**(ii) Mass Flow Rate of H₂ (2 marks):**

The H₂ consumption rate is given by Faraday's law:

$$\dot{n}_{H_2,consumed} = \frac{I}{nF} = \frac{200}{2 \times 96485} = 1.0364 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/s}$$

Mass consumed: $\dot{m}_{H_2,consumed} = 1.0364 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.016 = 2.089 \times 10^{-3}$ g/s

Mass supplied (with $\mu_{fuel} = 0.90$):

$$\dot{m}_{H_2,supplied} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2,consumed}}{\mu_{fuel}} = \frac{2.089 \times 10^{-3}}{0.90} = 2.321 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2,supplied} = 8.36 \text{ g/h}$$

**(iii) Mass Flow Rate of Air (2 marks):**

For each mole of H₂ consumed, 0.5 mol O₂ is required. The stoichiometric air requirement:

$$\dot{n}_{O_2,stoich} = 0.5 \times \dot{n}_{H_2,consumed} = 0.5 \times 1.0364 \times 10^{-3} = 5.182 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/s}$$

With $\lambda = 2.0$ and O₂ mass fraction 0.233 in air:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \lambda \cdot \frac{\dot{n}_{O_2,stoich} \cdot M_{air}}{0.21} = 2.0 \times \frac{5.182 \times 10^{-4} \times 28.97}{0.21}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 2.0 \times \frac{0.01502}{0.21} = 2.0 \times 0.07152 = 0.1430 \text{ g/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.1430 \text{ g/s} = 8.58 \text{ g/min}$$

**(iv) Heat Generation Rate (1 mark):**

$$Q_{gen} = P_{net} \times \left(\frac{V_{LHV} - V_{cell}}{V_{cell}}\right) = 5000 \times \left(\frac{1.482 - 0.72}{0.72}\right)$$

- $\frac{1.482 - 0.72}{0.72} = \frac{0.762}{0.72} = 1.0583$
- $Q_{gen} = 5000 \times 1.0583 = 5291.7$ W

$$Q_{gen} \approx 5.29 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Thermal Management Strategy in PEMFC Systems (7 marks):**

The thermal management subsystem is **critical** because:
- At $V_{cell} = 0.72$ V, the heat generated is **nearly equal to the electrical power output**.
- The stack temperature must be held between **60–80 °C** (PEM) to balance membrane hydration, catalyst activity, and degradation rate.

**Comparison: Liquid Cooling vs. Air Cooling:**

| Feature | Liquid Cooling | Air Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer coefficient | High (500–1500 W/m²K) | Low (50–200 W/m²K) |
| Coolant | Deionized water + glycol | Ambient/process air |
| Power removed per unit area | High | Limited |
| System complexity | High (pump, radiator, plumbing) | Low (blower + fins) |
| Stack power range | > 1 kW to MW | < 1 kW |
| Uniformity of cooling | Excellent | Poor at high power |
| Weight & volume | Higher | Lower |
| Parasitic power | Pump (1–3 % of stack) | Blower (3–8 % of stack) |
| Application | Automotive FCEV, stationary | Portable, small backup |

**Role of Coolant Parameters (3 marks):**

1. **Coolant Composition:** Deionized water + 40–60 % ethylene glycol is standard. Glycol raises boiling point and lowers freezing point. Deionization (< 1 μS/cm) prevents ionic contamination of the membrane.

2. **Flow Rate:** Higher flow rate increases convective heat removal (Nu ∝ Re^0.8) but increases pump parasitic power. Optimal flow corresponds to a coolant temperature rise of **5–10 °C** across the stack.

3. **Inlet Temperature:** Lower inlet temperature → higher $\Delta T$ for heat removal → larger temperature gradient → risk of membrane dry-out at the inlet. Higher inlet temperature → reduces cooling capacity but improves reaction kinetics. Typical inlet temperature: **55–65 °C**.

4. **Coolant Channel Design:** Parallel, serpentine, or interdigitated. Serpentine gives uniform temperature distribution; parallel minimizes pressure drop.

5. **Heat Exchanger (Radiator) Sizing:** Sized such that at maximum ambient temperature (e.g., 40 °C), the coolant exit temperature remains below **75 °C** to prevent membrane damage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Part (b):**
> 
> 1. Students often forget to mention **why** liquid cooling is preferred for high-power systems. Always tie your answer to the **heat generation rate** derived in part (a).
> 2. Do NOT confuse **stack temperature** with **cathode inlet air temperature**. They are different.
> 3. Always state that **deionization** of the coolant is mandatory to avoid membrane contamination by metal ions (Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺).
> 4. Mention **thermal cycling** as a degradation mechanism. Examiners award 0.5 mark extra for this insight.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Fuel Cell System** = Stack + Balance of Plant (BOP)
- ☐ **BOP** = all auxiliaries: H₂ supply, air supply, thermal, water, power electronics, ECU
- ☐ **LHV voltage equivalent** = **1.482 V** (use this unless HHV is specified, in which case use **1.229 V**)
- ☐ **HHV** includes latent heat of water vaporization; **LHV** does not

### Key Formulas (must memorize)
- ☐ $E^0 = \dfrac{237.13 \times 10^3}{2 \times 96485} = 1.229$ V (reversible)
- ☐ $\eta_{stack} = \dfrac{\mu_{fuel} \cdot V_{cell}}{1.482}$ (LHV)
- ☐ $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{stack} \left(1 - \dfrac{P_{aux}}{P_{gross}}\right)$
- ☐ $Q_{gen} = P_{stack} \left(\dfrac{1.482 - V_{cell}}{V_{cell}}\right)$
- ☐ $\dot{m}_{H_2,supplied} = \dfrac{I \cdot M_{H_2}}{2F \cdot \mu_{fuel}}$
- ☐ $\dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot 0.5 \cdot I \cdot M_{air}}{nF \cdot 0.21}$

### Design Tradeoffs
- ☐ Higher current density → higher power density but lower $\eta_{stack}$
- ☐ Higher $\mu_{fuel}$ → more fuel efficient but risk of concentration loss
- ☐ Higher $\lambda_{air}$ → better cooling & O₂ distribution but more parasitic power
- ☐ Higher stack pressure → higher Nernst voltage but more compressor work
- ☐ Lower cell temperature → less degradation but slower kinetics & risk of flooding

### Common Pitfalls
- ☐ Don't use $V_{cell}$ alone in efficiency — must multiply by $\mu_{fuel}$
- ☐ Don't confuse system efficiency with stack efficiency
- ☐ Don't forget to subtract $P_{aux}$ in the system efficiency
- ☐ Don't mix up LHV (1.482 V) and HHV (1.229 V) basis
- ☐ Always include **units** in every numerical answer

### Real-World Values to Remember
- ☐ PEMFC operating temperature: **60–80 °C**
- ☐ PEMFC operating pressure: **1–3 atm**
- ☐ Typical $\mu_{fuel}$: **0.80 – 0.90**
- ☐ Typical $\lambda_{air}$: **1.5 – 2.5**
- ☐ Compressor parasitic: **5–15 %** of gross power
- ☐ Membrane RH requirement: **80–100 %**
- ☐ Coolant: **deionized water + 40–60 % ethylene glycol**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip from KTU 2024 Perspective:**
> The single most-tested concept in this module is the **efficiency chain**:
> 
> **Fuel Energy → Stack Efficiency (LHV) → Parasitic Subtraction → System Efficiency → Inverter Loss → Net AC Efficiency**
> 
> Master this chain and you can answer 60 % of the numerical questions in this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen-Air Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen-Air Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydrogen-Air System** in the context of Polymer Electrolyte Membrane (PEM) Fuel Cells is a complete electrochemical energy conversion assembly in which **gaseous hydrogen (H₂)** is supplied as the fuel at the anode and **ambient air (21% O₂, 79% N₂)** is supplied as the oxidant at the cathode. The system operates on the **hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR)** at the anode and the **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** at the cathode, separated by a proton-conducting polymer membrane.

The global electrochemical reaction is:
$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Hydrogen-Air System is *not* just the fuel cell stack itself. It is a complete **Balance of Plant (BOP)** integrating hydrogen delivery, air supply, thermal management, water management, humidification, and power conditioning subsystems to deliver regulated DC electrical output.

### 1.2 Key Operating Parameters (KTU Board Favourites)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value at 25°C, 1 atm |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Standard Gibbs Free Energy | ΔG° | **−237.2 kJ/mol** |
| Standard Enthalpy | ΔH° | **−285.8 kJ/mol** |
| Standard Cell Potential | E° | **1.229 V** |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | HHV(H₂) | 141.86 MJ/kg |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | LHV(H₂) | 119.96 MJ/kg |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Faraday's Constant | F | **96,485 C/mol** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Hydrogen-Air Breathing Engine":**
> Imagine a battery that *breathes*. Instead of storing energy in chemicals locked inside sealed cells (like a Li-ion battery), a Hydrogen-Air PEM Fuel Cell is constantly fed two "ingredients" from outside — **hydrogen gas** (think of it as the food/fuel) and **air** (think of it as the oxygen a human inhales). Inside the cell, a special plastic membrane acts like a **one-way turnstile for protons (H⁺ ions)**, allowing them to pass from the hydrogen side to the air side, but forcing the electrons to take the long way around through an external circuit — which is exactly the electrical current you use to power your device. The only by-product is pure water and heat. It is essentially a **power plant in a sandwich**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction:** A PEM fuel cell using *pure H₂* and *pure O₂* is rarely used in commercial systems because producing and storing pure oxygen is expensive. **Air is free**, but it brings nitrogen (79%), which dilutes the oxygen and must be factored into system design through the **stoichiometric flow ratio (λ)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Half-Cell Reactions and the Global Reaction

The two half-cell electrochemical reactions in a PEM hydrogen-air fuel cell are:

**Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):**
$$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \quad E^\circ_{anode} = 0.000 \text{ V vs. SHE}$$

**Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):**
$$\frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \quad E^\circ_{cathode} = +1.229 \text{ V vs. SHE}$$

**Global Reaction:**
$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(l)} \quad E^\circ_{cell} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Hydrogen Delivery:** Pressurized or stored H₂ is regulated (typically to **1.2–3.0 bar absolute**) and fed uniformly across the anode flow channels through a manifold.
- **Step 2 — HOR at the Anode Catalyst Layer:** H₂ adsorbs onto Pt catalyst nanoparticles, dissociates into H atoms, and each H atom loses an electron to form H⁺. Electrons travel through the external circuit.
- **Step 3 — Proton Conduction:** H⁺ ions migrate through the hydrated PEM (Nafion-type) from anode to cathode, dragged by the electric field and the concentration gradient.
- **Step 4 — Air Delivery:** A compressor or blower supplies air at **1.2–2.5 stoichiometry ratio** to ensure sufficient O₂ delivery despite nitrogen dilution.
- **Step 5 — ORR at the Cathode Catalyst Layer:** The sluggish ORR (rate-limiting step) combines O₂, H⁺, and e⁻ to produce liquid water.
- **Step 6 — Water and Heat Removal:** Product water is ejected through the cathode exhaust; waste heat is managed via coolant loops or air cooling.
- **Step 7 — Power Conditioning:** The low-voltage high-current DC stack output is converted to usable AC/DC via DC-DC boost converters and inverters.

### 2.3 Why the Cell Voltage Drops Below 1.229 V (Polarization Losses)

The theoretical **1.229 V** is never achieved in practice. The actual operating cell voltage **V_cell** is governed by:
$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$

Where each loss term has a specific physical origin:

- **η_act (Activation Loss):** Kinetic barrier at the electrode-catalyst interface. Dominant at **low current densities**. Caused by slow ORR kinetics.
- **η_ohm (Ohmic Loss):** Ionic resistance of the membrane and electronic resistance of electrodes/bipolar plates. **Linear with current** (follows Ohm's law: η_ohm = iR).
- **η_conc (Concentration Loss):** Mass transport limitations when reactant gases cannot reach reaction sites fast enough. Dominant at **high current densities**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Nernst Equation** | $E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | E in V; P in atm | Predicts OCV at non-standard T, P |
| **Thermodynamic Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ}$ | dimensionless | Theoretical max efficiency of fuel cell |
| **Voltage Efficiency** | $\eta_{V} = \frac{V_{cell}}{E^\circ}$ | dimensionless | Loss due to polarization |
| **Overall System Efficiency** | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{th}\cdot\eta_{V}\cdot\eta_{BOP}$ | dimensionless | Practical electrical output ÷ HHV input |
| **H₂ Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2}\cdot I}{nF}$ | kg/s; I in A | H₂ consumption per ampere |
| **Air Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{\lambda \cdot M_{air} \cdot I}{4F \cdot 0.21}$ | kg/s; λ = stoich. ratio | Compressor/blower sizing |
| **Heat Generated** | $Q_{gen} = I(E_{Nernst} - V_{cell})$ | Watts | Thermal management design |
| **Current Density Relation** | $i = \frac{I}{A_{cell}}$ | A/cm² | Normalizes performance across cell sizes |
| **Faraday's Law (H₂ consumption)** | $V_{H_2} = \frac{RT \cdot I}{nF \cdot P}$ | m³/s; P in Pa | Volumetric H₂ flow demand |
| **Power Output** | $P = V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Watts | Stack power rating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked for "efficiency of a hydrogen-air fuel cell," always specify *which* efficiency: **thermodynamic** (ΔG/ΔH), **voltage** (V_cell/E°), or **system** (η_th × η_V × η_BOP). Mixing them up is a **2-mark deduction trap** in KTU valuation.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Hydrogen-Air Systems

Hydrogen-air PEMFC systems are deployed in:
- **Transportation:** Passenger cars (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo), buses, trucks, forklifts
- **Stationary Power:** Backup power for telecom towers, residential CHP units (1–10 kW)
- **Portable Power:** Military field equipment, laptop chargers
- **Aerospace / UAVs:** Lightweight extended-endurance platforms

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Context:** Polymer electrolyte membranes are the focus of this course (PEPOT743). The H₂-air PEMFC is the most commercially relevant embodiment of the polymer electrolyte fuel cell technology studied in this syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Open-Circuit Voltage from Thermodynamics

We begin with the fundamental relationship between Gibbs free energy and electrical work:
$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$$

Where:
- ΔG° is the standard Gibbs free energy change = **−237,200 J/mol** (for H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O$_{(l)}$)
- n is the number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂ = **2**
- F is Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- E° is the standard cell potential (V)

Rearranging to solve for E°:
$$E^\circ = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$$

Substituting values:
$$E^\circ = -\frac{-237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E^\circ = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E^\circ = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the *theoretical maximum* voltage obtainable from a single H₂/O₂ cell at standard conditions. Real cells operate at **0.6–0.8 V** under load.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Nernst Equation for Hydrogen-Air Cell (Non-Standard Conditions)

The general Nernst equation is:
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$$

For the cell reaction H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O$_{(l)}$, the reaction quotient Q is:
$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2O}}{a_{H_2}\cdot a_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

Assuming pure liquid water ($a_{H_2O} = 1$) and using partial pressures for gas activities:
$$Q = \frac{1}{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

Therefore:
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{1}{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$

Applying the logarithm rule $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$:
$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$$

**Final Nernst form for H₂-air cell:**
$$\boxed{E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1: OCV Calculation at Elevated Temperature

**Problem:** [KTU University Exam Style] A PEM fuel cell operates at **80°C** (353 K) and **3.0 bar** pressure. Air is supplied at the cathode with **21% O₂**, so partial pressure of O₂ = 0.21 × 3.0 = 0.63 bar. H₂ partial pressure = **2.5 bar**. Calculate the theoretical open-circuit voltage using the Nernst equation. (Use R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96,485 C/mol, E° = 1.229 V.)

**Step 1:** Write the Nernst equation in natural logarithm form.
$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the term $\frac{RT}{2F}$.
$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{2934.84}{192{,}970}$$

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the term inside the logarithm.
$$P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2} = 2.5 \times (0.63)^{1/2}$$

$$(0.63)^{1/2} = 0.7937$$

$$P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2} = 2.5 \times 0.7937 = 1.9843$$

**Step 4:** Compute the natural logarithm.
$$\ln(1.9843) = 0.6857$$

**Step 5:** Compute the voltage correction term.
$$\Delta E = 0.01521 \times 0.6857 = 0.01043 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6:** Add to standard potential.
$$E = 1.229 + 0.01043 = 1.2394 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.239 \text{ V}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The voltage *increases* slightly with pressure because higher reactant concentration drives the reaction forward (Le Chatelier's principle for electrochemistry).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2: H₂ Mass Flow Rate and Air Requirement

**Problem:** A 50-cell PEM fuel cell stack operates at **0.7 V per cell** and **100 A** current. Air is supplied at **λ = 2.0** stoichiometry. Calculate:
- (a) H₂ mass flow rate (kg/s)
- (b) Air mass flow rate (kg/s)
- (c) Heat generated (W)
- (d) Electrical power output (W)

**Given:** M(H₂) = 2.016 g/mol; M(air) = 28.97 g/mol; F = 96,485 C/mol.

#### Part (a): Hydrogen Mass Flow Rate

The H₂ consumption equation (per the full cell reaction, n = 2):
$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2}\cdot I}{nF}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol} \times 100 \text{ A}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{0.2016}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.045 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s} = 3.76 \text{ g/hr}}$$

#### Part (b): Air Mass Flow Rate

The stoichiometric O₂ requirement: for every H₂, we need ½ O₂. With n = 2, the per-amp O₂ demand is:
$$\dot{m}_{O_2,stoich} = \frac{M_{O_2}\cdot I}{4F} = \frac{32 \times 10^{-3} \times 100}{4 \times 96{,}485} = 8.29 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}$$

Air contains 21% O₂ by mass (approximately; precise value is by mole × molecular weight):
$$\dot{m}_{O_2} = 0.233 \text{ (mass fraction of O}_2\text{ in air)} \times \dot{m}_{air}$$

Solving with λ = 2.0:
$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{\lambda \cdot M_{air}\cdot I}{4F\cdot 0.21} = \frac{2.0 \times 28.97 \times 10^{-3} \times 100}{4 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.21}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{5.794}{81{,}047.4}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{air} = 7.149 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s} = 0.257 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

#### Part (c): Heat Generated

Per-cell heat generation:
$$q_{cell} = I \times (E_{Nernst} - V_{cell})$$

Assuming $E_{Nernst} \approx E^\circ = 1.229$ V for simplicity:
$$q_{cell} = 100 \times (1.229 - 0.7) = 100 \times 0.529 = 52.9 \text{ W per cell}$$

For 50 cells in series (current is the same):
$$\boxed{Q_{stack} = 50 \times 52.9 = 2645 \text{ W} = 2.645 \text{ kW}}$$

#### Part (d): Electrical Power Output

$$P = N_{cells}\cdot V_{cell}\cdot I = 50 \times 0.7 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{P = 3500 \text{ W} = 3.5 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Balance Check:** Electrical output (3.5 kW) + Heat rejected (2.645 kW) = 6.145 kW, which matches the LHV-based chemical energy input at 100% conversion (small discrepancies arise from using E° instead of the actual reversible potential at operating T, P).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Loss Mechanisms and Mitigation Strategies

| Loss Term | Physical Origin | Dominant Current Regime | Typical Magnitude | Mitigation Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **η_act (Activation)** | Sluggish ORR kinetics; charge transfer barrier | Low current density < 0.1 A/cm² | 0.2–0.4 V | Pt-based catalysts; elevated temperature; higher catalyst loading |
| **η_ohm (Ohmic)** | Membrane ionic resistance; contact resistance | All current densities (linear) | iR drop; 0.05–0.15 Ω·cm² | Thinner membranes; higher hydration; better BPs |
| **η_conc (Concentration)** | Reactant starvation at high i | High current density > 1.0 A/cm² | 0.1–0.3 V | Higher stoich. ratio; optimized GDL; better flow fields |
| **Crossover Losses** | H₂ permeates through membrane | All currents | < 5% loss | Thinner reinforced membranes (e.g., GORE-SELECT) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen-Air PEM Fuel Cell System Block Diagram

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the complete Balance of Plant (BOP) architecture of a hydrogen-air PEM fuel cell system, mapping the major subsystems and their functional interdependencies.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H2Tank[H2 Storage Tank] -->|Pressurized H2| PR[Pressure Regulator 1.2-3 bar]
    PR -->|Humidified H2| HU[Humidifier Unit]
    HU -->|H2 Fuel Stream| ANODE[Anode Flow Field HOR Reaction]

    AIRIN[Ambient Air Intake Filter] -->|Filtered Air| COMP[Blower Compressor]
    COMP -->|Pressurized Air| HUM[Air Humidifier]
    HUM -->|Humidified Air| CATH[Cathode Flow Field ORR Reaction]

    ANODE <-->|PEM Proton Conduction| CATH
    ANODE -->|Anode Exhaust H2 Unreacted| RECIRC[H2 Recirculator]
    RECIRC -->|Recycled H2| PR

    CATH -->|Cathode Exhaust H2O N2| WVM[Water Vapor Separator]
    WVM -->|Liquid Water| WT[Water Tank]
    WVM -->|Dry Exhaust| EXH[Vent to Atmosphere]

    CATH -->|Waste Heat Qgen| CL[Coolant Loop Radiator]
    CL -->|Cooled Coolant| CATH

    CATH -->|Low V High I DC| BOOST[DC-DC Boost Converter]
    BOOST -->|Conditioned DC| INV[DC-AC Inverter]
    INV -->|AC Output| LOAD[External Load AC or DC]

    ECU[Electronic Control Unit ECU] -.->|PWM Control| COMP
    ECU -.->|Flow Control| PR
    ECU -.->|Temperature Control| CL
    ECU -.->|Voltage Regulation| BOOST
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Solid arrows represent **mass/energy flow** (gases, liquids, electricity, heat). Dashed arrows represent **control signal flow** from the ECU. The PEM is implicitly represented by the bidirectional connection between the Anode and Cathode blocks, where protons cross from anode to cathode.

### 4.2 Functional Subsystem Interaction Map

This second Mermaid diagram presents the **logical subsystem relationships** as a hierarchical architecture, useful for understanding how each BOP component interfaces with the stack.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUP[Fuel and Oxidant Supply Subsystem]
        A1[H2 Cylinder]
        A2[Air Filter and Compressor]
        A3[Pressure Regulators]
    end

    subgraph MGT[Moisture and Thermal Management Subsystem]
        B1[Membrane Humidifier]
        B2[Cathode Water Separator]
        B3[Coolant Pump and Radiator]
    end

    subgraph STK[Electrochemical Core PEMFC Stack]
        C1[Anode Catalyst Layer HOR]
        C2[Polymer Electrolyte Membrane]
        C3[Cathode Catalyst Layer ORR]
    end

    subgraph POW[Power Conditioning Subsystem]
        D1[DC-DC Boost Converter]
        D2[Inverter for AC Output]
        D3[Voltage and Current Sensors]
    end

    subgraph CTL[Monitoring and Control Subsystem]
        E1[ECU Microcontroller]
        E2[Safety Interlocks]
        E3[Pressure and Temperature Sensors]
    end

    SUP --> STK
    MGT --> STK
    STK --> POW
    CTL -.-> SUP
    CTL -.-> MGT
    CTL -.-> POW
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked to "draw a block diagram of a hydrogen-air PEMFC system" in the exam, ensure you include **all five subsystems** above. A common error is omitting the **Control/Monitoring subsystem** (ECU + interlocks), which examiners explicitly look for under Module 4 of PEPOT743.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper Style, Module 4]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** What is meant by the term "stoichiometric ratio" in a hydrogen-air PEM fuel cell system, and why is air typically supplied at a ratio of **λ = 2.0** instead of λ = 1.0?

**Model Answer:**
The stoichiometric ratio **λ** is the ratio of the actual reactant (air) supplied to the minimum theoretical reactant required for complete reaction.
- **λ = 1.0** corresponds to the *exact* stoichiometric demand (theoretically sufficient).
- In practice, **λ = 1.5 to 2.5** is used because:
  1. The sluggish ORR kinetics at the cathode require excess O₂ to maintain reaction rate at high current densities.
  2. Air contains 79% inert N₂ that does not participate in the reaction but must be flushed out to prevent O₂ depletion at the catalyst.
  3. Excess air flow helps evacuate product water from the cathode, preventing flooding.
  4. Lowering λ below 1.5 leads to severe concentration polarization and potential cell reversal.

**Q2. [3 Marks]** List any **three** components of the Balance of Plant (BOP) of a hydrogen-air PEMFC system and state the function of each.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Hydrogen Humidifier:** Maintains the proton conductivity of the PEM by keeping the membrane hydrated (typically 75–100% relative humidity). Without it, the membrane dries and resistance increases.
2. **Air Compressor/Blower:** Pressurizes ambient air (1.2–2.5 bar) to overcome stack pressure drop and improve cell voltage per the Nernst equation.
3. **Coolant Loop with Radiator:** Removes waste heat from the exothermic electrochemical reaction. Approximately 40–50% of the HHV energy is released as heat and must be dissipated to maintain operating temperature (60–80°C for PEM).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Style, Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Problem on H₂-Air System Thermodynamics and Sizing*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Nernst equation for a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell starting from the fundamental relationship $\Delta G = -nFE$. Calculate the theoretical open-circuit voltage at 80°C when the partial pressures are: $P_{H_2} = 2.5$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 0.5$ atm. (Use R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96,485 C/mol, E° = 1.229 V)

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PEM fuel cell stack with **40 cells** operates at an average cell voltage of **0.65 V** and delivers a current of **150 A**. Air is supplied at a stoichiometric ratio of λ = 2.0. Calculate:
- (i) Hydrogen consumption rate in kg/hr
- (ii) Air consumption rate in kg/hr
- (iii) Electrical power output in kW
- (iv) Heat generated in kW (assume E ≈ 1.18 V)
- (v) Thermodynamic efficiency using LHV of H₂ = 119.96 MJ/kg

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation and Calculation:**

Starting from the thermodynamic identity:
$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

The change in Gibbs free energy at non-standard conditions is related to standard conditions by:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT\ln(Q)$$

Equating electrical work to Gibbs free energy:
$$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT\ln(Q)$$

Rearranging:
$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(Q)$$

For the cell reaction H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O$_{(l)}$:
$$Q = \frac{1}{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}$$

Therefore the Nernst equation becomes:
$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}\right)$$

**Numerical evaluation at T = 353 K (80°C):**

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

$$P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2} = 2.5 \times (0.5)^{1/2} = 2.5 \times 0.7071 = 1.7678$$

$$\ln(1.7678) = 0.5694$$

$$E = 1.229 + (0.01521 \times 0.5694) = 1.229 + 0.00866$$

$$\boxed{E = 1.2377 \text{ V} \approx 1.238 \text{ V}}$$

**Part (b) — Stack Sizing Calculations:**

**(i) Hydrogen mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2}\cdot I}{nF} = \frac{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \times 150}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.567 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s} = 0.00564 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{H_2} \approx 0.00564 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**(ii) Air mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{\lambda \cdot M_{air}\cdot I}{4F\cdot 0.21} = \frac{2.0 \times 28.97 \times 10^{-3} \times 150}{4 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.21}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{8.691}{81{,}047.4} = 1.072 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting to kg/hr:
$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{air} = 0.386 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**(iii) Electrical power output:**

$$P = N_{cells}\cdot V_{cell}\cdot I = 40 \times 0.65 \times 150 = 3900 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P = 3.9 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iv) Heat generated:**

Per-cell heat (using given E = 1.18 V):
$$q_{cell} = I(E - V_{cell}) = 150 \times (1.18 - 0.65) = 79.5 \text{ W}$$

Total stack heat:
$$\boxed{Q_{stack} = 40 \times 79.5 = 3180 \text{ W} = 3.18 \text{ kW}}$$

**(v) Thermodynamic efficiency:**

Chemical power input from H₂:
$$P_{chem} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times LHV = 1.567 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s} \times 119.96 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$$

$$P_{chem} = 187.97 \text{ W}$$

Wait — for efficiency calculation, we use the H₂ rate that would deliver this power, but the proper efficiency compares actual electrical output to chemical input:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{electrical}}{P_{chem}} = \frac{3900}{P_{chem}}$$

But this is inconsistent in our problem framing. The correct interpretation:

The voltage efficiency:
$$\eta_V = \frac{V_{cell}}{E} = \frac{0.65}{1.18} = 0.551 \text{ or } 55.1\%$$

The thermodynamic (HHV or LHV) efficiency:
$$\eta_{th,LHV} = \frac{V_{cell}\cdot nF}{LHV_{per mole}} = \frac{0.65 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{119.96 \times 10^{6}/1000 \text{ J/mol}}$$

Wait — for LHV per mole of H₂: LHV = 119.96 MJ/kg × 2.016×10⁻³ kg/mol = 241.8 kJ/mol.

$$\eta_{th,LHV} = \frac{nF \cdot V_{cell}}{\Delta H_{LHV}} = \frac{2 \times 96{,}485 \times 0.65}{241{,}800}$$

$$\eta_{th,LHV} = \frac{125{,}430.5}{241{,}800} = 0.519$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{th,LHV} \approx 51.9\%}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice: System Architecture, Polarization, and BOP Analysis*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete Balance of Plant (BOP)** of a hydrogen-air PEM fuel cell system. Explain the function of any **four** major BOP components in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the three major **polarization losses** in a hydrogen-air PEM fuel cell. Sketch a typical **polarization curve** (cell voltage vs. current density) and identify the region where each loss is dominant. State the typical operating current density range for automotive PEMFC applications.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — BOP Components and Functions:**

A Hydrogen-Air PEMFC system requires the following Balance of Plant components:

1. **Hydrogen Supply Subsystem:**
   - High-pressure H₂ tank (350 bar or 700 bar)
   - Pressure regulator to reduce pressure to stack inlet level
   - Humidifier to maintain membrane hydration
   - H₂ recirculator/ejector to recover unreacted H₂ from anode exhaust (improves utilization to >95%)

2. **Air Supply Subsystem:**
   - Air filter to remove particulates
   - Compressor/blower (parasitic power is 10–20% of stack output)
   - Air humidifier for membrane hydration
   - Back-pressure regulator

3. **Thermal Management Subsystem:**
   - Coolant pump circulating deionized water
   - Radiator to dissipate heat
   - Temperature sensors and 3-way mixing valves

4. **Water Management Subsystem:**
   - Water separator at cathode exhaust
   - Condenser/recirculation pump to recover product water
   - Reservoir tank

5. **Power Conditioning Subsystem:**
   - DC-DC boost converter (raises stack voltage from ~0.6–0.8 V/cell to 200–400 V DC bus)
   - Inverter for AC output
   - EMI filters

6. **Control and Safety Subsystem:**
   - ECU (Electronic Control Unit)
   - H₂ leak detectors
   - Emergency shut-off valves
   - Voltage and current monitoring

**Part (b) — Polarization Losses and Curve:**

The three losses in a hydrogen-air PEMFC are:

1. **Activation Polarization (η_act):** Caused by the kinetic barrier for the ORR at the cathode. Dominant at low current densities (< 0.1 A/cm²). Described by the **Butler-Volmer equation**. Magnitude: 0.2–0.4 V.

2. **Ohmic Polarization (η_ohm):** Linear loss from ionic resistance in the PEM and contact resistance. Follows Ohm's law: $\eta_{ohm} = iR_{internal}$. Dominant in the mid-current range (0.1–1.0 A/cm²).

3. **Concentration Polarization (η_conc):** Caused by mass-transport limitations at high current. Reactant gases cannot diffuse through the GDL fast enough. Dominant at high current densities (> 1.0 A/cm²). Described by a limiting current density $i_L$.

**Polarization Curve Description:**

The cell voltage starts at E° = 1.229 V (no load) and decreases with current density in three characteristic regions:
- **Region I (0 to ~0.1 A/cm²):** Steep drop due to activation losses
- **Region II (~0.1 to ~1.0 A/cm²):** Linear, gradual drop from ohmic losses
- **Region III (> 1.0 A/cm²):** Sharp drop from concentration losses

**Automotive operating point:** Typically **0.5–1.5 A/cm²** at cell voltage **0.6–0.7 V** (e.g., Toyota Mirai stack operates around 0.65 V/cell at ~1.0 A/cm²).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hydrogen-Air System Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing ΔG° with ΔH°:** ΔG° (−237.2 kJ/mol) gives the *electrical* potential (1.229 V), while ΔH° (−285.8 kJ/mol) gives the *thermodynamic* efficiency ceiling (83%). Using ΔH° in the Nernst derivation yields 1.481 V — a common 3-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 (n = 2):** In the Nernst equation, n = 2 electrons per H₂. Omitting this gives 2.46 V — a frequent blunder.
> 3. **Air at 21% O₂, not 100%:** Cathode calculations must use $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \times P_{total}$ (mole fraction). Using $P_{O_2} = P_{total}$ overestimates Nernst potential by ~30 mV.
> 4. **Stoichiometry vs. Utilization:** λ = 2.0 means *twice* the air needed is supplied, NOT 50% utilization. λ and utilization are reciprocals only in idealized systems.
> 5. **LHV vs. HHV confusion:** For H₂, the difference between HHV (141.86 MJ/kg) and LHV (119.96 MJ/kg) is significant (~15%). Automotive systems use HHV for fair comparison with gasoline; stationary systems often use LHV.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hydrogen-Air Systems (Module 4)**

- **Global Reaction:** H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O$_{(l)}$; n = 2 electrons per H₂ molecule.
- **Standard OCV:** E° = 1.229 V at 25°C, 1 atm. (Derive from ΔG° = −nFE°)
- **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln(P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2})$
- **Thermodynamic Efficiency:** η_th = ΔG°/ΔH° = 83% (HHV) or 94% (LHV theoretical)
- **Practical Operating Voltage:** 0.6–0.8 V per cell under load.
- **Three Polarization Losses:** Activation (ORR kinetics), Ohmic (iR), Concentration (mass transport).
- **Stoichiometric Ratio (λ):** Typically 1.5–2.5 for air supply in commercial systems.
- **H₂ Consumption:** $\dot{m}_{H_2} = M_{H_2}\cdot I / (nF)$; ≈ 0.05 g/s per 1000 A.
- **Heat Generation:** ~40–50% of HHV energy is released as heat; thermal management is critical.
- **BOP Subsystems:** Fuel delivery, Air supply, Humidification, Thermal management, Water management, Power conditioning, Control & Safety.
- **PEM Operating Temperature:** 60–80°C (limited by membrane dehydration above 90°C and Pt poisoning below 50°C).
- **PEM Hydration Requirement:** 75–100% relative humidity for adequate proton conductivity.
- **Air Composition Used:** 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ (mole basis); partial pressure $P_{O_2} = 0.21 \cdot P_{total}$.
- **H₂ Storage Pressure:** 350 bar (older) / **700 bar** (current automotive standard).
- **Faraday's Constant (F):** 96,485 C/mol — memorize this value.
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** 8.314 J/(mol·K) — use SI units consistently.
- **Engineering Reality:** Real system efficiency (electrical output / HHV input) is **40–60%** due to all combined losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra for Module 4:** *"Memorize E° = 1.229 V, F = 96,485 C/mol, and the Nernst form. Practice at least three numerical problems on OCV, H₂ flow rate, and air flow rate. Draw the BOP block diagram with all five subsystems at least once before the exam. These four items cover 80% of the marks."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cell Systems with Fuel Processor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT743 - POLYMERS & FUEL CELL TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cell Systems with Fuel Processor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fuel Cell System with Fuel Processor** is an integrated electrochemical energy conversion assembly in which a hydrocarbon or alcohol-based primary fuel (e.g., natural gas, methanol, gasoline, diesel) is catalytically converted (reformed) into a hydrogen-rich gas stream suitable for feeding a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC) or similar low-temperature fuel cell stack. The fuel processor unit is the upstream subsystem that performs **desulfurization, reforming, water-gas shift (WGS), and preferential CO oxidation (PROX)**, while the downstream fuel cell stack performs the electrochemical conversion to electricity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fuel processor is the critical bridge between the existing hydrocarbon fuel infrastructure and the hydrogen-demanding fuel cell. Without an efficient fuel processor, on-board or stationary fuel cell systems cannot operate on conventional fuels.

### 1.2 System Block Definition

The complete system can be formally represented as:

$$\text{Primary Fuel} \xrightarrow{\text{Processor}} \text{H}_2\text{-rich stream} \xrightarrow{\text{Stack}} \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Translator" in a Multilingual Office**
> Imagine an international office where the boss (the **fuel cell stack**) only understands **English (H₂)**. The visitors from around the world (the **fuels** — methane, methanol, gasoline) speak different languages. The **fuel processor** is the multilingual translator sitting at the reception desk. The translator first cleans the visitors' documents (desulfurization), then translates their native language into English (reforming), polishes the translation to remove the awkward German accent that would confuse the boss (CO removal via WGS + PROX), and finally hands over a clean, perfectly translated brief to the boss, who can now act on it (electrochemical reaction producing electricity).

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following **standard metrics** are universally cited in fuel processor design:

- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂:** 120 MJ/kg
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂:** 142 MJ/kg
- **Operating temperature of PEMFC stack:** 60–80 °C
- **Reformer operating temperature:** 600–900 °C
- **Maximum tolerable CO at PEMFC anode:** < 10 ppm (with PROX)
- **Standard cell voltage (PEMFC):** 1.229 V (theoretical, reversible)
- **Faraday's constant:** 96485 C/mol

### 1.5 Why Fuel Processors Are Needed

A PEMFC stack strictly requires **pure hydrogen** at the anode. Yet global fuel distribution still relies heavily on **liquid hydrocarbons** (gasoline, diesel, kerosene) and **natural gas (CH₄)**. The fuel processor solves this logistical impasse by:

1. Allowing the use of existing fuel distribution networks.
2. Enabling both **stationary** (home CHP) and **mobile** (vehicle) applications.
3. Producing H₂ on-demand without cryogenic storage.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Sub-Stages of a Fuel Processor

A standard fuel processor consists of four sequential sub-units:

1. **Desulfurization Unit** — Removes sulfur compounds (H₂S, COS, thiophenes) that poison reformer and stack catalysts.
2. **Reformer** — Converts the desulfurized fuel into a **synthesis gas (syngas)** mixture of H₂, CO, CO₂, and unconverted fuel.
3. **Water-Gas Shift (WGS) Reactor** — Converts the toxic CO into additional H₂ using steam.
4. **Preferential Oxidation (PROX) Reactor** — Selectively oxidizes residual CO to < 10 ppm, protecting the Pt anode catalyst of the PEMFC.

### 2.2 Core Reforming Reactions (Step-by-Step Logic)

#### A. Steam Reforming (SR) — Endothermic

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Why endothermic?** The C–H and H–O bonds broken absorb more energy than the C–O and H–H bonds formed.
- **How it works:** Steam is mixed with the hydrocarbon over a Ni/Al₂O₃ catalyst at 700–850 °C. The H₂ yield is the highest of all reforming methods.

#### B. Partial Oxidation (POX) — Exothermic

$$\text{CH}_4 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} + 2\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -36 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Why exothermic?** The C–O bond formation in CO releases energy.
- **How it works:** Sub-stoichiometric oxygen is supplied. Faster startup than SR but lower H₂ yield.

#### C. Autothermal Reforming (ATR) — Thermoneutral

ATR is a hybrid of SR and POX, occurring in a single catalytic bed with simultaneous steam and oxygen feed.

$$\text{CH}_4 + a\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + b\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow c\,\text{H}_2 + d\,\text{CO} + e\,\text{CO}_2$$

The autothermal condition is achieved when the exothermic POX heat exactly balances the endothermic SR heat demand, giving a net $\Delta H \approx 0$.

#### D. Water-Gas Shift (WGS) — Mildly Exothermic

$$\text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

- **Why important?** It doubles the H₂ produced per mole of fuel and removes the major CO contaminant.
- **Two-stage operation:** High-Temperature Shift (HTS) at 350–450 °C over Fe–Cr catalyst, followed by Low-Temperature Shift (LTS) at 190–250 °C over Cu–Zn–Al catalyst.

#### E. Preferential Oxidation (PROX)

$$\text{CO} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -283 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Conducted at 80–200 °C over Pt, Ru, or Au catalysts. Selectivity over the competing H₂ oxidation is the critical performance metric.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Symbol | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | $m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | Hydrogen consumption rate |
| H₂ consumption rate | $\dot{n}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I}{2F}$ | mol/s | Stoichiometric feed |
| Nernst Equation (cell) | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | V | Open-circuit cell voltage |
| System Efficiency (HHV) | $\eta_{sys} = \dfrac{V_{cell}\cdot I}{m_{fuel}\cdot HHV_{fuel}}$ | — | Overall system performance |
| Reformer Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ref} = \dfrac{LHV_{H_2,out}}{LHV_{fuel,in} + Q_{external}}$ | — | Reformer heat management |
| Steam-to-Carbon Ratio (S/C) | $S/C = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2O}}{\dot{n}_{C,in fuel}}$ | — | Critical SR control parameter |
| CO conversion (WGS) | $X_{CO} = \dfrac{y_{CO,in} - y_{CO,out}}{y_{CO,in}}$ | — | WGS performance metric |
| PROX selectivity | $S_{PROX} = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot \dot{n}_{CO,oxid}}{\dot{n}_{O_2,cons}}$ | — | Catalyst selectivity metric |
| H₂ recovery factor | $R_{H_2} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{H_2,out}}{\dot{n}_{H_2,theoretical,max}}$ | — | System-level H₂ yield |
| Thermal neutrality (ATR) | $\Delta H_{SR} + \Delta H_{POX} = 0$ | kJ/mol | ATR stoichiometric balance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Gas Constant (R):** 8.314 J/(mol·K) — used in every thermodynamic relation above.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stationary Combined Heat and Power (CHP):** Natural gas → fuel processor → 1–5 kW PEMFC stack for residential use, with > 80% total efficiency (electrical + thermal).
- **Automotive Applications:** Methanol or gasoline fuel processors feed a 50–100 kW PEMFC stack in Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs). The Toyota Mirai uses **high-pressure H₂ tanks** instead, but fuel processor research targets heavy-duty trucks and auxiliary power units.
- **Auxiliary Power Units (APU):** Diesel fuel processors in trucks, ships, and aircraft cabins for emission-free on-board power.
- **Portable Power:** Cartridge-based methanol reformers for laptop and military field power.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical H₂ Yield from Methanol Steam Reforming

**Given Reaction (Methanol SR):**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2$$

**Step 1 — Count the carbon atoms.**

$$\text{C atoms (left)} = 1 \quad ; \quad \text{C atoms (right)} = 1 \quad \checkmark \text{ (balanced)}$$

**Step 2 — Count the hydrogen atoms.**

$$\text{H atoms (left)} = 4 + 2 = 6 \quad ; \quad \text{H atoms (right)} = 2 \cdot 3 = 6 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Count the oxygen atoms.**

$$\text{O atoms (left)} = 1 + 1 = 2 \quad ; \quad \text{O atoms (right)} = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric H₂ yield per mole of methanol.**

$$\boxed{Y_{H_2} = \frac{3 \text{ mol H}_2}{1 \text{ mol CH}_3\text{OH}}}$$

**Step 5 — Mass-based H₂ yield.**

Using molar masses $M_{CH_3OH} = 32$ g/mol and $M_{H_2} = 2$ g/mol:

$$Y_{H_2,\,mass} = \frac{3 \times 2}{32} = \frac{6}{32} = 0.1875$$

So 1 kg of methanol theoretically yields **187.5 g of H₂** through steam reforming.

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Steam-to-Carbon Ratio for Coke Prevention

**Given:** SR of methane, $T = 800$ °C $= 1073$ K, $P = 1$ atm.

**Step 1 — Identify the coking reactions.**

$$\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow \text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \quad \text{(methane cracking, endothermic)}$$

$$\text{2CO} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{C} \quad \text{(Boudouard reaction, exothermic)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply equilibrium condition for Boudouard reaction at 1073 K.**

The Gibbs free energy change of the Boudouard reaction at 1073 K is approximately $\Delta G = -12.5$ kJ/mol. The equilibrium constant:

$$\Delta G = -RT \ln K_p$$

$$-12{,}500 = -8.314 \times 1073 \times \ln K_p$$

$$\ln K_p = \frac{12{,}500}{8920.6} = 1.401$$

$$K_p = 4.06$$

**Step 3 — Express $K_p$ in terms of partial pressures.**

$$K_p = \frac{P_{CO_2}}{P_{CO}^2} = 4.06$$

**Step 4 — Set the boundary condition for no carbon deposition.**

For no carbon: $\dfrac{P_{CO_2}}{P_{CO}^2} \geq 4.06$ (over the catalyst surface).

Using the SR + WGS equilibrium composition, the minimum steam-to-carbon ratio to satisfy this is:

$$\boxed{(S/C)_{min} \approx 2.5 \text{ to } 3.0}$$

This is the **standard design rule** for industrial steam reformers to prevent coking on the Ni catalyst.

### 3.3 Derivation: Fuel Processor Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define inputs and outputs.**

- **Input energy:** $Q_{in} = m_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel}$ + external heat $Q_{ext}$
- **Useful output energy:** H₂ chemical energy in reformer exit = $m_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}$

**Step 2 — Write the efficiency equation.**

$$\eta_{ref} = \frac{m_{H_2} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}{m_{fuel} \cdot LHV_{fuel} + Q_{ext}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical example (methanol SR, S/C = 1.5, 100% conversion).**

From §3.1, 1 kg methanol → 0.1875 kg H₂.

$$LHV_{CH_3OH} = 19.9 \text{ MJ/kg} \quad ; \quad LHV_{H_2} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

With $Q_{ext} = 0$ (adiabatic case, using heat recovery from stack exhaust):

$$\eta_{ref} = \frac{0.1875 \times 120}{1 \times 19.9 + 0} = \frac{22.5}{19.9} = 1.131$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An efficiency > 1 in this HHV/LHV mismatched definition is physically consistent because $LHV_{H_2}$ (120 MJ/kg) is referenced to gaseous H₂, while $LHV_{CH_3OH}$ (19.9 MJ/kg) is on a mass basis of liquid fuel. The energy balance is correct, but **mass-based comparison can be misleading**. For a true thermodynamic analysis, the LHV of H₂ must be reported on the same mass basis as the fuel input.

**Step 5 — Corrected (mole-based) efficiency.**

Per mole of methanol: 3 mol H₂ produced.

$$\eta_{ref,\,molar} = \frac{3 \times 241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}}{726 \text{ kJ/mol} + Q_{ext}} = \frac{725.4}{726 + Q_{ext}/n}$$

With zero external heat input:

$$\eta_{ref,\,molar} \approx 0.999 \approx 100\%$$

This shows the **near-ideal thermodynamic reversibility** of the methanol SR reaction.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reforming Method Comparison

| Parameter | Steam Reforming (SR) | Partial Oxidation (POX) | Autothermal Reforming (ATR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| H₂/CH₄ ratio (syngas) | 3.0 | 2.0 | 2.5 |
| Thermal character | Endothermic | Exothermic | Thermoneutral |
| Operating T (°C) | 700–850 | 900–1100 | 600–900 |
| Startup time | Slow (needs external heat) | Fast | Moderate |
| External heat needed | Yes | No | No |
| Catalyst | Ni/Al₂O₃ | Rh, Pt | Ni + Pt |
| Coke formation risk | High (requires S/C > 2.5) | Low | Moderate |
| H₂ yield per mole CH₄ | Highest | Lowest | Intermediate |
| Preferred application | Stationary CHP | Mobile, fast-start | Automotive, mobile |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Fuel Cell System with Fuel Processor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid or Gaseous Primary Fuel e.g. CH4 CH3OH C8H18] --> B[Desulfurization Adsorber ZnO or NiMo Catalyst]

    C --> D[High Temperature Shift Reactor HTS 350 to 450 C Fe Cr Catalyst]
    D --> E[Low Temperature Shift Reactor LTS 190 to 250 C Cu Zn Al Catalyst]
    E --> F[Preferential Oxidation PROX 80 to 200 C Pt or Ru Catalyst]
    F --> G[PEMFC Stack Anode Side H2 Inlet]
    G --> H[Power Conditioning Unit DC DC Converter and Inverter]
    H --> I[AC or DC Electrical Load 1 to 250 kW]
    G --> J[Cathode Air Inlet Blower]
    J --> G
    G --> K[Thermal Management Heat Exchanger Stack Cooling]
    K --> C
    K --> D
    A2[Process Water Reservoir] --> C
    A2 --> D
    A2 --> E
    A2 --> F
```
