## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Choose A or B]**

**(a)** Describe the **four pillars of the hydrogen economy** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat schematic, explain the operation of a **PEM electrolyzer** for green hydrogen production. Derive the expression for hydrogen production rate using Faraday's law. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a):** 7 Marks

The **four pillars** that must function synergistically for the hydrogen economy to operate are:

**Pillar 1 — Production:**
Hydrogen must be generated in large volumes with low-carbon intensity. Examples:
- *Green H₂* via PEM electrolysis using solar/wind electricity.
- *Blue H₂* via SMR + CCS in transitional phase.
- *Pink H₂* via nuclear-powered electrolysis.

**Pillar 2 — Storage & Transportation:**
Hydrogen has a low volumetric energy density, requiring engineered solutions:
- *Compressed gaseous* storage at 350 or 700 bar in Type IV carbon-fiber tanks.
- *Liquid hydrogen* (LH₂) at **−253 °C** for long-distance shipping.
- *LOHC* (e.g., dibenzyltoluene) for safe chemical binding and release.
- *Underground salt caverns* for seasonal grid-scale storage (e.g., Teesside, UK).

**Pillar 3 — End-Use Applications:**
Hydrogen substitutes fossil fuels in:
- *Mobility*: FCEVs (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO).
- *Industry*: Green ammonia, methanol, DRI steelmaking.
- *Power*: Hydrogen turbines (GE 7HA.03), fuel cells (Bloom Energy SOFC).
- *Buildings*: Micro-CHP units (PEMFC, ~1 kW).

**Pillar 4 — Policy, Market & Finance Frameworks:**
Government interventions to bridge the cost gap:
- *Production-linked incentives* (PLI), e.g., India's ₹19,744 crore Green Hydrogen Mission.
- *Contracts-for-Difference* (CfD) guaranteeing minimum price.
- *Carbon Contracts* (EU Innovation Fund).
- *Hydrogen Standard & Guarantees of Origin* (ISO 19870).

*(2 marks for naming pillars, 3 marks for examples, 2 marks for comprehensive coverage.)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b):** 7 Marks

**PEM Electrolyzer — Schematic & Operation:**

A PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) electrolyzer consists of:
- **Anode** (positive electrode, Pt/Ir-coated Ti) where water is split:
$$
H_2O \rightarrow \dfrac{1}{2} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^-
$$
- **Cathode** (negative electrode, Pt-coated C) where H⁺ recombines with electrons:
$$
2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2
$$
- **Membrane** (Nafion™) — selectively transports H⁺ ions from anode to cathode.
- **Bipolar plates** distribute water and extract gases.
- **Operating conditions:** 50–80 °C, 1–80 bar differential pressure.

**Faraday's Law Derivation:**

For an electrolyzer with **N** cells, current **I** (A), for time **t** (s):

$$
\text{Moles of } H_2 = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{z \cdot F}
$$

where **z = 2** (electrons per H₂ molecule), **F = 96,485 C/mol** (Faraday constant).

Converting to Nm³/h using the molar volume **Vₘ = 22.4 L/mol** at STP:

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot V_m}{z \cdot F \cdot t} = \dfrac{I \cdot V_m}{z \cdot F}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \times 22.4 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96485} = 1.161 \times 10^{-7} \, Nm^3/s \text{ per ampere}
$$

For **N** cells in series:

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{0.4184 \cdot N \cdot I}{F} \quad \text{(Nm}^3\text{/h)}
$$

**Numerical illustration:**
For a 1 MW stack at 500 A/m² with active area 2000 cm², **I = 1000 A**:

$$
\dot{V}_{H_2} = \dfrac{0.4184 \times N \times 1000}{96485} \approx 4.34 \times 10^{-3} \, Nm^3/h \text{ per cell}
$$

*(3 marks for schematic + half-cell reactions, 2 marks for Faraday derivation, 2 marks for numerical example.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **challenges and barriers** in transitioning from a fossil-fuel economy to a hydrogen economy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydrogen refueling station stores **50 kg** of H₂ at **700 bar** and **298 K**. Calculate (i) the volume of the storage tank, (ii) the energy stored using LHV, and (iii) the equivalent volume of gasoline having LHV = 44 MJ/kg and density = 0.74 kg/L. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a):** 7 Marks

**Challenges and Barriers:**

**1. Production-side barriers:**
- *High cost of green H₂* (USD 4–6/kg vs USD 1–1.5/kg grey).
- *Renewable intermittency* requires buffering or hybrid grids.
- *Water scarcity* in arid regions (electrolysis needs ~9 L water per kg H₂).
- *Critical mineral dependency*: platinum, iridium, rare-earths.

**2. Storage and transport barriers:**
- Hydrogen's **low volumetric density** — requires extreme compression/liquefaction.
- **Hydrogen embrittlement** of steel pipelines and tanks.
- **Liquefaction energy penalty** ≈ 30–40% of LHV.
- High cost of new H₂-dedicated pipeline infrastructure (~$1–2 million/km).

**3. End-use barriers:**
- FCEV cost ~2–3× equivalent BEV.
- Lack of refueling infrastructure (1 H₂ station per ~500 BEV chargers).
- Public safety perception (Hindenburg legacy).
- Inefficient H₂ ICE (20–35% vs 50–60% for fuel cell).

**4. Regulatory & market barriers:**
- Absence of universal **H₂ certification** and standards.
- Cross-border trade tariffs on hydrogen.
- Underdeveloped insurance & liability frameworks.
- Stranded fossil-fuel asset risk & political resistance.

*(Award ~1.75 marks per category: Production, Storage/Transport, End-use, Regulatory.)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b):** 7 Marks

**Given:** m = 50 kg, P = 700 bar = 7 × 10⁷ Pa, T = 298 K, LHV_H₂ = 120 MJ/kg.

**Part (i): Tank volume using ideal gas law.**

Moles of H₂:

$$
n = \dfrac{m}{M_{H_2}} = \dfrac{50}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} = 24{,}801.6 \, mol
$$

Volume:

$$
V = \dfrac{nRT}{P} = \dfrac{24{,}801.6 \times 8.314 \times 298}{7 \times 10^7}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{6.146 \times 10^7}{7 \times 10^7} = 0.878 \, m^3 = 878 \, L
$$

> **Real tank volume (with compressibility Z ≈ 1.2 at 700 bar):** V_real ≈ 1054 L.

**Part (ii): Energy stored (LHV basis).**

$$
E = m \times LHV_{H_2} = 50 \, kg \times 120 \, MJ/kg = 6000 \, MJ = 6000/3.6 = 1666.7 \, kWh
$$

**Part (iii): Equivalent gasoline volume.**

Mass of gasoline delivering 6000 MJ:

$$
m_{gas} = \dfrac{E}{LHV_{gas}} = \dfrac{6000 \, MJ}{44 \, MJ/kg} = 136.36 \, kg
$$

Volume of gasoline:

$$
V_{gas} = \dfrac{m_{gas}}{\rho_{gas}} = \dfrac{136.36}{0.74} = 184.3 \, L
$$

**Insight:** 50 kg of H₂ stored in 878 L of high-pressure tank ≈ 184 L of gasoline in volume — about **4.7× larger** for the same energy. This is the core *engineering challenge* motivating liquid H₂ or 1000-bar research.

*(2 marks each for parts (i), (ii), (iii), 1 mark for engineering interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse HHV (142 MJ/kg) with LHV (120 MJ/kg)** — KTU answers using the wrong constant lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. In gas-law calculations, **always convert bar → Pa** (multiply by 10⁵). Students frequently forget and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **LHV is at constant pressure, water leaves as vapor** — for combustion products analysis, use HHV if water condenses. State your assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Absolute vs gauge pressure**: Always add 1.013 bar to gauge pressure before applying ideal gas law.
> 5. For PEM/alkaline electrolyzer Faraday derivation, **state z = 2** clearly — losing this costs 0.5–1 mark.
